title: About This Book
text:
This introductory textbook presents ICT as the service infrastructure through which libraries, archives, records centers, museums, and other information organizations store, describe, retrieve, deliver, preserve, protect, govern, and improve information services.
The completed manuscript contains a detailed twelve-chapter course outline and all twelve chapters, progressing from ICT infrastructure, networking, databases, and metadata through digital services, retrieval, programming, APIs, accessibility, preservation, cybersecurity, data literacy, and capstone integration.
Unless otherwise indicated, this book is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International License (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0).
crumbs: About This Book

title: Disclaimer
text:
Examples, cases, activities, and datasets are provided for learning purposes. Any resemblance to specific persons, organizations, or events is unintended unless explicitly identified. Readers should adapt the discussions and activities to their own institutional context, available resources, legal obligations, community needs, and professional standards.
Technology, platforms, laws, and standards change over time. Users should verify current requirements, software documentation, privacy regulations, accessibility guidelines, and cybersecurity practices before applying them in real library, archive, records, museum, or information-service settings.
As a self-published open textbook, the author, who is also the publisher, assumes no liability for errors, omissions, or outcomes arising from the use of this textbook. Responsibility for implementation, compliance, risk assessment, and ethical decision-making rests with the reader and their institution.
This textbook, Information and Communication Technologies for Library and Information Science: Foundations, Systems, and Services, is licensed under CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 unless otherwise indicated.
crumbs: Disclaimer

title: Preface
text:
Course architecture and assessment
Information and communication technology (ICT) in Library and Information Science (LIS) is not merely a list of office applications. It is the sociotechnical infrastructure through which information organizations acquire, describe, connect, retrieve, deliver, preserve, protect, govern, and evaluate information. A catalogue search, a digitized newspaper, an archival finding aid, a circulation notice, and a research-data dashboard all depend on interactions among people, policies, metadata, standards, devices, networks, and software.
This book is intended for undergraduate LIS students with little or no formal technical background. It begins with service problems and moves toward technical ideas only as those ideas become useful. Metadata and knowledge organization form the book’s spine. Hands-on work starts in Week 1, ethical questions recur throughout, and each technical topic is tied to professional responsibilities in libraries, archives, records centers, museums, and related information organizations.
The present volume contains a detailed plan and complete text for all twelve chapters. Citations use Pandoc/Quarto keys such as (Star and Ruhleder 1996) and narrative forms such as Borgman (2007). The bibliography is maintained separately in references.bib so that sources can be reused and checked as the manuscript is revised.
The six-part architecture moves from foundations to description, services, code, stewardship, and synthesis. Table 1 shows how the twelve chapters create that progression and where the course assessments fit. The table matters because assessment evidence is distributed: students repeatedly practice small skills before integrating them in a capstone.
Table 1: Course architecture and assessment alignment





Architecture
Chapters
Principal evidence
Weight




Foundations of ICT in LIS
1-2
Short labs and quizzes
20%


Data Structures and Description
3-4
Metadata and cataloguing work
15%


Discovery and Digital Services
5-6 and 9
UX or discovery critique
10%


Coding and Web Data
7-8
Database or API mini-project
20%


Stewardship, Risk, and Governance
10-11
Ethics and preservation brief
10%


Data Practice and Synthesis
12
Capstone
25%
The sequence is cumulative rather than strictly linear. For example, privacy first appears as a design consideration in Chapter 1, becomes a data issue in Chapter 3, and returns as a legal and governance obligation in Chapter 11. Likewise, metadata introduced in Chapter 1 is formalized in Chapter 4 and then reused in repositories, discovery, linked data, and preservation.
crumbs: Preface

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
text:
Chapter 1. ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
Summary. The opening chapter reframes ICT from “computers and software” to a service infrastructure made of people, data, metadata, standards, workflows, policies, networks, and interfaces. Students follow a library request through a layered system, distinguish digitization from digital transformation, and examine how technical choices affect access, privacy, reliability, and professional values.
Learning outcomes. By the end of the chapter, students will be able to:
Major concepts. ICT; infrastructure; sociotechnical system; information system; data; metadata; hardware; software; network; platform; workflow; standard; interoperability; automation; digitization; digitalization; digital transformation; availability; stakeholder; digital divide.
Practical activity. Conduct a service infrastructure audit of a familiar catalogue, repository, circulation service, or reference channel using a five-part worksheet.
Expected output. A one-page service map, a failure-and-impact table, and a 150-word reflection proposing one realistic improvement.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
text:
Define ICT, information infrastructure, system, platform, interoperability, and sociotechnical system in LIS terms.
Map the people, data, technology, policy, and workflow components of an information service.
Trace one user request through interface, application, metadata, data, and infrastructure layers.
Distinguish digitization, digitalization, automation, and digital transformation.
Identify likely service failures, affected stakeholders, and basic safeguards.
Evaluate an ICT decision using access, privacy, inclusion, sustainability, and accountability criteria.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 2. Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
Chapter 2. Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
Summary. Students investigate what happens between entering a URL and receiving a web page. They connect clients, servers, IP addresses, domain names, DNS, HTTP/HTTPS, browsers, HTML, CSS, hosting, and content management systems to library websites and remote access services. Website governance and low-bandwidth design are treated as service concerns.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Diagram a basic client-server transaction.
Explain the roles of IP addresses, DNS, URLs, HTTP, and HTTPS.
Inspect the structure of a simple HTML document.
Evaluate a library website for findability, trust, mobile use, and connection constraints.
Publish a small standards-based web page with semantic headings and links.

Major concepts. LAN; WAN; internet; web; protocol; packet; bandwidth; latency; client; server; DNS; URL; HTTP; HTTPS; HTML; CSS; browser; hosting; CMS; responsive design.
Practical activity. Use browser developer tools and a text editor to inspect and revise a small library-hours page.
Expected output. An annotated request-path diagram, valid HTML file, and short website critique. This lab contributes to the short-lab component and prepares students for the mobile and accessibility critique in Chapter 9.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
text:
Chapter 3. Databases for Library Data
Summary. This chapter explains why structured storage is necessary for collections, users, transactions, and authorities. Students move from a messy spreadsheet to a simple relational model and learn tables, fields, records, keys, relationships, validation, normalization, queries, backups, and access control without assuming prior programming experience.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Distinguish flat files, spreadsheets, and relational databases.
Identify entities, attributes, primary keys, and relationships in an LIS scenario.
Create a small normalized database schema.
Enter validated data and run basic SELECT, WHERE, and ORDER BY queries.
Explain privacy and retention risks in patron and transaction data.

Major concepts. Database; DBMS; table; row; column; entity; attribute; schema; primary key; foreign key; relationship; normalization; SQL; query; constraint; transaction; backup.
Practical activity. Build a three-table SQLite database for a community reading-room collection, creators, and loans using DB Browser for SQLite.
Expected output. Entity-relationship diagram, .sqlite file, five queries with results, and a brief data-minimization note. This can begin the database or API mini-project.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
text:
Chapter 4. Metadata and Cataloguing
Summary. Metadata is presented as structured, governed description that enables identification, selection, access, management, rights control, and preservation. Students compare descriptive, administrative, structural, technical, and preservation metadata; examine elements, values, schemas, encoding, content standards, identifiers, authority control, and application profiles; and connect MARC 21, RDA, Dublin Core, and emerging linked-data models.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Classify metadata by function.
Distinguish element sets, content standards, encoding formats, and value vocabularies.
Create a small Dublin Core record using controlled values.
Explain the purpose of identifiers and authority control.
Detect and correct common metadata quality problems.
Discuss how description can include, exclude, or misrepresent communities.

Major concepts. Metadata; element; value; schema; application profile; MARC 21; RDA; Dublin Core; identifier; authority control; controlled vocabulary; subject access; provenance; metadata quality; interoperability.
Practical activity. Describe a digitized local-history item in a shared metadata template, then exchange records for quality review.
Expected output. Completed metadata record, validation checklist, and revision log. This is the core metadata and cataloguing assessment.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 5. Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
Chapter 5. Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
Summary. Students compare digital libraries, institutional repositories, digital archives, digital exhibits, and commercial content platforms by purpose, content model, workflow, governance, and preservation responsibility. Ingest, storage, metadata, access, rights, identifiers, and platform selection are tied to collection policy and institutional capacity.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Differentiate major kinds of digital content systems.
Diagram a basic ingest-to-access workflow.
Compare repository platforms using service and stewardship criteria.
Prepare files and metadata for a small digital collection.
Identify rights, consent, cultural sensitivity, and sustainability risks.

Major concepts. Digital library; repository; collection management system; CMS; ingest; submission information package; access copy; master file; OCR; persistent identifier; embargo; takedown; platform governance.
Practical activity. Create a three-item prototype collection in Omeka S or a structured local simulation.
Expected output. Collection statement, item records, rights labels, screenshots or export, and workflow diagram.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 6. Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
Chapter 6. Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
Summary. This chapter follows the path from a query to a ranked result set. It explains indexes, fields, tokenization, Boolean logic, phrase searching, facets, relevance ranking, precision, recall, federated search, link resolution, discovery layers, and evaluation. Students learn that retrieval quality depends as much on metadata and interface choices as on algorithms.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Explain how an index supports retrieval.
Construct fielded, Boolean, phrase, and faceted searches.
Calculate and interpret precision and recall in a small example.
Diagnose retrieval problems involving metadata, indexing, ranking, or interface design.
Compare catalogue, database, repository, and discovery-layer results.

Major concepts. Information retrieval; corpus; index; token; query; Boolean operator; fielded search; facet; relevance; ranking; precision; recall; stemming; synonym; federated search; discovery layer; link resolver.
Practical activity. Run the same information need through three discovery systems and analyze the first ten results.
Expected output. Search log, relevance judgments, simple precision comparison, and discovery-improvement recommendation.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 7. Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
Chapter 7. Programming for LIS and Automation
Summary. Programming is introduced as a method for expressing repeatable decisions, not as an end in itself. Using R, students work with variables, vectors, data frames, conditions, loops or vectorized operations, functions, packages, errors, and reproducible scripts to clean filenames or metadata and generate a report.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Read and modify a short R script.
Represent metadata in vectors and data frames.
Apply conditions and functions to automate a repetitive task.
Import, clean, and export a small CSV file.
Test results and document assumptions.
Identify tasks that should remain under human review.

Major concepts. Algorithm; program; script; variable; data type; vector; data frame; condition; iteration; function; package; input/output; exception; debugging; reproducibility; automation.
Practical activity. Use R and tidyverse packages to standardize dates, trim spaces, flag missing titles, and export a metadata quality report.
Expected output. Commented .R or .qmd script, cleaned CSV, exception report, and verification checklist.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 8. APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
Chapter 8. APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
Summary. Students learn how systems exchange structured data through APIs and how linked data expresses relationships using web identifiers and triples. Requests, responses, endpoints, parameters, status codes, JSON, XML, RDF, URIs, vocabularies, and SPARQL are introduced through library authority and bibliographic data.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Describe a basic API request-response exchange.
Read nested JSON and identify relevant fields.
Retrieve and transform a small public API response in R.
Express a bibliographic relationship as RDF-like subject-predicate-object triples.
Explain the benefits and risks of identifiers and linked open data.

Major concepts. API; endpoint; request; response; parameter; authentication; status code; JSON; XML; URI; RDF; triple; vocabulary; ontology; knowledge graph; SPARQL; linked open data.
Practical activity. Query an open books or authority API, select five fields, and build a small subject-guide table with source attribution.
Expected output. Reproducible API script, saved raw response, cleaned dataset, data dictionary, and attribution note. This completes the database or API mini-project.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 9. UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
Chapter 9. UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
Summary. User experience (UX) is treated as evidence-based service design. Students examine user research, task analysis, information architecture, usability, accessibility, responsive and mobile-first design, assistive technologies, plain language, and WCAG. They consider constrained devices, prepaid data, intermittent connectivity, and multilingual interaction in Southeast Asian contexts.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Distinguish usability, UX, and accessibility.
Create a task-based usability test.
Evaluate a service against selected WCAG 2.2 criteria.
Identify barriers in navigation, content, forms, and mobile layouts.
Prioritize changes by user impact and implementation effort.

Major concepts. UX; usability; accessibility; inclusive design; user research; persona; task; information architecture; responsive design; mobile first; assistive technology; alt text; keyboard access; contrast; WCAG.
Practical activity. Test a library discovery task on desktop and mobile with an accessibility checklist and one peer participant.
Expected output. Test protocol, observation notes, annotated screens, severity-ranked findings, and redesign sketch. This is the UX or discovery critique.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 10. Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
text:
Chapter 10. Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
Summary. Preservation is distinguished from storage, backup, and digitization. Students examine threats, file formats, checksums, fixity, redundancy, migration, emulation, preservation metadata, storage tiers, cloud service models, vendor dependence, cost, jurisdiction, and exit planning through the lifecycle of a digital object.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Distinguish backup, storage, digitization, and digital preservation.
Identify common threats to digital continuity.
Generate and verify checksums.
Propose a basic 3-2-1 storage and monitoring plan.
Compare local and cloud options using cost, control, security, jurisdiction, and exit criteria.

Major concepts. Digital preservation; bitstream; fixity; checksum; integrity; authenticity; redundancy; 3-2-1 rule; migration; emulation; obsolescence; preservation metadata; IaaS; PaaS; SaaS; service-level agreement; vendor lock-in.
Practical activity. Create a small preservation package with inventory, checksums, format-risk notes, three copies, and a recovery test.
Expected output. Package manifest, fixity log, storage diagram, and preservation action plan.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 11. Cybersecurity, Law, Ethics, and Project Planning
text:
Chapter 11. Cybersecurity, Law, Ethics, and Project Planning
Summary. The chapter integrates risk management with professional responsibility. Students study confidentiality, integrity, availability, threats, vulnerabilities, controls, authentication, authorization, least privilege, phishing, malware, incident response, copyright, privacy, intellectual freedom, procurement, scope, stakeholders, schedules, budgets, and project risks.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Conduct a basic asset-threat-vulnerability-control analysis.
Recommend proportionate administrative, technical, and physical controls.
Apply privacy, copyright, and intellectual-freedom questions to an ICT project.
Recognize common social-engineering attempts and report them appropriately.
Draft a project charter with scope, deliverables, roles, timeline, and risk register.
Explain when legal, IT, records, or community expertise is required.

Major concepts. Confidentiality; integrity; availability; asset; threat; vulnerability; risk; control; authentication; authorization; least privilege; encryption; incident; privacy impact assessment; copyright; fair use; intellectual freedom; project charter; scope; milestone; risk register.
Practical activity. Prepare a security, ethics, and project-start brief for a proposed small repository.
Expected output. Threat model, privacy checklist, rights workflow, one-page project charter, and risk register. This forms the ethics and preservation brief with Chapter 10.
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: Detailed Textbook Outline
section: Chapter 12. Data Literacy and Capstone Integration
text:
Chapter 12. Data Literacy and Capstone Integration
Summary. Students bring the course together by framing questions, evaluating data provenance and quality, cleaning and summarizing data, choosing honest visualizations, documenting limitations, and turning evidence into service decisions. Capstone planning emphasizes users, metadata, interoperability, ethics, sustainability, evaluation, and communication.
Learning outcomes. Students will be able to:

Formulate an answerable service question.
Assess a dataset for provenance, completeness, validity, consistency, and bias.
Produce a reproducible descriptive analysis and accessible visualization in R.
Interpret results without overstating what the data can support.
Integrate technical, metadata, user, ethical, and sustainability requirements in a capstone.
Present and defend a prototype or policy deliverable to mixed stakeholders.

Major concepts. Data literacy; provenance; data quality; tidy data; missingness; descriptive statistics; denominator; visualization; dashboard; inference; reproducibility; documentation; evaluation metric; theory of change; capstone.
Practical activity. Analyze a small anonymized service dataset in R and use the evidence to refine a capstone proposal.
Expected output. .qmd analysis, cleaned data, one table, one accessible chart, limitations statement, capstone artifact, presentation, and individual reflection.

Capstone options
Students choose one of the six options in Table 1. Each option demands an integrated response rather than a single technical artifact: the assessed evidence includes service rationale, metadata or data structures, user needs, ethical analysis, implementation documentation, evaluation, and sustainability.



Table 1: Capstone options and required integration





Option
Core artifact
Required integration




Campus digital exhibit
Curated exhibit prototype
Selection, description, rights, access, preservation


API-powered subject guide
Working guide and API workflow
API, data transformation, attribution, accessibility


Discovery improvement study
Evidence-based findings and redesign
Retrieval, metadata, UX testing, evaluation


Small repository policy pack
Policy and workflow set
Governance, metadata, preservation, security


Metadata cleanup and enrichment project
Cleaned records and enrichment log
Quality rules, automation, authority work, audit trail


Mobile service redesign
Tested mobile prototype
User research, accessibility, privacy, low-bandwidth design








Capstone rubric
Table 2 makes the capstone expectations visible early. Technical execution is important, but it receives only one portion of the grade; an impressive prototype that is inaccessible, poorly described, undocumented, or unsustainable does not meet LIS professional standards.



Table 2: Common capstone rubric





Criterion
Weight
Strong performance




Service problem and users
15%
Need and stakeholders are specific and evidence based


Metadata and data quality
20%
Structures and values are consistent, justified, and interoperable


Technical implementation
20%
Artifact works reliably; tests and limitations are documented


Ethics, rights, privacy, and accessibility
15%
Risks are identified and addressed through concrete choices


Evaluation and evidence
15%
Criteria and evidence support a defensible judgment


Documentation, sustainability, and communication
15%
Another team could understand, maintain, and explain the work








Recurring chapter design
Every full chapter follows the same learning rhythm: encounter a service problem, develop a vocabulary, construct a working model, practice with a small tool or dataset, examine an ethical tension, and transfer the learning to a case. This regularity reduces unnecessary cognitive load while allowing the technical content to grow more demanding.
Each lab also produces a portfolio object. By Week 12, the learner has maps, schemas, records, scripts, critiques, policies, and reflections from which to build a capstone. Short reflective prompts ask not only “Did it work?” but also “For whom did it work, under what assumptions, and who carries the risk when it fails?”
crumbs: Detailed Textbook Outline

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.1 Chapter overview
A student searches an online public access catalogue for a Filipino-language edition of a novel. The screen reports no results. What failed? The library may not own the edition. The record may use a different title, language code, or name form. The search index may be stale. The server may be unavailable. A network link may be slow. The mobile interface may hide useful filters. The student may not know the catalogue’s vocabulary. Each explanation points to a different part of the service, yet the user experiences only one outcome: “the library does not have it.”
The central problem of this chapter is therefore how to see an information service as a whole system. ICT work in LIS is not confined to repairing computers or teaching software. It involves designing and governing the connected arrangements through which people encounter collections and records. Infrastructure is often least visible when it works and most visible when it breaks. Star and Ruhleder (1996) argues that infrastructure is relational and embedded in practice: the same network, identifier, or standard can be enabling for one community and obstructive for another.
This systems view matters across LIS careers. A cataloguer’s authorized access point affects discovery. An archivist’s file-naming and preservation decisions affect future authenticity. A reference librarian’s chat platform affects privacy and reach. A records manager’s retention rule affects both accountability and exposure. A systems librarian’s authentication configuration affects who can use licensed resources from home. Technical and professional decisions are already intertwined.
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Define ICT, information infrastructure, information system, platform, interoperability, and sociotechnical system in LIS terms.
Identify the people, data, metadata, technology, policy, and workflow components of a library or information service.
Trace a user request through interface, application, metadata, data, and physical infrastructure layers.
Distinguish digitization, digitalization, automation, and digital transformation using concrete examples.
Diagnose likely points of service failure and connect them to affected stakeholders.
Evaluate an ICT choice using access, privacy, inclusion, reliability, sustainability, and accountability criteria.
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.3 Key concepts
Table 1.1 provides the working vocabulary for the chapter. Notice that several terms describe relationships rather than objects. For instance, interoperability is not a feature of one database in isolation; it describes whether systems can exchange and meaningfully use information.



Table 1.1: Key concepts for understanding ICT as service infrastructure





Term
Working definition




ICT
Technologies and practices used to create, process, store, communicate, secure, and use information.


Information infrastructure
The embedded people, institutions, standards, data, technologies, and routines that sustain information activity.


Sociotechnical system
An arrangement in which social and technical components shape one another.


Information system
An organized combination of people, processes, data, and technology that supports defined activities or decisions.


Data
Recorded representations of observations, entities, events, or transactions.


Metadata
Structured information that describes, manages, explains, locates, or preserves other information resources.


Hardware
Physical equipment such as servers, scanners, routers, storage devices, and user devices.


Software
Programs and instructions that direct computing activity.


Network
Connected devices and protocols that permit communication and resource sharing.


Platform
A technical and organizational base on which services, content, or interactions are built.


Workflow
A defined sequence of tasks, decisions, roles, and handoffs that produces an outcome.


Standard
An agreed specification or rule that promotes consistency and coordination.


Interoperability
The capacity of systems or organizations to exchange information and use it meaningfully.


Automation
The execution of a repeatable task or decision by a machine according to defined rules.


Digitization
Conversion of analog material into digital form.


Digitalization
Use of digital tools to improve an existing process or service.


Digital transformation
Substantial redesign of services, roles, or organizational models around digital possibilities and needs.


Availability
The degree to which an authorized user can obtain a service or resource when needed.


Stakeholder
A person, group, or institution that affects, uses, governs, or is affected by a service.


Digital divide
Unequal ability to obtain devices, connectivity, skills, support, and meaningful benefits from digital participation.
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.4 Core discussion

1.4.1 From tools to infrastructure
It is useful to know how to operate a spreadsheet, content management system, or scanner. Yet tool competence alone does not answer the questions LIS professionals face: What information should be collected? How should it be described? Who should have access? What happens when a vendor closes? How will a community correct harmful description? Can a user complete a task on an old phone over a slow connection? Who is accountable after a breach?
An infrastructure perspective widens the unit of analysis. Infrastructure includes visible components, such as computers and interfaces, and less visible components, such as classification practices, identifiers, procurement rules, staff expertise, maintenance schedules, electrical supply, and agreements with vendors. Borgman (2007) shows that information infrastructures support knowledge work through relationships among content, tools, communities, and institutions. They are built over time, inherit earlier decisions, and require continuing labor.
Consider an institutional repository. Its website is only the public surface. Behind it are submission policies, file-format rules, author names, department identifiers, metadata templates, copyright permissions, review workflows, storage systems, backups, domain registration, authentication, staff training, and preservation commitments. Changing one component may affect many others. A new login system can improve account security yet exclude alumni contributors. A cheaper cloud tier can reduce current expense yet increase retrieval fees or complicate exit. A metadata shortcut can accelerate ingest yet weaken later discovery.


1.4.2 ICT is a sociotechnical system
A sociotechnical system contains technical components and social components that mutually shape outcomes. People do not simply “use” a neutral technology after it has been designed. Their workarounds, skills, authority, incentives, languages, and values influence what the system becomes. Conversely, software defaults and data structures shape what staff can record and what users can find.
Suppose a school library adopts a barcode circulation system. The technical components include barcodes, scanners, computers, software, a database, and perhaps a network. The social components include borrowing rules, student identities, staff roles, training, consent and notice, exception handling, discipline practices, and expectations about confidentiality. If the software requires a permanent address but some learners do not have one, a database field becomes a social barrier. If staff share one administrator account because individual accounts are inconvenient, a workflow workaround becomes a security and accountability problem.
No single professional needs to master every layer. LIS competence includes knowing enough to ask precise questions, document requirements, recognize consequences, collaborate with IT staff and vendors, and protect professional values. “The system requires it” is rarely the end of an inquiry; requirements are produced by design choices, contracts, policies, and institutional priorities.


1.4.3 The layers of an information service
Figure 1.1 presents a simplified route through a discovery service. A user’s task enters through an interface. An application interprets the request. An index and metadata make matching possible. Data stores hold descriptions, content, accounts, or transactions. Networks, compute, storage, power, and facilities support every layer. People, policies, standards, and governance cross all layers rather than sitting at the bottom.






flowchart TB
    U["User need and context"] --> I["Interface: catalogue, app, website, kiosk"]
    I --> A["Application: search, circulation, repository workflow"]
    A --> M["Metadata and indexes: fields, terms, identifiers, rankings"]
    M --> D["Data and content: records, files, accounts, transactions"]
    D --> P["Physical and network infrastructure: devices, servers, storage, connectivity, power"]
    G["People, policy, standards, governance, and maintenance"] --- I
    G --- A
    G --- M
    G --- D
    G --- P
    P --> D
    D --> M
    M --> A
    A --> I
    I --> R["Service outcome"]




Figure 1.1: A layered model of an LIS information service





The arrows in Figure 1.1 run both ways because services are interactive. A search response returns from lower layers to the interface, while logs, corrections, and transactions may travel back into the system. The figure is deliberately not a vendor architecture. It is a thinking tool: when a service fails, ask what each layer contributed and which cross-cutting rule or responsibility governs the response.

1.4.3.1 Interface layer
The interface is the point of interaction: catalogue screen, website, mobile app, self-check machine, digital exhibit, or staff dashboard. It determines which actions are visible, what language is used, how errors are explained, and whether people using keyboards, screen readers, small screens, or slow networks can participate. Accessibility is therefore a system requirement, not a decorative feature added after launch. WCAG 2.2 provides testable guidance for perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust web content (World Wide Web Consortium 2023).


1.4.3.2 Application and workflow layer
The application performs service logic. A circulation module checks whether an item can be borrowed, calculates a due date, and records a transaction. A repository may route a submission from depositor to reviewer to publication. These rules encode policy. Staff should know which rules are configurable, who approved them, and how exceptions are handled.


1.4.3.3 Metadata and index layer
Metadata enables systems and people to identify, manage, retrieve, interpret, and preserve resources. NISO’s broad account includes descriptive, structural, and administrative functions (Understanding Metadata 2004). A title, creator identifier, subject term, rights statement, file checksum, and relationship between pages are all metadata, though they serve different purposes.
Metadata is central because retrieval cannot operate directly on meaning. Systems match representations: characters, fields, identifiers, terms, embeddings, links, and rules. If names are inconsistent or local-language terms are absent, an apparently sophisticated discovery system may still fail. MARC 21, for example, provides a structured format for communicating bibliographic data, but meaningful use also depends on cataloguing rules, controlled vocabularies, local policy, and system configuration (Library of Congress 2024). Chapter 4 examines these distinctions in depth.


1.4.3.4 Data and content layer
This layer includes bibliographic records, authority files, digital objects, finding aids, membership records, use logs, preservation metadata, and research datasets. Data have a lifecycle: they are collected or created, validated, stored, used, shared, retained, archived, or disposed of. Each step should have a purpose and an accountable role. “Keep everything” is not neutral; it increases security, privacy, discovery, storage, and governance burdens.


1.4.3.5 Physical and network layer
Digital services rely on material conditions: electricity, cables, radio links, routers, servers, storage media, cooling, devices, and repair capacity. A cloud service still runs on physical infrastructure owned and governed somewhere. In an archipelagic region, bandwidth, latency, weather, power stability, and last-mile connectivity can materially shape service design. ASEAN’s regional digital agenda recognizes both the economic role of digital infrastructure and persistent gaps in access and capability (Association of Southeast Asian Nations 2021).



1.4.4 Data, metadata, and knowledge organization
The boundary between data and metadata depends on context. In a catalogue, “Quezon City” may be metadata describing place of publication. In a geographic authority file, Quezon City may be the entity being described. The practical question is not “Is this always metadata?” but “What does this element describe, under which rules, for which function?”
Knowledge organization supplies intellectual and technical structures for bringing related resources together and distinguishing unlike resources. Classification, subject headings, taxonomies, thesauri, authority files, ontologies, and entity relationships all support navigation and retrieval. Their values are never merely mechanical. Terms may reflect the history and assumptions of the institutions that created them. Local description can repair gaps, but uncontrolled local changes can also weaken interoperability. LIS work therefore balances consistency, context, community language, and exchange.
Interoperability has several dimensions:

Technical interoperability means systems can connect and exchange bits through compatible protocols and formats.
Syntactic interoperability means exchanged data follow a shared structure, such as agreed JSON properties or MARC fields.
Semantic interoperability means participants interpret the data with sufficiently shared meaning.
Organizational interoperability means policies, responsibilities, permissions, and workflows allow the exchange to be used in practice.

A successful file transfer proves only the first two dimensions. If one repository uses “date” for creation date and another uses it for upload date, the same syntax can carry incompatible meaning. If two agencies agree on meaning but lack a lawful data-sharing basis, semantic agreement does not create organizational permission.


1.4.5 Standards, platforms, and local judgment
A standard coordinates practice by providing agreed specifications. Standards can support predictable exchange, reduce duplicated work, and help collections travel across systems. WorldCat, for example, depends on large-scale contribution and reuse of structured bibliographic records across institutions (OCLC 2025). Yet standards require interpretation and local policy. Adopting a field list without deciding who supplies values, which vocabulary is used, and how quality is checked does not create interoperable metadata.
A platform provides a base for multiple activities or services. An integrated library system may support acquisitions, cataloguing, circulation, serials, and reporting. A repository platform may support submission, review, publication, search, and statistics. Platforms are attractive because they integrate work, but they also concentrate dependencies. Institutions should consider data export, documentation, accessibility, security updates, local skills, cost over time, and an exit path before adoption.
Open-source software can offer inspectability and community control, but it is not cost-free. Hosting, configuration, upgrades, backups, training, and support require labor. Proprietary software can provide contracted support, but a contract does not eliminate risks related to lock-in, inaccessible design, weak export, price changes, or business closure. A professional evaluation asks how the choice fits the institution’s mission and capacity.


1.4.6 Digitization, digitalization, automation, and transformation
These four terms are related but not interchangeable. Table 1.2 compares them. The distinctions prevent organizations from calling every scan or subscription “transformation” and help teams set appropriate outcomes.



Table 1.2: Forms of digital change in information organizations





Term
Central change
LIS example
Success question




Digitization
Analog representation becomes digital data
Scanning a local newspaper and creating master image files
Is the digital surrogate accurate and documented?


Digitalization
An existing process uses digital tools
Replacing a paper room-booking log with an online form and calendar
Is the process more usable, reliable, and accountable?


Automation
A repeatable action is performed by programmed rules
Sending due-date reminders from circulation transactions
Are rules correct, exceptions visible, and results checked?


Digital transformation
Services, roles, governance, or models are substantially redesigned
Building a community-controlled digital heritage program with new consent, description, access, and preservation workflows
Does the redesign create lasting public value without transferring unacceptable risk?






As Table 1.2 shows, scale alone does not determine the category. Scanning one page and scanning one million pages are both digitization. Transformation concerns changes in relationships, responsibilities, and value creation. Automation is especially suitable for consistent, high-volume, low-ambiguity tasks, such as flagging blank required fields. Human review remains important where context, consequence, or contested meaning is high, such as deciding culturally appropriate access to sensitive community materials.


1.4.7 Reliability, maintenance, and graceful failure
Information services need more than a successful launch. They require maintenance: software updates, metadata correction, documentation, staff development, storage monitoring, accessibility testing, vendor management, and incident response. Maintenance is professional work even when it produces no dramatic new feature.
Availability is the ability of authorized users to obtain a service when needed. High availability may require redundant network links, monitored servers, backups, documented recovery procedures, and staff coverage. The appropriate level depends on the service. A public safety information portal and an internal exhibit-planning tool do not carry the same consequences of downtime.
Services should also fail gracefully. An unavailable catalogue can display a clear status message, alternative contact method, and realistic restoration estimate. A form can preserve a draft when connectivity drops. A digital exhibit can provide smaller images for low-bandwidth users. A circulation desk can maintain a documented offline procedure. Graceful failure treats disruption as a predictable condition rather than a personal failure by the user.


1.4.8 A service-centered decision framework
Before selecting or changing technology, ask:

Purpose: What service problem is being addressed, and what evidence shows it matters?
People: Who uses, operates, maintains, governs, and is affected by the service?
Information: What data, metadata, content, and records are needed? What should not be collected?
Workflow: What tasks, decisions, handoffs, exceptions, and approvals occur?
Technology: What devices, software, networks, integrations, and skills are required?
Values and obligations: How will the design protect access, privacy, intellectual freedom, accessibility, cultural rights, and accountability?
Continuity: How will the institution fund, document, maintain, preserve, evaluate, and eventually replace or retire the service?

This framework keeps procurement from becoming the first step. A product demonstration shows what a platform can do under selected conditions; requirements analysis identifies what the institution and its communities actually need.

Reflective prompt. Recall a digital service that made you feel competent and one that made you feel at fault. Which interface, metadata, policy, or support choices produced those feelings?
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

1.5.1 Academic library: remote access is more than a subscription
A Philippine state university licenses journals for students across several campuses. Buying access is only one component. Students need current accounts, understandable authentication, a working discovery route, device-compatible pages, sufficient bandwidth, and reference support. Librarians need usage evidence, privacy-conscious logs, license knowledge, and escalation contacts. When students share screenshots of articles because login repeatedly fails, the workaround signals an infrastructure problem rather than simply “user error.”


1.5.2 Public library: a low-bandwidth local information portal
A city public library develops a portal for disaster contacts, scholarship notices, livelihood resources, and local ordinances. A video-heavy homepage may look modern in the office yet perform poorly for residents using prepaid mobile data. The service team chooses compressed images, text-first pages, downloadable low-size PDFs, Filipino and local-language labels, clear update dates, and a telephone alternative. This is not a lesser website. It is a better fit between infrastructure and public purpose.


1.5.3 School library: accountable circulation automation
A school library replaces handwritten loans with a small integrated library system. Barcodes shorten transactions and reports reveal heavily used materials. At the same time, the library decides not to display students’ borrowing histories to teachers by default. It assigns individual staff accounts, documents corrections, sets a retention period for transaction histories, and keeps an offline procedure for power interruptions. Under the Philippine Data Privacy Act, personal information processing should have a lawful purpose and follow principles including transparency, legitimate purpose, and proportionality (Republic of the Philippines 2012; National Privacy Commission 2023).


1.5.4 Cultural heritage institution: description with communities
A regional museum digitizes photographs of an Indigenous community. Staff can scan the photographs and enter legacy captions, but technical completion does not settle questions of authority, sensitivity, or access. The institution consults community representatives about names, context, culturally restricted knowledge, acceptable online resolution, correction processes, and takedown requests. It records provenance and access decisions as metadata. Here, infrastructure includes relationships of trust and procedures for continued governance.


1.5.5 National and regional discovery
National libraries and archives increasingly provide digital collection access alongside on-site services; the National Library of the Philippines, for example, maintains web-based routes to collections and services (National Library of the Philippines 2024). Across Southeast Asia, institutions work in multilingual settings and under unequal connectivity, staffing, and funding conditions. Shared standards can improve exchange, but local scripts, names, colonial description, community protocols, and jurisdiction-specific law require informed adaptation rather than mechanical import.
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.6 Hands-on activity: Service infrastructure audit

1.6.1 Purpose
Map a familiar information service as a sociotechnical system and identify one improvement that is useful, ethical, and feasible.


1.6.2 Tools

A web browser and any familiar library or information service: catalogue, repository, library website, digital exhibit, circulation desk, online reference channel, or records-request portal
Paper and pen, a slide tool, or a diagram tool such as diagrams.net
A word processor or Quarto document for the final one-page report
Optional: browser developer tools for viewing network or mobile behavior



1.6.3 Steps

Choose one task. State it from the user’s perspective, such as “Find whether the library has an online copy of a thesis” or “Ask a reference question outside opening hours.” Keep the task narrow.
Perform and record. Complete the task once. Record the starting point, major actions, messages, result, time, and any uncertainty. Do not enter real sensitive data for this exercise.
Map the components. Identify the interface, application or workflow, metadata and indexes, stored data or content, physical/network dependencies, people and roles, policies or standards, and external services.
Trace the information. Show what the user supplies, what the system returns, and what data or logs may remain afterward.
Test one disruption. Without damaging the service, simulate or inspect one constraint: narrow the browser window, use a browser’s offline mode after loading, disable images, search a spelling variant, or try keyboard-only navigation.
Identify failures and impacts. List at least three possible failure points, the people affected, the visible symptom, and one preventive or recovery measure.
Recommend one improvement. Use the service-centered decision framework. Explain the benefit, required work, possible risk, responsible role, and simple success measure.
Reflect. Answer the question below in about 150 words.



1.6.4 Expected output
Submit one PDF, DOCX, HTML page, or .qmd containing:

a task statement and brief observation log;
a labeled service map with at least eight components;
a failure-and-impact table with at least three rows;
one prioritized recommendation; and
a 150-word reflection.


Reflection question. Which part of the service was least visible before the audit, and how did recognizing it change your judgment about who is responsible for service quality?



1.6.5 Activity checklist
Use the checklist in Table 1.3 before submission. The checklist emphasizes traceable reasoning rather than graphic polish.



Table 1.3: Service infrastructure audit submission checklist





Check
Complete




Task is specific and user centered
[ ]


Map includes social and technical components
[ ]


Information inputs, outputs, and traces are shown
[ ]


At least three failures connect cause to user impact
[ ]


Recommendation names owner, risk, and measure
[ ]


No real personal or confidential data appear
[ ]


Reflection explains a changed understanding
[ ]








1.6.6 Short lab rubric
Table 1.4 allocates 20 points. A map can be simple and still earn full credit if its relationships are accurate and clearly explained.



Table 1.4: Rubric for the service infrastructure audit





Criterion
Points
Full credit




Task and observation
3
Task is bounded; observations separate evidence from assumption


Systems map
6
At least eight relevant components and meaningful relationships are labeled


Failure analysis
4
Three plausible failures connect causes, symptoms, stakeholders, and responses


Recommendation
4
Improvement is feasible and addresses benefit, risk, owner, and measure


Reflection and presentation
3
Reflection is specific; output is readable, accessible, and privacy conscious
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
section: 1.7 Ethical and professional issue: convenience, privacy, and unequal access
text:
1.7 Ethical and professional issue: convenience, privacy, and unequal access
Digital services often promise convenience through personalization, analytics, and persistent accounts. A discovery system may remember searches; a chat service may keep transcripts; a building-entry system may record visits; an e-book platform may observe reading behavior. Each feature can support service improvement, but each also creates information about people.
LIS ethics gives special weight to privacy because inquiry can reveal political belief, health concern, religious interest, legal difficulty, identity, or personal vulnerability. The IFLA code connects privacy, confidentiality, equitable service, access to information, and professional responsibility (International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions 2012). Privacy is not the same as secrecy. It concerns a person’s ability to understand and influence how information about them is collected, used, shared, retained, and secured.
The Philippine Data Privacy Act applies principles of transparency, legitimate purpose, and proportionality to personal information processing (Republic of the Philippines 2012). For an ICT project, these principles lead to practical questions:

Is each collected field necessary for a stated service purpose?
Is the notice understandable at the moment of collection?
Can the task be completed with less identification or shorter retention?
Who can view the data, and are privileges reviewed?
Does a vendor reuse or transfer the information?
What happens when a person seeks access, correction, or deletion where applicable?
How will a breach be detected, contained, documented, and communicated?

Privacy can also conflict with accessibility and inclusion if handled simplistically. Requiring an app-based one-time password may strengthen authentication while excluding users without compatible phones or stable signals. Removing all service data may protect privacy but prevent the library from detecting systematic exclusion. Good governance seeks proportionate, documented solutions: collect the minimum useful data, restrict access, aggregate where possible, retain only as justified, offer accessible alternatives, and evaluate outcomes.
Digital exclusion is broader than whether a connection exists. Meaningful access also depends on affordability, device quality, language, accessibility, skills, time, confidence, and support. The 2022 IFLA-UNESCO Public Library Manifesto explicitly includes access through digital technologies and the development of information and digital literacy within contemporary public library missions (International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions and UNESCO 2022). An online-only service can therefore expand reach for some people while withdrawing it from others.

Ethical checkpoint. A vendor offers a free discovery interface in exchange for using search logs to improve commercial products. What information would you need before recommending acceptance? Who should take part in the decision?
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.8 Review questions

Identification: What is a sociotechnical system?
Identification: Define metadata and give one descriptive and one administrative example.
Short answer: Why is a library website an interface rather than the entire information service?
Short answer: Distinguish technical, syntactic, semantic, and organizational interoperability.
Short answer: Explain why cloud services still depend on physical infrastructure.
Applied analysis: A repository accepts PDF files but records all dates in one field without defining what the date means. Which dimensions of interoperability may succeed, and which may fail?
Applied analysis: A public library scans rare local newspapers but does not create titles, dates, page order, rights information, or backups. What has it accomplished, and what service and stewardship work remains?
Scenario: Students repeatedly share one staff account to operate a circulation system. Identify two risks and two improvements that consider both workflow and security.
Scenario: A library replaces all in-person renewals with a mobile app. Evaluate the change using access, privacy, reliability, and accountability criteria.
Synthesis: Choose one LIS role and explain how a technical choice in that role can affect discovery, preservation, or user rights elsewhere in the system.
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
section: 1.9 Mini case: The provincial heritage portal
text:
1.9 Mini case: The provincial heritage portal
The Provincial Library and Archives receives a grant to build an online heritage portal before the province’s founding anniversary. The collection includes Spanish-period documents, postwar photographs, oral-history recordings, and recent community festival videos. The grant budget covers scanning equipment and one year of commercial hosting. A supplier promises to launch within three months using automatically generated descriptions. The archives has two staff members, neither assigned full-time to the project. Some photographs name children; several oral histories discuss land disputes; community representatives have not yet been consulted. Internet service is intermittent in upland municipalities, and the contract does not state how data can be exported when hosting ends.
Guide questions

Map at least eight social and technical components that must be included in the project beyond scanning equipment and a website.
Identify the three most serious risks. For each, name the affected stakeholders and one immediate mitigation.
Recommend a realistic first phase that could produce public value while protecting rights and improving long-term continuity.
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure
text:
1.10 Further reading and tools

1.10.1 Readings and standards

IFLA-UNESCO Public Library Manifesto 2022. A concise statement of public-library missions in access, culture, education, inclusion, and digital participation (International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions and UNESCO 2022).
NISO, Understanding Metadata. An accessible introduction to metadata functions and concepts (Understanding Metadata 2004).
Library of Congress MARC 21 documentation. A primary reference for the structure and communication of bibliographic records (Library of Congress 2024).
WCAG 2.2. The W3C standard used to evaluate web accessibility (World Wide Web Consortium 2023).
Republic Act No. 10173 and National Privacy Commission guidance. Primary Philippine references for personal data protection (Republic of the Philippines 2012; National Privacy Commission 2023).
Star and Ruhleder’s infrastructure framework. A foundational scholarly account of infrastructure as relational, embedded, learned, and visible upon breakdown (Star and Ruhleder 1996).



1.10.2 Open and widely accessible tools

diagrams.net: browser-based system and workflow diagramming.
LibreOffice: open-source word processing, spreadsheets, presentations, and database tools.
Zotero: open-source reference collection and citation management.
OpenRefine: interactive cleaning and reconciliation of messy structured data.
DB Browser for SQLite: graphical introduction to relational databases and SQL.
R and RStudio or Positron: reproducible data analysis and automation (R Core Team 2026).
Quarto: open-source publishing from Markdown and executable code to HTML, DOCX, PDF, and other formats (Posit 2026).



1.10.3 Chapter 1 bridge
The next chapter opens the network and web layers shown in Figure 1.1. You will trace how a browser locates a server, requests a resource, receives HTML, and presents a library page. Keep the systems map from this chapter: in Chapter 2, you will revise it with protocols, addresses, security, hosting, and web accessibility.




Association of Southeast Asian Nations. 2021. ASEAN Digital Masterplan 2025. https://asean.org/book/asean-digital-masterplan-2025/.


Borgman, Christine L. 2007. Scholarship in the Digital Age: Information, Infrastructure, and the Internet. MIT Press. https://doi.org/10.7551/mitpress/7434.001.0001.


International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions. 2012. IFLA Code of Ethics for Librarians and Other Information Workers. IFLA. https://www.ifla.org/g/faife/professional-codes-of-ethics-for-librarians/.


International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions, and UNESCO. 2022. IFLA-UNESCO Public Library Manifesto 2022. IFLA. https://repository.ifla.org/handle/123456789/2006.


Library of Congress. 2024. MARC 21 Format for Bibliographic Data. https://www.loc.gov/marc/bibliographic/.


National Library of the Philippines. 2024. NLP Digital Collections. https://web.nlp.gov.ph/.


National Privacy Commission. 2023. Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://privacy.gov.ph/data-privacy-act/.


OCLC. 2025. Inside WorldCat. https://www.oclc.org/en/worldcat/inside-worldcat.html.


Posit. 2026. Quarto Documentation. https://quarto.org/docs/guide/.


R Core Team. 2026. R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing. R Foundation for Statistical Computing. https://www.R-project.org/.


Republic of the Philippines. 2012. Republic Act No. 10173: Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://lawphil.net/statutes/repacts/ra2012/ra_10173_2012.html.


Star, Susan Leigh, and Karen Ruhleder. 1996. “Steps Toward an Ecology of Infrastructure: Design and Access for Large Information Spaces.” Information Systems Research 7 (1): 111–34. https://doi.org/10.1287/isre.7.1.111.


Understanding Metadata (2004). https://www.niso.org/publications/understanding-metadata-2017.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2023. Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2. https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/.
crumbs: 1  ICT in LIS as Service Infrastructure

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
2.1 Chapter overview
A library website may be open twenty-four hours a day, but that does not mean every user can reach or use it. A student taps a database link on a phone. The page takes too long to load, redirects to an unfamiliar login screen, and reports that the session has expired. A parent searches a public-library site for today’s opening hours but finds an outdated poster. A researcher follows a repository link from a search engine and receives a “404 Not Found” response. These are not merely website inconveniences. They are failures in the delivery of information services.
The central problem of this chapter is how to make a library service reliably reachable and understandable across networks, browsers, devices, and user contexts. Solving that problem requires more than visual design. It requires a working model of networks, internet addressing, the Domain Name System (DNS), URLs, HTTP and HTTPS, servers, browsers, HTML, CSS, content management, accessibility, performance, and website governance.
Chapter 1 described an information service as a layered sociotechnical system. This chapter opens two of those layers: network infrastructure and the web interface. You will trace a request from a browser to a library server and back, then create a small semantic web page. The aim is not to train you as a network engineer or professional web developer. It is to give you enough technical understanding to diagnose service problems, communicate requirements, maintain trustworthy content, and collaborate effectively with IT staff, vendors, designers, and users.
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
2.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Distinguish the internet, the World Wide Web, local networks, and web-based library services.
Diagram a client-server exchange involving a device, network, DNS, server, HTTP request, and response.
Interpret the principal parts of a URL and common HTTP status codes.
Explain how HTML, CSS, and JavaScript contribute different functions to a web page.
Create a small semantic HTML page with headings, links, lists, contact information, and basic accessibility features.
Evaluate a library website for findability, security, accessibility, mobile use, low-bandwidth performance, and governance.
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
2.3 Key concepts
Table 2.1 introduces the vocabulary used throughout the chapter. Several concepts operate at different layers. DNS helps a client locate a server, while HTML gives structure and meaning to the document returned by that server.



Table 2.1: Key concepts for networking and library websites





Term
Working definition




Network
Connected devices that exchange data and share resources.


LAN
A local area network covering a limited site such as a building or campus.


WAN
A wide area network connecting sites across larger geographic areas.


Internet
The global system of interconnected networks using the Internet Protocol suite.


World Wide Web
Interlinked resources identified by URLs and transferred mainly through HTTP or HTTPS.


Protocol
An agreed set of rules for communication between systems.


Packet
A formatted unit into which data is divided for network transmission.


Bandwidth
The amount of data a connection can carry during a period of time.


Latency
The delay between sending a request and receiving a response.


Client
A device or program that requests a service or resource.


Server
A device or program that responds to requests and provides resources.


IP address
A numerical address used to identify and route traffic to a network interface.


Domain name
A human-readable name associated with internet resources.


DNS
The distributed system that translates domain names into network information.


URL
A structured address identifying a resource and how to access it.


HTTP
The application protocol used to request and transfer web representations.


HTTPS
HTTP protected by Transport Layer Security.


Browser
Software that requests, interprets, and presents web resources.


HTML
The markup language representing the structure and meaning of web content.


CSS
A style language controlling presentation and responsive layout.


JavaScript
A programming language adding browser behavior and dynamic interaction.


Hosting
Server, storage, network, and related resources that make a site available.


CMS
A content management system for creating, organizing, and publishing content.


Responsive design
Layouts and content that adapt to different screens and devices.


Semantic HTML
HTML elements used according to meaning and purpose rather than appearance alone.
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
2.4 Core discussion

2.4.1 Networks as service infrastructure
A network exists when devices exchange data according to agreed rules. In a small library, a local area network may connect staff computers, printers, self-check stations, wireless access points, an on-site server, and a router. A university may connect several campus libraries through a larger institutional network. Remote branches, cloud systems, and vendor platforms depend on wide area and internet connections.
Networks allow information organizations to share catalogues, storage, authentication, printers, internet access, and applications. They also create dependencies. If the local network fails, a cloud-hosted circulation system may be unreachable even though the vendor server operates normally. If the internet connection works but DNS fails, a service may be reachable by numerical address while its familiar name appears unavailable. Diagnosis begins by locating the layer at which communication stopped.
Network data are divided into packets, transmitted through links and routing devices, and reassembled at the destination. Two measures help explain user experience:

Bandwidth concerns how much data a connection can carry. Large images and video load slowly on a low-bandwidth link.
Latency concerns delay. Even a small page can feel slow when it requires many round trips over a high-latency connection.

Bandwidth and latency are not interchangeable. Website teams should measure whether users can complete real tasks rather than treating one advertised connection speed as a full description of service quality.


2.4.2 Internet and web are not synonyms
The internet is the global network infrastructure. The World Wide Web is one service that uses it. Email, file transfer, voice communication, time synchronization, and many APIs also use the internet but are not identical to the web.
The distinction matters. A library can have a working internet connection while its website is unavailable. A catalogue hosted inside a building may work on the LAN during an internet outage but be unreachable from home. An offline exhibit installed on a kiosk can use web technologies without being published on the internet. Accurate language helps teams identify the actual service boundary.


2.4.3 Protocols and layers
A protocol is an agreed set of communication rules. At a simplified level, IP supports addressing and routing; TCP supports a reliable ordered connection for many applications; DNS associates human-readable names with network information; HTTP defines web requests and responses; and TLS protects data in transit for HTTPS.
Students do not need to memorize the full protocol stack. The useful habit is to ask which protocol is responsible for which task. A DNS problem, an expired TLS certificate, and an HTTP “Not Found” response can all block access, but they require different remedies and responsible staff.


2.4.4 From a URL to a page
Figure 2.1 traces a simplified request for a library-hours page. The browser interprets the URL, asks DNS for an address, connects through network infrastructure, establishes a protected session for HTTPS, sends an HTTP request, receives a response, and interprets the returned HTML and related resources.






sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant B as Browser client
    participant D as DNS resolver
    participant N as Networks
    participant S as Library web server
    U->>B: Enter or follow an HTTPS URL
    B->>D: Request address for the domain
    D-->>B: Return network address
    B->>N: Connect and establish protected session
    N->>S: Deliver HTTP request
    S-->>N: Return status, headers, and representation
    N-->>B: Deliver HTTP response
    B->>B: Interpret HTML, CSS, and other resources
    B-->>U: Present the page and actions




Figure 2.1: A simplified request path from a library user to a web service





Figure 2.1 reveals that the browser is an active client. It sends headers, manages cache entries and cookies, validates secure connections, constructs a document model, applies styles, and may run scripts. Browser differences, extensions, privacy settings, and older devices can therefore affect service behavior.


2.4.5 IP addresses, domain names, and DNS
An IP address identifies a network interface for communication and routing. People remember names more easily than numerical addresses, so services use names such as library.example.edu.
DNS is a distributed hierarchical naming system rather than one universal address book. Its foundational design separates the name space from the many servers that answer queries about portions of it (Mockapetris 1987). DNS information is cached for a defined period, so changes may not appear everywhere immediately.
Domain governance is an LIS concern because names support continuity, trust, and citation. An institution should know who controls registration, who manages DNS records, when registrations and certificates expire, how changes are authorized, and which services depend on each name. A public service should not depend permanently on a domain registered under one employee’s private account.


2.4.6 Reading a URL
Consider:
https://library.example.edu/research/guides.html?subject=history#databases
Its scheme is https; its host is library.example.edu; its path is /research/guides.html; its query is subject=history; and its fragment is databases. URLs appear in catalogues, citations, QR codes, syllabi, and archival records. A stable URL policy therefore supports discovery and continuity.
Changing a URL without a redirect breaks inbound links. A migration should inventory important URLs, map old addresses to new ones, configure permanent redirects, and monitor “Not Found” reports. Human-readable paths help users judge purpose, but appearance alone is not proof of safety. Users should inspect the complete host name before entering credentials.


2.4.7 HTTP requests and responses
HTTP is a request-response protocol. RFC 9110 defines current HTTP semantics for methods, status codes, fields, and representations (Fielding et al. 2022). A request includes a method, target, headers, and sometimes a body. A response includes a status code, headers, and often HTML, JSON, an image, or a PDF.
Common methods include GET for retrieving a representation, HEAD for retrieving response metadata, POST for submitting data, and PUT or PATCH for creating or modifying resources in systems that support them.
Table 2.2 lists status codes that LIS staff may encounter. A status is evidence, not a complete diagnosis. A 404 response says that the server found no current resource at that path; it does not explain whether the resource was removed, renamed, mistyped, or migrated without a redirect.



Table 2.2: Common HTTP status codes in information services





Code
Meaning
LIS interpretation




200
OK
The requested representation was returned.


301
Moved Permanently
Use the new stable location; old links should normally redirect.


302
Temporary redirect
The client is being sent temporarily elsewhere.


400
Bad Request
Inspect the URL, form, or submitted data.


401
Authentication required
The user must authenticate or supplied credentials failed.


403
Forbidden
The server understood the request but refuses access.


404
Not Found
Check deletion, migration, spelling, and link maintenance.


429
Too Many Requests
Relevant to APIs, crawlers, and automated harvesting.


500
Internal Server Error
The application failed while processing the request.


503
Service Unavailable
The service is temporarily unavailable because of maintenance or overload.








2.4.8 HTTPS, certificates, and trust
HTTPS protects HTTP traffic with TLS. Correct configuration supports confidentiality, integrity in transit, and server authentication. It does not prove that content is accurate or ethical, and it does not protect data after they reach an insecure server. It secures an important channel, not the entire service.
Certificate warnings may indicate expiration, a host-name mismatch, incomplete configuration, or interception. Libraries should monitor certificates and renew them before expiration. Staff should never make bypassing a warning the routine solution.


2.4.9 HTML, CSS, and JavaScript
The browser commonly receives HTML, then requests style sheets, scripts, images, and fonts. Figure 2.2 separates three technologies that beginners often confuse.






flowchart LR
    H["HTML<br/>structure and meaning"] --> P["Usable web page"]
    C["CSS<br/>presentation and responsive layout"] --> P
    J["JavaScript<br/>behavior and interaction"] --> P
    S["Content, metadata, links, and policy"] --> H
    A["Accessibility and progressive enhancement"] --- H
    A --- C
    A --- J




Figure 2.2: The complementary roles of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript





HTML identifies headings, paragraphs, navigation, lists, forms, tables, and images. CSS controls presentation and responsive layout. JavaScript adds behavior such as menu expansion, form validation, and asynchronous updates. The WHATWG standard defines HTML as a language for structuring web documents and applications (WHATWG 2026).
As Figure 2.2 shows, technology does not replace content governance. A large bold paragraph may look like a heading but is not programmatically identified as one. A clickable generic container styled like a button lacks the native semantics and behavior of a real button. Semantic HTML helps browsers, screen readers, search engines, and maintenance tools interpret meaning.


2.4.10 A minimal semantic library page
This example publishes an essential service with meaningful structure:
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>Library Hours | Mabini Community Library</title>
</head>
<body>
  <header>
    <h1>Mabini Community Library</h1>
    <nav aria-label="Primary">
      <a href="index.html">Home</a>
      <a href="catalogue.html">Catalogue</a>
      <a href="contact.html">Contact</a>
    </nav>
  </header>
  <main>
    <h2>Library hours</h2>
    <p>Monday-Friday: 8:00 a.m.-5:00 p.m.</p>
    <p>Saturday: 9:00 a.m.-12:00 noon</p>
    <h2>Before you visit</h2>
    <ul>
      <li>Bring an ID for registration.</li>
      <li>Contact us before requesting archival materials.</li>
    </ul>
  </main>
  <footer>
    <address>Email: <a href="mailto:library@example.org">library@example.org</a></address>
    <p>Last reviewed: 18 June 2026</p>
  </footer>
</body>
</html>
The language attribute supports language-aware tools. The character encoding supports consistent display. The viewport instruction supports mobile layouts. The title distinguishes the page in browser tabs and search results. Headings communicate organization, link text names destinations, and the review date supports content governance.


2.4.11 Progressive enhancement and resilience
Progressive enhancement begins with meaningful content and basic functions that work under varied conditions, then adds richer presentation and behavior. Opening hours should remain readable if a script fails. A catalogue destination should be a real link. A form should retain understandable labels when visual styling is unavailable.
This approach does not prohibit modern applications. It asks teams to identify the essential service and protect it from avoidable dependencies. Resilience matters for older devices, assistive technologies, intermittent signals, data-saving settings, and restricted institutional computers.


2.4.12 Library websites as service gateways
A library website is not an online brochure. It connects users to catalogues, databases, repositories, guides, reference help, policies, events, spaces, accounts, and staff expertise. Its information architecture should reflect user tasks rather than the institution’s internal chart.
Common tasks include checking hours, finding a known item, searching a topic, accessing electronic resources, renewing materials, asking for help, learning access rules, and verifying whether information is current and official.
Library usability research demonstrates the value of observing users rather than assuming professional terms are self-explanatory (Emde et al. 2009).
