Accessibility research similarly shows that meaningful access requires continuing attention rather than a one-time launch check (Billingham 2014). Recent homepage work still connects structural and visual decisions with usability and accessibility (Vargas 2026).


2.4.13 Content management and hosting
A content management system may provide templates, navigation, revision histories, user roles, scheduled publication, and approval workflows. It can improve distributed publishing, but it creates governance and security work. Plugins require updates, accounts require review, themes may introduce accessibility problems, and old pages may remain searchable. The institution still needs content owners, review schedules, backup procedures, and an exit plan.
Hosting may use institutional servers, a managed provider, cloud infrastructure, or a hosted software service. The choice affects control, expertise, security updates, cost, performance, jurisdiction, backup, export, and vendor dependence. “In the cloud” does not remove institutional responsibility.
Remote access may involve a proxy, VPN, or federated identity service. Each transition should tell users where they are going and why authentication is required. Repeated redirects and unclear domains can make legitimate access resemble phishing. Libraries should document operators, data exchange, support responsibility, and what happens when a subscription ends.


2.4.14 Accessibility, mobile use, and performance
WCAG 2.2 requires web content to be perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust (World Wide Web Consortium 2023). Mobile accessibility is not separate: small screens, touch input, orientation, magnification, and device constraints interact with the same principles (World Wide Web Consortium 2015).
Beginner checks include meaningful titles, logical headings, text alternatives, form labels, keyboard operation, adequate contrast, text reflow, clear link purpose, and understandable errors. Accessibility benefits many users, but that broad benefit should not erase the specific rights and expertise of persons with disabilities.
Performance is also an access issue. Large posters, multiple fonts, trackers, and third-party widgets consume data and add failure points. Regional digital plans recognize that meaningful participation depends on both infrastructure and capability (Association of Southeast Asian Nations 2021). Practical measures include compressing images, avoiding autoplay, limiting external scripts, offering text alternatives, making essential information available without accounts, and testing on a low-cost phone and constrained connection.


2.4.15 Website governance
Library websites are published information resources and organizational records. Table 2.3 connects components to accountable questions. No component is purely technical: each has a service owner, policy implication, and maintenance need.



Table 2.3: Website governance responsibilities and evidence





Component
Accountable question
Evidence




Domain and DNS
Who controls registration, records, renewal, and emergency changes?
Domain inventory and renewal record


Hosting and platform
Who patches, monitors, backs up, and supports the service?
Agreement and maintenance log


Page content
Who owns accuracy, accessibility, approval, and review dates?
Content register and review history


Links and redirects
Who tests destinations and preserves high-value old URLs?
Link report and redirect map


Accounts and permissions
Who receives privileges, and when are they reviewed or removed?
Role list and access review


Analytics and logs
What is collected, why, for how long, and with whose access?
Data inventory and retention rule


Backups and archives
What can be restored or preserved, and has recovery been tested?
Backup log, archive policy, and recovery test






As Table 2.3 suggests, publishing is a lifecycle. A page needs a purpose, audience, owner, approval status, review date, and eventual disposition. Event notices expire quickly; policy pages need revision histories; research guides require subject review; and significant exhibits may warrant preservation.

Reflective prompt. Recall a website that prevented you from completing a task. Which layer failed: connection, naming, security, response, page structure, content, accessibility, or governance? What evidence supports your diagnosis?
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
2.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

2.5.1 State university library: the remote-access chain
A Philippine state university links subscribed databases from its library website. On campus, access is recognized by network address. Off campus, students authenticate through a proxy. Success depends on current vendor links, DNS, proxy configuration, identity records, HTTPS certificates, license rules, and help documentation. When a vendor changes its domain, old proxy links fail. The library responds with a link inventory, off-campus testing, clear escalation information, and coordinated updates.


2.5.2 Municipal library: hours during a typhoon
A municipal library normally posts hours as a colorful image. During a typhoon, staff update social media but forget the website image and PDF. Residents see three schedules. The library redesigns the page so current status appears as lightweight HTML with an update time. One role approves emergency updates, and expired notices are removed through a documented workflow.


2.5.3 School library: service during internet outages
A rural school library uses a local server to provide reading materials over campus Wi-Fi. The collection remains available inside the school when the external connection fails. Staff distinguish the local web from the internet, maintain offline backups, and use a text-first interface suitable for older phones.


2.5.4 Regional heritage portal: multilingual stable links
A Southeast Asian heritage consortium aggregates collections from several museums. Partners use different scripts, languages, domains, and systems. They adopt Unicode, language metadata, institutional identifiers, persistent item URLs, and redirects. The interface presents multilingual values and provenance rather than replacing local description with one regional label.


2.5.5 Public information service: external maps and privacy
A public library embeds a commercial map and analytics on every page. The map helps some visitors but adds tracking and a failure dependency. The library provides its address, landmarks, transport directions, and telephone number as HTML before the map. It then evaluates whether analytics can collect less data.
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
section: 2.6 Hands-on activity: Trace and build a library web page
text:
2.6 Hands-on activity: Trace and build a library web page

2.6.1 Purpose
Trace a real web request, then create a semantic HTML page that communicates an essential library service under mobile and low-bandwidth conditions.


2.6.2 Tools

A browser with developer tools, such as Firefox, Chrome, or Edge
A text editor such as Notepad, Visual Studio Code, or Positron
The W3C Nu HTML Checker at https://validator.w3.org/nu/
A folder named chapter2-lab



2.6.3 Steps

Open a library, archive, museum, or repository page that requires no personal information.
Record its URL and label the scheme, host, path, query, and fragment where present.
Open the browser Network panel, reload, and select the main document request.
Record the method, status, content type, transferred size, and domain.
Count contacted domains and distinguish institutional from external services.
Use a narrow mobile viewport to find hours, the catalogue, or contact help.
Create hours.html with a document type, language, encoding, viewport metadata, and unique title.
Add header, navigation, main, and footer landmarks with one h1 and logical h2 headings.
Add fictional hours, a status message, visit requirements, contacts, and a review date.
Add three meaningful links. If using an image, resize it and supply useful alternative text.
Test two viewport widths, validate the HTML, and correct errors you understand.
Ask a peer to locate Saturday hours and a contact method without explanation.



2.6.4 Expected output
Submit the annotated request record, three network and mobile observations, hours.html, validation evidence, the peer-task result, and a 150-word reflection.

Reflection question. Which improvement came from understanding the network, which came from HTML structure, and which came from observing a user?



2.6.5 Activity checklist
Table 2.4 moves from technical validity to service validity. The page must load and be structured correctly, but it must also communicate current, usable information.



Table 2.4: Chapter 2 web lab submission checklist





Check
Complete




URL parts, method, status, type, size, and domain are recorded
[ ]


External domains and one dependency risk are identified
[ ]


Document includes language, encoding, viewport, and unique title
[ ]


Semantic landmarks and logical headings are used
[ ]


Essential hours and contact information are HTML text
[ ]


Links have meaningful text and work locally
[ ]


Page works at narrow width and without its optional image
[ ]


Validation and peer-task results are documented
[ ]


No real personal or confidential information is included
[ ]








2.6.6 Short lab rubric
Table 2.5 allocates 20 points. Visual sophistication is not a scoring category; clarity, semantics, resilience, and evidence matter more.



Table 2.5: Rubric for the Chapter 2 web lab





Criterion
Points
Full credit




Request trace
4
Records request evidence accurately and identifies a dependency


Semantic HTML
6
Uses valid metadata, landmarks, headings, lists, links, and contact structure


Access and resilience
4
Essential content works at narrow width without images or complex scripts


Testing and correction
3
Reports validation and peer testing and makes evidence-based corrections


Reflection and presentation
3
Explains learning across network, document, and user layers
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
section: 2.7 Ethical and professional issue: access, surveillance, and secure delivery
text:
2.7 Ethical and professional issue: access, surveillance, and secure delivery
Library websites can broaden access while excluding, observing, or endangering users. An online-only form may disadvantage people without stable connectivity, compatible devices, data allowance, accessible design, or required identification. The IFLA-UNESCO Public Library Manifesto connects digital access with inclusion and equitable service (International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions and UNESCO 2022). Digital channels should expand options rather than silently eliminate necessary alternatives.
Web requests can disclose IP addresses, device information, referring pages, cookies, and behavioral patterns. Third-party fonts, maps, videos, chat widgets, and social-media embeds may transmit data before intentional interaction. Before adding one, ask what is sent, to whom, for what purpose, for how long, and whether the essential service works without it.
The Philippine Data Privacy Act’s principles of transparency, legitimate purpose, and proportionality apply to forms, logs, analytics, and vendor integrations (Republic of the Philippines 2012; National Privacy Commission 2023). A notice cannot by itself legitimize unnecessary collection.
HTTPS should be standard, especially for authentication, forms, and accounts. Interface choices also influence security. A database link that silently opens an unfamiliar domain and asks for credentials may train users to ignore phishing indicators. The page should name the provider or authentication step, use consistent domains where possible, and offer a trusted help route.
Security messages should support rather than shame users. “Your session expired. Sign in again through the library portal” is more useful than “Invalid error.” Good error design helps distinguish a normal interruption from a suspicious request.

Ethical checkpoint. A free chat widget permits evening reference service, but its vendor stores transcripts and device identifiers in another jurisdiction. What benefit, data flow, contract term, alternative, and user notice should be examined?
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
2.8 Review questions

Identification: What is the difference between the internet and the World Wide Web?
Identification: Define bandwidth and latency. How can each affect a library website?
Short answer: What role does DNS play after a user enters a domain name?
Short answer: Identify the URL parts in https://archive.example.ph/items/42?view=full#description.
Short answer: Distinguish the roles of HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
Applied analysis: A catalogue works on staff computers but not from homes. Give three plausible causes from different layers and one test for each.
Applied analysis: After a repository migration, search results lead to 404 responses. What should the team have prepared, and what should it do now?
Scenario: A homepage loads 12 MB of poster images before showing hours. Evaluate access, performance, metadata, and maintenance.
Scenario: A browser reports an invalid certificate. Why should staff not tell users to bypass the warning?
Synthesis: Choose one component from Table 2.3 and explain how its maintenance connects to an LIS responsibility.
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
section: 2.9 Mini case: The multicampus library website
text:
2.9 Mini case: The multicampus library website
Eastern Bay University has three campuses and a growing distance-learning program. Each library maintains separate CMS pages. Labels differ: one says “OPAC,” another “Books,” and the third “Library Search.” Hours are poster images, two from last semester. Database links work on the main campus but fail for many remote students. The website loads six tracking and social-media domains. A screen-reader user reports repeated unlabeled links. Communications controls templates, IT controls authentication and DNS, vendors control database platforms, and no office believes it owns the complete journey.
Guide questions

Map the technical and organizational components from homepage to authenticated database article. Where should evidence be collected?
Prioritize five improvements using impact, urgency, effort, accessibility, privacy, and institutional control.
Propose governance naming content owners, technical owners, review intervals, escalation paths, and two success measures.
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites
text:
2.10 Further reading and tools

2.10.1 Standards and documentation

RFC 1034, Domain Names—Concepts and Facilities. Foundational DNS concepts (Mockapetris 1987).
RFC 9110, HTTP Semantics. HTTP methods, status codes, fields, and representations (Fielding et al. 2022).
WHATWG HTML Living Standard. The normative reference for HTML semantics (WHATWG 2026).
WCAG 2.2. Testable web accessibility requirements (World Wide Web Consortium 2023).
W3C mobile accessibility guidance. Application of accessibility guidance to mobile contexts (World Wide Web Consortium 2015).



2.10.2 LIS readings

Emde et al. (2009) demonstrates task-based testing with academic-library users.
Billingham (2014) discusses improving academic-library access for people with disabilities.
Vargas (2026) connects homepage structure and aesthetics with usability and accessibility.



2.10.3 Open and widely accessible tools

Browser developer tools: inspect requests, status codes, structure, responsive layouts, and accessibility.
W3C Nu HTML Checker: identify HTML conformance errors and warnings.
WAVE: support human accessibility review by marking potential issues.
Lighthouse: generate performance and accessibility diagnostics.
WebPageTest: examine loading under varied devices and connections.
diagrams.net: draw request paths and responsibility maps.
Visual Studio Code or Positron: edit HTML with syntax support.



2.10.4 Chapter 2 bridge
Web pages and network requests produce and consume structured information. The next chapter moves below the interface to databases, where records, fields, identifiers, relationships, validation rules, and queries organize library data. Keep your URL analysis and request trace: both already contain structured components that can be modeled as data.




Association of Southeast Asian Nations. 2021. ASEAN Digital Masterplan 2025. https://asean.org/book/asean-digital-masterplan-2025/.


Billingham, Lisa. 2014. “Improving Academic Library Website Accessibility for People with Disabilities.” Library Management 35 (8/9): 565–81. https://doi.org/10.1108/LM-11-2013-0107.


Emde, Judith Z., Sara E. Morris, and Monica Claassen-Wilson. 2009. “Testing an Academic Library Website for Usability with Faculty and Graduate Students.” Evidence Based Library and Information Practice 4 (3): 24–36. https://doi.org/10.18438/B8TK7Q.


Fielding, Roy T., Mark Nottingham, and Julian Reschke. 2022. HTTP Semantics. RFC No. 9110. Internet Engineering Task Force. https://doi.org/10.17487/RFC9110.


International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions, and UNESCO. 2022. IFLA-UNESCO Public Library Manifesto 2022. IFLA. https://repository.ifla.org/handle/123456789/2006.


Mockapetris, Paul. 1987. Domain Names—Concepts and Facilities. RFC No. 1034. Internet Engineering Task Force. https://doi.org/10.17487/RFC1034.


National Privacy Commission. 2023. Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://privacy.gov.ph/data-privacy-act/.


Republic of the Philippines. 2012. Republic Act No. 10173: Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://lawphil.net/statutes/repacts/ra2012/ra_10173_2012.html.


Vargas, Isabel. 2026. “Elements, Structure, and Aesthetics: Improving Usability and Accessibility on a Library Website Homepage.” Weave: Journal of Library User Experience, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.3998/weaveux.7583.


WHATWG. 2026. HTML Living Standard. https://html.spec.whatwg.org/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2015. Mobile Accessibility: How WCAG 2.0 and Other W3C/WAI Guidelines Apply to Mobile. W3C. https://www.w3.org/TR/mobile-accessibility-mapping/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2023. Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2. https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/.
crumbs: 2  Networking, Web Basics, and Library Websites

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
text:
3.1 Chapter overview
A student returns a book, but the circulation system still shows it as overdue. A local-history spreadsheet lists the same author under four spellings. A report counts titles when the director asked for physical copies. A staff member deletes a borrower row and unexpectedly removes the only record of several transactions. Each incident looks different, but all point to one central problem: how can an information organization structure data so that facts remain consistent, connected, retrievable, and appropriately protected?
Libraries, archives, records centers, museums, and repositories depend on databases. An integrated library system stores bibliographic descriptions, item copies, patrons, acquisitions, vendors, loans, holds, fines, and staff actions. A repository stores submissions, files, creators, departments, review states, rights, and identifiers. An archive may maintain accessions, agents, descriptions, locations, restrictions, and preservation events. Even when staff see only a form or dashboard, structured data and rules operate underneath.
This chapter introduces relational databases without assuming prior programming. You will distinguish a database from a spreadsheet, identify entities and attributes, assign keys, model relationships, use constraints, reduce duplication through normalization, and read basic SQL. Metadata remains central: a database can store values efficiently, but LIS standards and policies determine what those values mean, how they are controlled, and whether they support discovery and accountability.
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
text:
3.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Distinguish flat files, spreadsheets, relational databases, and database management systems.
Identify entities, attributes, records, tables, primary keys, foreign keys, and relationships in an LIS scenario.
Construct a simple entity-relationship model for collections, creators, copies, users, or transactions.
Apply data types, required fields, unique values, and referential constraints to improve data quality.
Write and interpret basic SQL queries using SELECT, FROM, WHERE, ORDER BY, JOIN, and aggregate functions.
Evaluate the collection, access, retention, security, and reporting risks of patron and transaction data.
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
text:
3.3 Key concepts
Table 3.1 defines the chapter’s working vocabulary. A crucial distinction is between a database, which is organized data, and a DBMS, which is the software used to define, store, query, control, and recover that data.



Table 3.1: Key concepts for relational databases





Term
Working definition




Database
An organized collection of data designed for reliable storage, retrieval, and management.


DBMS
Software that defines, stores, queries, secures, and administers databases.


Relational database
A database representing data in related tables of rows and columns.


Table
A named structure containing rows about one kind of entity or event.


Row or record
One stored occurrence, such as one item copy or one loan.


Column or field
A defined property recorded for every applicable row.


Entity
A distinguishable thing or event about which data are maintained.


Attribute
A property of an entity, such as title, date, or status.


Schema
The formal design of tables, fields, keys, relationships, and rules.


Primary key
A field or combination of fields that uniquely identifies a row.


Foreign key
A field whose value refers to a key in another table.


Relationship
An association between entities or tables.


Cardinality
The permitted number of related occurrences, such as one-to-many.


Constraint
A rule limiting stored values or operations.


Referential integrity
Consistency of references between related rows.


Normalization
Organizing tables to reduce harmful duplication and dependency.


SQL
Structured Query Language, used to define, retrieve, and modify relational data.


Query
A request for selected data or an operation on data.


Index
An additional structure that can speed retrieval for selected fields.


Transaction
A group of operations treated as one logical unit of work.


Backup
A separate recoverable copy used after loss, corruption, or failure.


Data dictionary
Documentation of elements, definitions, types, values, rules, and stewardship.
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
text:
3.4 Core discussion

3.4.1 From lists to managed data
People often begin with a list. A small reading room may record title, author, shelf, borrower, and due date in one spreadsheet. This is understandable: spreadsheets are familiar, flexible, and effective for modest analysis. The problem appears when the same sheet tries to represent several different kinds of things.
Imagine one row for every loan. The title and author are repeated whenever the same copy is borrowed. The borrower’s name and contact details are repeated for every transaction. Correcting an author’s name requires finding every occurrence. Deleting the last loan may also delete the only stored copy of a borrower’s registration information. These are not simply typing errors; they are consequences of the structure.
A database management system adds capabilities that a basic file or spreadsheet does not reliably provide: defined schemas, simultaneous access, constraints, relationships, transactions, permissions, indexes, queries, and recovery mechanisms. Table 3.2 compares three common arrangements. The table does not declare one tool universally superior; it shows that fitness depends on purpose, scale, relationships, risk, and workflow.



Table 3.2: Comparing common structures for LIS data





Arrangement
Strength
Limitation
Appropriate example




Flat file or CSV
Portable, simple, transparent exchange format
No built-in relationships, types, permissions, or transaction control
Export of selected repository records


Spreadsheet
Flexible entry, formulas, sorting, and small analyses
Easy to mix meanings, formulas, display, and data; weak relationship enforcement
Temporary inventory or one-time metadata review


Relational database
Explicit relationships, constraints, queries, transactions, and controlled multi-table storage
Requires schema design, administration, documentation, and suitable tools
Circulation, acquisitions, accessions, or repository workflow






As Table 3.2 suggests, CSV is valuable for exchange and spreadsheets remain useful. The professional question is not “database or spreadsheet?” in the abstract. It is whether the chosen structure can enforce necessary meaning, preserve relationships, protect sensitive data, and support the intended work.


3.4.2 Relational thinking
The relational model represents data through relations that can be manipulated without making users follow physical storage paths. Codd’s foundational paper argued for this independence between data relationships and storage representation (Codd 1970). In practical introductory work, relations are usually presented as tables.
Each table should describe one kind of entity or event. Examples include:

Work: an intellectual creation;
Item: one physical or digital copy;
Creator: a person or organization associated with a work;
Patron: an authorized service user;
Loan: an event connecting an item and patron over time;
Accession: an event through which materials enter an archive; and
Preservation event: an action performed on a digital object.

This way of thinking connects directly to metadata. Before creating a field, ask what entity it describes. A publication date belongs to a publication or manifestation context, not necessarily to the physical copy. A barcode identifies an item copy, not the abstract title. A due date describes a loan event. Many reporting errors begin when these distinctions are blurred.


3.4.3 Tables, rows, and columns
A table has a name and defined columns. Each row represents one occurrence. Each column represents one defined attribute. Good column definitions are consistent: one field should not mean “publication year” for some rows and “acquisition year” for others.
Column names should be concise but accompanied by a data dictionary. A field named date is ambiguous. A field named date_acquired is better, but documentation should still specify format, timezone if relevant, source, whether an unknown value is allowed, and who is responsible for correction.
Data types communicate expected form and support validation. Common types include:

integer for whole numbers and surrogate identifiers;
text for names, titles, codes, and notes;
real or decimal forms for measured or monetary values where supported;
date or date-time representations;
Boolean values for true/false conditions; and
binary data for bytes, though many content systems store files outside the database and keep paths plus technical metadata inside it.

SQLite uses a flexible type system, so application rules and constraints remain important. A field’s declared type is not a substitute for testing actual data.


3.4.4 Keys and identity
A primary key uniquely identifies a row. Good primary keys are unique, stable, present, and not reused. A system-generated integer may serve as an internal key. A barcode, accession number, ISBN, ORCID, URI, or local identifier may carry professional meaning, but each identifies a different entity and follows different rules.
Identifiers should not be treated as interchangeable merely because all look like codes. An ISBN generally identifies a publication rather than one copy. A shelf mark supports location and arrangement but may change. A person’s name is not a stable identifier because names can be shared, changed, transliterated, or entered differently.
A foreign key stores a reference to a key in another table. If a loan row contains item_id 42, that value should point to an existing row in the Item table. Foreign-key enforcement helps prevent orphan records. SQLite documents both the syntax and required enabling conditions for foreign-key constraints (SQLite Project 2026b).


3.4.5 Relationships and cardinality
Figure 3.1 models a small reading-room service. One creator may be connected to many resources, one resource may have many items, one patron may have many loans, and one item may appear in many loans over time. Each loan refers to exactly one item and one patron.






erDiagram
    CREATOR ||--o{ RESOURCE : creates
    RESOURCE ||--o{ ITEM : has
    PATRON ||--o{ LOAN : makes
    ITEM ||--o{ LOAN : appears_in
    CREATOR {
      integer creator_id PK
      text authorized_name
    }
    RESOURCE {
      integer resource_id PK
      integer creator_id FK
      text title
      integer publication_year
    }
    ITEM {
      integer item_id PK
      integer resource_id FK
      text barcode
      text status
    }
    PATRON {
      integer patron_id PK
      text patron_code
      text category
    }
    LOAN {
      integer loan_id PK
      integer item_id FK
      integer patron_id FK
      text checkout_date
      text due_date
      text return_date
    }




Figure 3.1: Entity-relationship model for a small reading-room database





The notation in Figure 3.1 shows cardinality. One-to-many relationships are common. Many-to-many relationships require an intermediate table. If a resource can have many creators and a creator can be associated with many resources, a ResourceCreator table would hold pairs of identifiers and possibly a role such as author, editor, translator, or photographer.
An entity-relationship diagram is a model, not reality itself. The simple design in Figure 3.1 allows one main creator directly on Resource for beginner practice. A production catalogue would require richer bibliographic entities, roles, authorities, and many-to-many relationships.


3.4.6 Constraints and validation
A constraint is a rule enforced by the database. Useful constraints include:

PRIMARY KEY: each row has a unique identifier;
FOREIGN KEY: a reference must point to an allowable related row;
NOT NULL: a value is required;
UNIQUE: duplicate values are prohibited;
CHECK: a value must satisfy a condition; and
DEFAULT: a defined value is supplied when none is entered.

Constraints should reflect justified business and metadata rules. Marking a birth date NOT NULL because a form designer prefers complete rows may force staff to invent dates or exclude users. Conversely, leaving barcode unrestricted may permit duplicates that make circulation unsafe. Requirements should be negotiated with service owners and documented.
Validation occurs at several levels. Syntactic validation asks whether a value has the right form. Semantic validation asks whether it means the right thing. Workflow validation asks whether the value was supplied by an authorized process. A four-digit year can be syntactically valid while still describing the wrong event.
Controlled vocabularies can improve consistent values for fields such as item status, patron category, material type, language, and rights. Database constraints may enforce a short list, while authority tables support richer identifiers, labels, variants, provenance, and change over time.


3.4.7 Normalization and anomalies
Normalization organizes data to reduce harmful redundancy and dependency. At an introductory level, use three questions:

Does each cell contain one value appropriate to its field?
Does each row describe one identifiable thing or event?
Does every non-key field describe that row’s entity rather than some other entity?

Consider one table containing LoanID, ItemBarcode, Title, PatronName, PatronEmail, CheckoutDate, and DueDate. Title repeats for every loan of an item; patron data repeat for every loan by that person. Table 3.3 explains the resulting anomalies and the normalized response.



Table 3.3: Common data anomalies and structural responses





Anomaly
Problem
Structural response




Update
A changed email must be corrected in many loan rows
Store patron contact once and reference patron_id


Insertion
A resource cannot be recorded until it has a loan
Store Resource and Item independently of Loan


Deletion
Deleting the last loan can remove the only stored patron or title facts
Keep entity records separate and apply explicit retention rules






Table 3.3 shows that normalization is not merely tidiness. It reduces inconsistent correction, supports independent lifecycle events, and prevents accidental information loss. However, normalization does not decide retention. A separated patron table can still be retained too long; governance must define when records are archived, anonymized, or deleted.


3.4.8 SQL as a language for questions
Structured Query Language is used to define structures, retrieve rows, and modify data. SQLite provides an accessible implementation and documents supported statements and expressions (SQLite Project 2026a). SQL keywords are conventionally written in uppercase for readability, though they are generally case-insensitive.
A basic query selects named columns from a table:
SELECT title, publication_year
FROM Resource;
WHERE filters rows:
SELECT title, publication_year
FROM Resource
WHERE publication_year >= 2020
ORDER BY publication_year DESC, title;
Never use SELECT star merely because it is short when a report needs only selected fields. Naming columns documents purpose, reduces unnecessary data exposure, and makes changes easier to detect.
JOIN combines related tables:
SELECT Resource.title, Item.barcode, Item.status
FROM Resource
JOIN Item ON Resource.resource_id = Item.resource_id
ORDER BY Resource.title;
An aggregate summarizes rows:
SELECT status, COUNT(*) AS item_count
FROM Item
GROUP BY status
ORDER BY item_count DESC;
Table 3.4 connects common clauses to LIS questions.



Table 3.4: SQL clauses as parts of an information question





Clause
Purpose
LIS question




SELECT
Choose fields or expressions
Which columns belong in this report?


FROM
Name the source table
Which entity is the starting point?


JOIN ... ON
Connect related rows
How are copies connected to titles?


WHERE
Filter individual rows
Which loans remain unreturned?


GROUP BY
Form groups for summaries
How many items have each status?


HAVING
Filter summarized groups
Which subjects have more than 50 items?


ORDER BY
Sort results
Which due dates come first?


LIMIT
Restrict the number returned
What are the ten most recent accessions?






As Table 3.4 emphasizes, a good query begins with a precise information need. “Show usage” is vague. “Count completed in-library loans by material type for January, excluding test records and without patron identifiers” is testable and privacy-aware.


3.4.9 Transactions and concurrent work
A database transaction groups operations into one logical unit. Imagine checking out an item: the system creates a loan, changes the item’s availability, and records an audit event. If only one operation succeeds, the database becomes inconsistent.
Figure 3.2 shows the decision. COMMIT makes the complete set durable; ROLLBACK reverses the pending set after error. SQLite is transactional and documents all-or-nothing behavior across crashes within its stated guarantees (SQLite Project 2026c).






flowchart LR
    B["BEGIN transaction"] --> V["Validate patron and item"]
    V --> L["Create loan row"]
    L --> S["Change item status"]
    S --> A["Record audit event"]
    A --> Q{"All operations valid?"}
    Q -- Yes --> C["COMMIT"]
    Q -- No --> R["ROLLBACK"]
    C --> O["Consistent checkout"]
    R --> E["No partial checkout; report error"]




Figure 3.2: A simplified database transaction for checking out an item





Figure 3.2 simplifies a real circulation system, which may include locks, holds, loan rules, notifications, and external services. Its lesson is that multi-step changes need defined boundaries and recovery.


3.4.10 Indexes, performance, and reporting
An index is an additional structure that can speed searches and joins on selected fields. A book index helps readers find topics without scanning every page; a database index serves a related purpose. Indexes consume storage and must be updated when data change, so more is not always better.
Common candidates include identifiers, foreign keys, frequently filtered dates, and fields used for sorting. Performance should be measured with representative data and queries. A design that works with 50 rows may behave differently with millions of transactions.
Reports also require definitions. Does “collection size” mean bibliographic records, physical items, digital files, linear meters, or licensed titles? Does “active patron” mean registered, currently eligible, or recently transacting? The database cannot resolve an undefined metric. A data dictionary should connect report labels to entities, fields, filters, date rules, and exclusions.


3.4.11 Backup, export, and recovery
A database file is not a backup of itself. A useful backup is a separate recoverable copy, protected from the same failure, created on a schedule, retained appropriately, and tested through restoration. Copying an actively changing database without supported procedures can produce an inconsistent copy.
Backup planning should identify recovery point and recovery time needs. How much recent work could be re-entered? How long can circulation or reference services operate offline? What records must be retained for accountability, and which sensitive records should not persist indefinitely in backups?
Exports serve exchange and migration, but exporting rows does not automatically preserve schema, relationships, controlled vocabularies, permissions, documentation, or audit history. Vendor evaluation should therefore test both data export and meaningful reconstruction.

Reflective prompt. Find a spreadsheet you know well. Which columns describe different entities? What repeats, and what could be lost if one row were deleted?
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
text:
3.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

3.5.1 Barangay reading center: one copy is not one title
A barangay reading center records donated books in a spreadsheet. Three copies of the same title occupy three rows, but each row also repeats title, author, and publisher. Staff cannot tell whether a report should count titles or copies. A small relational design separates Resource from Item. One resource description can link to three barcoded copies, each with its own condition and location.


3.5.2 State university: authority work across campuses
A university system merges holdings from several campus libraries. Creator names include “Jose Rizal,” “Rizal, Jose P.,” and forms with different punctuation. A database can relate resource records to one creator identifier, but staff must still choose an authorized form, retain variants, document sources, and support Filipino naming contexts. Database structure makes authority control possible; cataloguing policy gives the structure meaning.


3.5.3 Provincial archive: accessions and locations
A provincial archive tracks accessions, donors, boxes, shelves, restrictions, and digitization status. One spreadsheet cell contains several box numbers separated by commas. Staff redesign the data so each container has its own identifier and location relationship. This allows movement history and inventory checks without overwriting the description of the archival unit.


3.5.4 Public library consortium: shared system, local policy
Several city libraries share a hosted circulation platform. They use common item-status codes so consortium reports can be combined, but borrowing periods differ by city and material type. The database schema supports shared entities and local policy tables. Governance identifies which fields are consortium-wide, which are locally controlled, and who approves changes.


3.5.5 Community heritage project: consent is not one checkbox
A community heritage project records narrators, interviews, consent conditions, access restrictions, and review dates. A single yes/no consent field is inadequate. The database separates the interview from consent events and access conditions, allowing permissions to be documented, reviewed, and changed without rewriting historical facts.
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
section: 3.6 Hands-on activity: Build a small reading-room database
text:
3.6 Hands-on activity: Build a small reading-room database

3.6.1 Purpose
Create and query a beginner relational database for resources, items, and loans while documenting data quality and privacy decisions.


3.6.2 Tools

DB Browser for SQLite, an open-source graphical tool (DB Browser for SQLite Project 2026)
A diagram tool or paper for the entity-relationship model
The provided fictional data, or ten invented resource records
A secure working folder containing no real patron data



3.6.3 Schema
Create four tables:

Resource: resource_id, title, creator_name, publication_year
Item: item_id, resource_id, barcode, status
Patron: patron_id, patron_code, category
Loan: loan_id, item_id, patron_id, checkout_date, due_date, return_date

For this introductory lab, creator_name remains in Resource. In your reflection, explain why a future version may require Creator and ResourceCreator tables.


3.6.4 Steps

Draw the four entities, their primary keys, and their relationships.
Create a new database named reading-room.sqlite.
Enable foreign-key support and create the tables with suitable data types.
Make every primary key an integer. Make barcode and patron_code unique.
Require Resource.title, Item.status, Loan.checkout_date, and Loan.due_date.
Add foreign keys from Item to Resource and from Loan to Item and Patron.
Enter at least five resources, eight items, four fictional patrons, and six loans. Leave return_date empty for two active loans.
Attempt to enter a duplicate barcode and record the result.
Attempt to create a loan for an item_id that does not exist and record the result.
Run the five required queries below.
Export one non-sensitive report to CSV.
Close the program, copy the database to a separate backup folder, reopen the copy, and run one query to test it.



3.6.5 Required queries

List resources alphabetically by title.
List each item with its title, barcode, and status using JOIN.
List active loans where return_date is NULL.
Count items by status using GROUP BY.
Count loans by patron category without showing patron codes.



3.6.6 Expected output
Submit:

an entity-relationship diagram;
reading-room.sqlite;
a one-page data dictionary;
the five SQL queries and result screenshots or exports;
a constraint-test log;
one CSV report containing no patron identifiers;
backup restoration evidence; and
a 200-word reflection.


Reflection question. Which rule belongs in the schema, which belongs in metadata or service policy, and which requires human judgment?



3.6.7 Activity checklist
Table 3.5 should be completed before submission. The checklist treats documentation, constraint testing, privacy, and recovery as part of database work rather than optional extras.



Table 3.5: Chapter 3 database lab submission checklist





Check
Complete




Diagram shows four entities, keys, and correct relationships
[ ]


Primary and foreign keys are implemented
[ ]


Barcode and patron code are unique
[ ]


Required fields reject missing values where appropriate
[ ]


Fictional sample data preserve referential integrity
[ ]


Five required queries run and answer the stated question
[ ]


CSV report excludes direct patron identifiers
[ ]


Data dictionary defines fields and controlled values
[ ]


Backup copy opens and returns a verified query result
[ ]








3.6.8 Mini-project rubric
Table 3.6 allocates 30 points. This lab can serve as the foundation for the database or API mini-project in the course assessment.



Table 3.6: Rubric for the Chapter 3 database lab





Criterion
Points
Full credit




Model and schema
8
Entities, keys, cardinalities, fields, and relationships fit the service problem


Constraints and sample data
6
Rules prevent tested duplicates and broken references; data are plausible and fictional


Queries and interpretation
7
All queries are correct, readable, and interpreted in relation to the question


Documentation and privacy
5
Dictionary is clear; report minimizes personal data and records assumptions


Backup test and reflection
4
Recovery is demonstrated and reflection distinguishes schema, policy, and judgment

section: 3.7 Ethical and professional issue: patron data and purpose limitation

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
text:
3.8 Review questions

Identification: Distinguish a database from a DBMS.
Identification: Define primary key, foreign key, and referential integrity.
Short answer: Why is a person’s name usually a poor primary key?
Short answer: What is cardinality? Give one LIS one-to-many example.
Short answer: Explain update, insertion, and deletion anomalies.
Applied analysis: A table stores title, barcode, borrower email, and due date in every row. Identify the entities and propose a normalized design.
Applied analysis: Why might a syntactically valid four-digit year still be semantically wrong?
Scenario: A report says the library has 20,000 books. What definitions and database evidence must be checked before using the number?
Scenario: A staff member exports all loan records to a personal device to create statistics. Identify the risks and propose a safer workflow.
Synthesis: Explain how database design, metadata policy, and professional ethics jointly affect a user’s library experience.
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
section: 3.9 Mini case: The regional mobile library database
text:
3.9 Mini case: The regional mobile library database
A provincial library operates two bookmobiles serving coastal and upland municipalities. Each vehicle keeps a separate spreadsheet because connectivity is intermittent. Patron names, schools, telephone numbers, titles, barcodes, checkout dates, and notes appear in one sheet. Barcode duplicates exist across vehicles. Staff sometimes replace an old borrower’s row with a new transaction to save space. At month end, files are emailed to headquarters, where one employee manually combines them. Management wants branch-level use statistics, while community partners ask the library not to expose children’s reading histories.
Guide questions

Identify the entities, keys, relationships, controlled values, and offline synchronization problems in the current workflow.
Propose a phased database design and data-governance plan suitable for intermittent connectivity.
Define three useful management reports that minimize personal data, including the fields, filters, grouping, and retention assumptions for each.
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 3  Databases for Library Data
text:
3.10 Further reading and tools

3.10.1 Readings and documentation

Codd, “A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks.” The foundational relational-model paper (Codd 1970).
SQLite SQL Language documentation. Primary syntax reference for supported statements and expressions (SQLite Project 2026a).
SQLite Foreign Key Support. Practical explanation of referential constraints (SQLite Project 2026b).
SQLite Is Transactional. A concise description of transaction guarantees (SQLite Project 2026c).
NISO, Understanding Metadata. A reminder that field structure must be paired with documented meaning (Understanding Metadata 2004).
Philippine Data Privacy Act and NPC guidance. Primary sources for personal-data obligations (Republic of the Philippines 2012; National Privacy Commission 2023).



3.10.2 Open and widely accessible tools

DB Browser for SQLite: graphical creation, entry, browsing, and SQL querying (DB Browser for SQLite Project 2026).
SQLite command-line shell: direct database creation, querying, import, and export.
diagrams.net: entity-relationship and workflow diagrams.
LibreOffice Base: graphical work with small relational databases.
OpenRefine: preparation and reconciliation of messy tabular values before controlled import.
R packages DBI and RSQLite: reproducible access to SQLite from R in later programming work.



3.10.3 Chapter 3 bridge
A database schema defines where values can be stored and how records connect. It does not decide the descriptive rules for titles, names, subjects, identifiers, rights, or provenance. Chapter 4 turns to metadata and cataloguing: the standards, vocabularies, authorities, and quality practices that make structured fields meaningful across systems and communities.




Codd, E. F. 1970. “A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks.” Communications of the ACM 13 (6): 377–87. https://doi.org/10.1145/362384.362685.


DB Browser for SQLite Project. 2026. DB Browser for SQLite. https://sqlitebrowser.org/.


International Federation of Library Associations and Institutions. 2012. IFLA Code of Ethics for Librarians and Other Information Workers. IFLA. https://www.ifla.org/g/faife/professional-codes-of-ethics-for-librarians/.


National Privacy Commission. 2023. Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://privacy.gov.ph/data-privacy-act/.


Republic of the Philippines. 2012. Republic Act No. 10173: Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://lawphil.net/statutes/repacts/ra2012/ra_10173_2012.html.


SQLite Project. 2026a. SQL Language Expressions and Statements. https://www.sqlite.org/lang.html.


SQLite Project. 2026b. SQLite Foreign Key Support. https://www.sqlite.org/foreignkeys.html.


SQLite Project. 2026c. SQLite Is Transactional. https://www.sqlite.org/transactional.html.


Understanding Metadata (2004). https://www.niso.org/publications/understanding-metadata-2017.
crumbs: 3  Databases for Library Data

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
text:
4.1 Chapter overview
A digitized photograph appears in a repository with the title “Old picture,” creator “Unknown,” date “1900s,” and no subject, place, rights, or provenance information. The image file opens correctly, but can a student discover it? Can a community member correct its description? Can staff distinguish the original photograph from the digital scan? Can another institution reuse the record? Can a future curator explain where the item came from and what may legally be done with it?
The central problem of this chapter is how to describe resources consistently enough for people and systems to identify, select, obtain, understand, manage, connect, and preserve them without erasing important context. Metadata makes collections legible. Cataloguing applies professional principles, standards, vocabularies, and judgment to create and maintain that metadata.
Metadata is not decoration added after digitization, nor is cataloguing simply typing information into fixed boxes. Every record is a structured claim about entities, names, subjects, relationships, provenance, rights, and access. Those claims affect discovery and memory. They may reproduce inherited bias, or they may make previously obscured people and relationships visible.
This chapter connects database structure from Chapter 3 to descriptive meaning. Students will distinguish metadata schemas from content standards, encoding formats, value vocabularies, and conceptual models; examine MARC 21, RDA, Dublin Core, IFLA LRM, and BIBFRAME; practice authority and subject work; and create a small, quality-checked metadata record for a local heritage object.
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
text:
4.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Classify metadata as descriptive, administrative, structural, technical, rights, provenance, or preservation metadata.
Distinguish conceptual models, element sets, content standards, value vocabularies, encoding formats, and application profiles.
Create a Dublin Core record using defined elements, controlled values, identifiers, language tags, and rights information.
Explain the purposes of authority control, identifiers, classification, and subject vocabularies.
Evaluate and revise metadata for completeness, accuracy, consistency, provenance, accessibility, and interoperability.
Analyze how descriptive choices affect representation, intellectual freedom, cultural rights, privacy, and discovery.
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
text:
4.3 Key concepts
Table 4.1 introduces the chapter vocabulary. Notice that a metadata record contains both elements and values, while rules and vocabularies determine how those values are formed.



Table 4.1: Key concepts for metadata and cataloguing





Term
Working definition




Metadata
Structured information used to describe, manage, discover, interpret, or preserve resources.


Metadata record
A set of metadata statements describing an entity or resource in a defined context.


Element
A named property such as title, creator, language, or rights.


Value
The content supplied for an element.


Schema or element set
A defined set of metadata elements and their meanings.


Conceptual model
An abstract account of entities, attributes, relationships, and user tasks.


Content standard
Rules for selecting and recording values.


Encoding format
A machine-processable syntax or structure for representing and exchanging records.


Value vocabulary
A managed set of terms, names, codes, or identifiers used as values.


Application profile
A documented selection and refinement of elements and rules for a community or project.


Identifier
A string or URI intended to distinguish an entity or resource.


Authority control
The work of managing identities, preferred forms, variants, and relationships consistently.


Authorized access point
A standardized form chosen to represent an entity in retrieval.


Variant access point
An alternative name or form that directs users to the represented entity.


Controlled vocabulary
A deliberately maintained set of terms and relationships.


Classification
Systematic arrangement of resources into classes, often expressed through notation.


Subject heading
A controlled term or string representing the topic, form, place, period, or group of a resource.


Provenance
Information about origin, custody, ownership, or creation history.


Crosswalk
A mapping between elements in different metadata schemas.


Interoperability
The ability of systems and communities to exchange and meaningfully use information.


Metadata quality
Fitness of metadata for stated functions and communities.


Granularity
The level of detail at which entities, components, events, or relationships are described.
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
text:
4.4 Core discussion

4.4.1 Metadata as service infrastructure
Metadata links users to resources. A catalogue result depends on recorded titles, names, subjects, identifiers, relationships, holdings, and access conditions. A repository browse depends on consistent departments, dates, resource types, and languages. A digital exhibit depends on captions, provenance, rights, and connections among objects. Preservation depends on file formats, checksums, events, agents, and dependencies.
The phrase “data about data” is useful but incomplete. Metadata may describe a physical object, a person, an organization, an event, a place, a right, or a relationship. In one context a city name is descriptive metadata; in a geographic authority file, the city is the primary entity. What matters is the statement being made: which property describes which entity, according to which rules, for which purpose.
NISO groups metadata broadly into descriptive, structural, and administrative functions (Understanding Metadata 2004). In practice, administrative metadata is often divided further. Table 4.2 shows how the categories support different work. One value can serve more than one function, so categories are analytical aids rather than sealed boxes.



Table 4.2: Metadata functions in information organizations





Type
Purpose
Example




Descriptive
Support identification, discovery, selection, and contextual understanding
Title, creator, subject, language, description


Structural
Express how components are ordered or related
Page sequence or relationship between a photograph and album


Technical
Record characteristics needed to render or manage files and systems
File format, dimensions, software, capture device


Rights
State ownership, licenses, permissions, restrictions, and access conditions
Copyright holder, license URI, access restriction


Provenance
Document origin, custody, sources, and responsibility for statements
Donor, source collection, cataloguer, record history


Preservation
Support authenticity, integrity, monitoring, and long-term action
Checksum, preservation event, migration agent


Use or service
Support operations and evaluation while respecting privacy
Circulation status or aggregated download count






As Table 4.2 shows, a digitized object needs more than a title. Its page order, file characteristics, rights, source, and preservation history may determine whether it remains usable and trustworthy.


4.4.2 A stack of models, rules, elements, values, and syntax
Students often say that MARC, RDA, and Dublin Core are competing “metadata standards.” They are related, but they do not do the same job. Figure 4.1 separates the layers.






flowchart TB
    N["Community needs and user tasks"] --> M["Conceptual model<br/>entities and relationships"]
    M --> E["Element set or schema<br/>properties that may be recorded"]
    E --> C["Content standard<br/>rules for forming values"]
    C --> V["Value vocabularies and identifiers<br/>controlled names, terms, and codes"]
    V --> S["Encoding and exchange syntax<br/>MARC, XML, RDF, JSON, CSV"]
    A["Application profile and local policy"] --- E
    A --- C
    A --- V
    Q["Quality review, provenance, and maintenance"] --- S




Figure 4.1: Layers that shape a metadata record





Figure 4.1 is not a rigid production sequence. A project may begin with an existing platform or exchange format. The figure is a diagnostic tool: when records fail, ask whether the problem lies in the conceptual model, available elements, content rules, values, encoding, local profile, or maintenance.
A conceptual model defines entities and relationships at an abstract level. An element set defines properties such as title or creator. A content standard gives instructions for recording values. A value vocabulary supplies controlled names, subjects, types, or codes. An encoding format makes statements machine-processable. An application profile combines selected elements, obligations, vocabularies, and local rules for a specific community.


4.4.3 User tasks and bibliographic entities
Cataloguing is shaped by what users need to do. The IFLA Library Reference Model identifies broad user tasks: find, identify, select, obtain, and explore (IFLA Library Reference Model Review Group 2017). These tasks require both attributes and relationships. A user may need to distinguish a novel from its translation, identify an edition suitable for citation, obtain an accessible format, or explore other works by a creator.
IFLA LRM models bibliographic information through entities including Work, Expression, Manifestation, Item, Agent, Nomen, Place, and Time-span. In simplified terms:

Work concerns a distinct intellectual or artistic creation.
Expression concerns the realization of a work, such as a language or performance.
Manifestation concerns a publication or production embodiment.
Item concerns an individual exemplar.

These distinctions prevent common errors. A barcode identifies an item, while an ISBN generally identifies a manifestation. A translator contributes to an expression. Damage notes apply to an item. A publication statement belongs to a manifestation context.
Conceptual entities do not always appear as separate screens or database tables. They guide consistent description and help systems connect related resources.


4.4.4 MARC 21, RDA, Dublin Core, and BIBFRAME
Table 4.3 compares four frameworks students will encounter. The rows are deliberately phrased by role because the frameworks may be used together rather than selected as exact substitutes.



Table 4.3: Roles of selected metadata and cataloguing frameworks





Framework
Primary role
Typical expression
Important caution




MARC 21
Structured communication format for bibliographic and authority data
Tagged fields, indicators, subfields, codes
Field syntax alone does not supply cataloguing judgment


RDA
Guidance and data elements for resource description and access
Statements and relationships recorded according to instructions
RDA is not identical to MARC or one display style


Dublin Core terms
General-purpose metadata terms for cross-domain resource description
Properties such as title, creator, subject, date, and rights
Simple elements can become ambiguous without profiles and vocabularies


BIBFRAME
Linked-data model and vocabulary for bibliographic description
RDF resources and relationships centered on Work, Instance, and Item
Linked identifiers do not repair weak or biased source description






MARC 21 provides formats for communicating bibliographic, authority, holdings, classification, and community information. Its bibliographic format defines tags, indicators, subfields, and coded elements (Library of Congress 2024b). A MARC field such as 245 structures title information; cataloguing instructions determine which title to record and how.
RDA is designed around resource description and access, with entities, elements, relationships, and instructions. The open RDA Registry publishes element and vocabulary representations for machine use (RDA Steering Committee 2026). RDA is not a file format. RDA data may be encoded in MARC or other structures.
Dublin Core provides broadly applicable terms. The DCMI terms vocabulary includes properties such as title, creator, subject, date, type, format, identifier, source, language, relation, coverage, and rights (Dublin Core Metadata Initiative 2020). Its apparent simplicity is valuable for cross-domain projects, but projects must define obligations and value rules.
BIBFRAME is a Library of Congress initiative for representing bibliographic description as linked data. Its model centers on Work, Instance, and Item and uses RDF relationships (Library of Congress 2024a). Chapter 8 returns to RDF and linked data.


4.4.5 Elements, values, and application profiles
An element is not a screen label. It is a property with a definition, expected value, and domain of use. “Date” may mean creation, publication, capture, accession, modification, copyright, or availability. A profile that uses one undifferentiated date field will create ambiguity even if every value follows YYYY-MM-DD.
An application profile should document:

element name and URI where available;
definition and examples;
obligation: required, recommended, or optional;
repeatability;
expected value type or syntax;
controlled vocabulary or authority source;
language and script practices;
source and provenance rules;
display and indexing behavior; and
validation and maintenance responsibility.

Profiles turn general standards into accountable local practice. They should not redefine established elements casually. If a local need cannot be represented, a documented local extension may be preferable to silently changing an existing element’s meaning.


4.4.6 Identifiers and access points
An identifier distinguishes an entity within a defined system. Examples include ISBNs, ISSNs, DOIs, ORCID iDs, accession numbers, barcodes, control numbers, handles, and URIs. Identifiers improve matching and exchange only when their scope is understood.
A string can look unique without being persistent. File names, shelf locations, and database row numbers may change. Projects should document the identifier’s assigning authority, entity, uniqueness, persistence expectation, display form, and resolution behavior.
An access point is a name, term, code, or other representation through which a user can retrieve a record. An authorized access point provides a consistent form; variant access points preserve other forms and lead users to the represented entity.


4.4.7 Authority control
Authority control manages identities, preferred forms, variants, sources, and relationships. It is not simply forcing every name into one spelling. A useful authority record may connect:

an authorized form;
variant names, earlier names, pseudonyms, transliterations, and local forms;
identifiers from trusted systems;
dates or contextual attributes needed for distinction;
relationships to people, families, organizations, places, and works;
sources consulted and evidence for decisions; and
notes about scope, uncertainty, or community preference.

Figure 4.2 illustrates why authority work is relational. Different labels can point to one identity, while one ambiguous label can refer to different identities.






flowchart LR
    V1["Variant name"] --> E["Identified person or organization"]
    V2["Name in another script"] --> E
    V3["Earlier or later name"] --> E
    I1["Local authority ID"] --> E
    I2["External identifier"] --> E
    E --> W["Associated works"]
    E --> O["Affiliated organization"]
    E --> P["Related place"]
    S["Sources and provenance"] --- E




Figure 4.2: Authority control connects variants, identifiers, and relationships to an entity





As Figure 4.2 shows, authority data support collocation and exploration. They also prepare repositories for linked data (Wolverton and Jin 2013). However, an authority file is not infallible. Identities can be uncertain, names can be contested, and communities may prefer forms different from legacy sources. Records need provenance and revision pathways.


4.4.8 Subject access and classification
Subject access attempts to represent what a resource is about, what form it takes, and sometimes its geographic, chronological, or demographic context. Tools include subject-heading lists, thesauri, taxonomies, classifications, genre/form vocabularies, and locally maintained terms.
A controlled vocabulary improves consistency by managing preferred terms, synonyms, broader and narrower concepts, and related concepts. A classification scheme places resources into a systematic arrangement, often supporting shelf order and browsing. Neither is neutral. Categories emerge from particular histories, institutions, languages, and assumptions.
Good subject analysis requires evidence from the resource and appropriate contextual sources. Cataloguers should distinguish what a resource is about from whom it is by, where it is stored, or who is expected to use it. Exhaustivity and specificity should match project policy. Overly broad terms hide distinctions; excessive unsupported specificity creates false precision.
Local and community vocabularies may represent concepts absent from larger systems. Mapping can support interoperability, but a local concept should not be erased merely because no exact external equivalent exists. The mapping may be close, broader, narrower, related, or intentionally absent.


4.4.9 A Dublin Core example
Table 4.4 presents a simplified record for a fictional digitized photograph. The record is compact, but values are still governed: the title is supplied and marked as such, the creator uses an authorized form, language follows a code, type uses a DCMI term, and the rights statement is represented by a URI.



Table 4.4: Example Dublin Core record for a digitized photograph





Element
Value
Rule or source




title
[Students at the municipal library inauguration]
Supplied title, visibly identified by brackets


creator
Santos, Elena M.
Local authority file


date
1968-06-12
ISO-style date from event annotation


type
Image
DCMI Type Vocabulary


format
image/tiff
Media type


identifier
https://example.org/items/photo-0042
Repository persistent URI


source
Municipal Archives, Photograph Collection, Box 3
Local collection citation


spatial
Iloilo City (Philippines)
Geographic authority form


language
zxx
Language code: no linguistic content


subject
Public libraries--Philippines; Library buildings
Controlled subjects


rights
https://rightsstatements.org/vocab/InC/1.0/
RightsStatements.org URI


description
Black-and-white group photograph showing students outside the newly opened library.
Objective scope note






The record in Table 4.4 does not claim certainty about everything. Brackets identify a supplied title, and the description avoids naming people without evidence. A real profile would define repeatability, language tags, refinements, contributor roles, digitization metadata, record provenance, and validation.


4.4.10 Metadata quality
Quality means fitness for stated purposes and communities, not maximal fullness. A record can be complete according to a template yet inaccurate, inaccessible, or harmful. Research on repository metadata repeatedly identifies consistency, completeness, accuracy, provenance, and interoperability as recurring quality concerns (Park 2009).
Table 4.5 provides operational questions. Quality dimensions can conflict: adding detail may improve discovery but create privacy risk; using one controlled vocabulary may improve consistency but weaken community representation.



Table 4.5: Metadata quality dimensions and review evidence





Dimension
Review question
Possible measure




Accuracy
Does the statement match reliable evidence and the entity described?
Sample verification against source


Completeness
Are required elements present for the intended service?
Required-field rate


Consistency
Are comparable resources described with comparable rules and values?
Variant and duplicate analysis


Conformance
Does the record follow the profile, syntax, and controlled vocabularies?
Validation error rate


Provenance
Can users tell who supplied or changed a statement and from what source?
Presence of source and change history


Timeliness
Are changing names, rights, locations, and links reviewed?
Overdue review count


Accessibility
Can people understand labels, language, context, and access conditions?
User testing and language review


Interoperability
Will another system preserve enough structure and meaning to use the record?
Test export and crosswalk loss report






As Table 4.5 indicates, a quality program needs evidence. Validation catches some structural errors but cannot determine whether a culturally sensitive description is appropriate. Automated checks and human review should complement each other.


4.4.11 Interoperability, crosswalks, and loss
Metadata interoperability includes technical, syntactic, semantic, and organizational dimensions. Two systems can exchange XML while disagreeing about what “creator” or “date” means. Shared syntax does not guarantee shared semantics.
A crosswalk maps elements between schemas. Crosswalks support migration and aggregation but are rarely lossless. A detailed MARC statement with roles, indicators, and controlled subfields may map to one broad Dublin Core creator value. When mapping many source elements into one target element, reconstructing the original distinctions may be impossible.
Before conversion, teams should:

document source and target definitions;
identify one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-one, and unmappable cases;
preserve original records or exports;
test representative difficult records;
record transformation rules and software versions;
measure information loss and validation errors; and
obtain review from metadata specialists and affected communities.

Interoperability should not mean flattening every local distinction. Aggregators can retain local values and provide mapped access values with provenance.


4.4.12 Workflow, provenance, and maintenance
Metadata is created through workflows: selection, examination, transcription, normalization, authority work, subject analysis, rights review, encoding, validation, publication, correction, and migration. Each handoff should have an accountable role.
Record provenance should identify sources, creator or agent, creation and modification dates, imported versus locally supplied statements, automated transformations, review status, and uncertainty. Batch-generated or AI-suggested metadata should be marked and sampled for error.
Maintenance is essential. URLs break, organizations change names, rights expire, vocabularies revise terms, and users identify errors. A correction mechanism should allow evidence-based revision without silently erasing record history.

Reflective prompt. Find a catalogue or repository record that helped or hindered you. Which element, value, relationship, or missing context produced that experience?
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
text:
4.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

4.5.1 University theses: names, departments, and advisers
A Philippine university repository receives theses from many colleges. Department names change over time, students enter advisers in free text, and Filipino names appear in inconsistent order. The repository creates authority identifiers for people and organizations, retains historical department names with dates, and records adviser roles separately from authorship. This improves browse lists and prevents organizational history from being overwritten.


4.5.2 Municipal photographs: supplied titles and local knowledge
A city library digitizes photographs with captions written decades after the events. Staff distinguish transcribed captions from supplied titles and community-contributed identifications. Each statement records source and date. Community corrections are reviewed rather than inserted anonymously, preserving both improved access and provenance.


4.5.3 Indigenous cultural heritage: negotiated access metadata
A cultural institution holds recordings connected to an Indigenous community. Generic “open” or “closed” fields cannot express community protocols. Representatives participate in terminology, sensitivity review, access categories, and correction procedures. Public discovery metadata may remain visible while files have culturally governed conditions. Local values are documented and mapped only where appropriate.


4.5.4 Government records: office names across reorganizations
A government information service describes reports issued by agencies that have merged or changed names. Authority records connect earlier and later corporate bodies without pretending they are identical. Issuing body, current custodian, and subject are recorded separately. Users can search the historical name while navigating to related bodies.


4.5.5 Southeast Asian multilingual collections
A regional portal aggregates records in Filipino, Bahasa Indonesia, Malay, Thai, Vietnamese, and English. It preserves original scripts, adds language tags, stores transliterations as variant access points, and avoids treating English as the authoritative original. Shared type and rights vocabularies support regional filtering while local descriptions retain their source and context.
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
section: 4.6 Hands-on activity: Describe and review a local heritage item
text:
4.6 Hands-on activity: Describe and review a local heritage item

4.6.1 Purpose
Create a beginner Dublin Core record, document the rules behind its values, and improve it through peer quality review.


4.6.2 Tools

One instructor-provided digitized photograph, poster, letter, oral-history excerpt, or pamphlet
A spreadsheet or CSV template with metadata fields
DCMI Metadata Terms (Dublin Core Metadata Initiative 2020)
RightsStatements.org statements (RightsStatements.org 2026)
Appropriate name, geographic, language, type, and subject vocabularies
A shared class data dictionary



4.6.3 Steps

Examine the object and distinguish visible evidence from supplied contextual information.
State the entity being described: original object, digital surrogate, or both through separate records.
Complete title, creator, contributor, date, type, format, identifier, source, language, subject, spatial coverage, description, and rights fields.
Mark a supplied title according to class policy.
Use at least one controlled name or subject value and record its source.
Use a stable identifier for the item and a URI for the rights statement where applicable.
Record unknown, uncertain, approximate, and not-applicable values according to the profile; do not invent information.
Add record provenance: metadata creator, date created, sources consulted, and review status.
Exchange the record with a peer. The reviewer checks the object, profile, vocabularies, and quality dimensions.
Revise the record and maintain a short change log explaining accepted and rejected suggestions.



4.6.4 Expected output
Submit:

one completed metadata record;
a data dictionary excerpt for five selected elements;
authority or vocabulary evidence for at least three values;
a peer-review checklist;
a change log;
a short rights and sensitivity note; and
a 200-word reflection.


Reflection question. Which metadata decision required transcription, which required a standard, which required community or contextual knowledge, and which remained uncertain?



4.6.5 Activity checklist
Table 4.6 should be completed before submission. It makes uncertainty and provenance visible rather than rewarding unsupported completeness.



Table 4.6: Chapter 4 metadata lab submission checklist





Check
Complete




Described entity and relationship to digital surrogate are clear
[ ]


Required elements follow the class application profile
[ ]


Supplied and transcribed information are distinguishable
[ ]


Names, subjects, types, languages, and places cite value sources
[ ]


Identifier and rights value have appropriate scope
[ ]


Unknown and uncertain facts are not invented
[ ]


Record provenance and sources are documented
[ ]


Peer review addresses at least five quality dimensions
[ ]


Change log explains revisions and retained decisions
[ ]








4.6.6 Metadata assignment rubric
Table 4.7 allocates 30 points and corresponds to the course metadata and cataloguing assessment.



Table 4.7: Rubric for the Chapter 4 metadata and cataloguing assignment





Criterion
Points
Full credit




Entity analysis and transcription
6
Record clearly distinguishes evidence, supplied context, resource, and surrogate


Elements and content rules
7
Values follow definitions, obligations, repeatability, syntax, and uncertainty rules


Authorities, vocabularies, and identifiers
6
Controlled values and identifiers are appropriate, sourced, and consistently applied


Rights, ethics, and provenance
5
Rights and sensitivity are addressed; record origin and decisions are traceable
