Quality review, revision, and reflection
6
Peer evidence leads to justified revisions and a specific professional reflection
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
section: 4.7 Ethical and professional issue: description, power, and accountable revision
text:
4.7 Ethical and professional issue: description, power, and accountable revision
Description is an exercise of power. Cataloguers decide which names become prominent, which relationships are recorded, which terms group resources, and which histories remain invisible. Legacy records may contain colonial language, racial or ethnic slurs, medicalized terminology, imposed names, gender assumptions, or descriptions centered on collectors rather than represented communities.
Removing harmful language immediately may improve access for current users, but silently replacing every historical statement can erase evidence about institutional practice. A responsible strategy may preserve the original statement in restricted administrative provenance while presenting a revised public value and an explanatory note. The appropriate choice depends on purpose, harm, law, community expectations, and archival integrity.
Community participation should be more than asking people to approve a nearly finished record. Communities may help define entities, preferred names, protocols, access categories, and correction processes. Participation requires time, compensation where appropriate, consent, and clarity about who has final authority.
Rights metadata also has ethical consequences. “Copyright unknown” does not mean “free to use,” while “copyrighted” does not automatically mean inaccessible. Standardized rights statements can communicate known conditions consistently (RightsStatements.org 2026), but staff must document the evidence and jurisdiction behind the choice.
AI systems can suggest captions, subjects, names, translations, or summaries. They can also hallucinate identities, repeat bias, flatten cultural distinctions, and expose restricted content to external services. AI-generated metadata should never be treated as authoritative merely because it is fluent. Projects need approved tools, data-protection review, provenance, confidence thresholds, representative sampling, human validation, and a path for correction.
Professional neutrality does not mean avoiding responsibility. It means applying transparent principles, evidence, community knowledge, intellectual freedom, privacy, and accountable revision rather than personal preference or vendor defaults.

Ethical checkpoint. A legacy photograph caption uses a derogatory term but is the only evidence of how the institution described the community in 1950. What should remain, what should display publicly, who should be consulted, and how should the revision be documented?
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
text:
4.8 Review questions

Identification: Define metadata element, value, schema, and application profile.
Identification: Distinguish descriptive, structural, technical, rights, provenance, and preservation metadata.
Short answer: Why are MARC 21 and RDA not the same kind of standard?
Short answer: Explain the difference among Work, Expression, Manifestation, and Item.
Short answer: What are the purposes of authorized and variant access points?
Applied analysis: A repository uses one field called date for creation, publication, upload, and copyright. Diagnose the semantic problem and propose a profile revision.
Applied analysis: A project maps detailed MARC records to simple Dublin Core. What information loss should be tested and documented?
Scenario: A community member disputes a subject heading applied to sacred material. Outline an evidence-based and participatory response.
Scenario: An AI tool identifies people in historical photographs. What technical, ethical, privacy, and provenance controls are required before any value is published?
Synthesis: Explain how database design, metadata standards, authority work, and interface display jointly affect discovery.
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
section: 4.9 Mini case: The island heritage collection
text:
4.9 Mini case: The island heritage collection
A university library receives 2,000 digitized photographs, field notebooks, and audio recordings documenting an island community from 1948 to 1985. The donor spreadsheet has columns called Name, Date, Place, Tribe, Notes, and Copyright. Some rows mix photographers and depicted persons. Dates range from exact days to guesses. Place names use colonial and current forms. The term in Tribe is rejected by community representatives. Several recordings contain ceremonies not intended for unrestricted circulation. The repository platform offers simple Dublin Core and an automatic image-captioning plugin. The grant requires public launch in four months.
Guide questions

Design an application profile identifying entities, required elements, repeatability, vocabularies, uncertainty, provenance, and access conditions.
Propose a workflow for community participation, rights review, automated suggestions, quality control, correction, and phased release.
Identify what should not be mapped into one public field, and explain how original donor data can be preserved without controlling public description.
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing
text:
4.10 Further reading and tools

4.10.1 Standards and models

NISO, Understanding Metadata. A concise introduction to metadata functions and components (Understanding Metadata 2004).
DCMI Metadata Terms. Definitions and usage for Dublin Core properties, classes, and vocabularies (Dublin Core Metadata Initiative 2020).
MARC 21 Format for Bibliographic Data. Tags, indicators, subfields, and coded structures (Library of Congress 2024b).
IFLA Library Reference Model. Entities, relationships, and bibliographic user tasks (IFLA Library Reference Model Review Group 2017).
RDA Registry. Open machine-readable RDA elements and vocabularies (RDA Steering Committee 2026).
BIBFRAME 2.0 Model. Library of Congress linked-data model for bibliographic description (Library of Congress 2024a).
RightsStatements.org. Standardized rights statements for cultural heritage institutions (RightsStatements.org 2026).



4.10.2 Open and widely accessible tools

OpenRefine: clean, cluster, reconcile, and transform metadata with an operation history.
MarcEdit: validate, transform, and edit MARC and related metadata formats.
Zotero: inspect bibliographic metadata and identifiers from common sources.
Library of Congress Linked Data Service: browse and retrieve authority and vocabulary data.
Getty Vocabularies: reference resources for art, architecture, people, and places.
Wikidata: a multilingual linked-data source that requires normal source evaluation.
CSVLint and XML validators: check structural conformance of exchange files.



4.10.3 Chapter 4 bridge
Metadata becomes operational inside content systems. Chapter 5 examines digital libraries, repositories, digital archives, exhibits, and content platforms: how objects and records move through selection, ingest, review, storage, publication, rights management, access, and preservation workflows.




Dublin Core Metadata Initiative. 2020. DCMI Metadata Terms. https://www.dublincore.org/specifications/dublin-core/dcmi-terms/.


IFLA Library Reference Model Review Group. 2017. IFLA Library Reference Model: A Conceptual Model for Bibliographic Information. International Federation of Library Associations; Institutions. https://repository.ifla.org/handle/123456789/40.


Library of Congress. 2024a. BIBFRAME 2.0 Model. https://www.loc.gov/bibframe/docs/bibframe2-model.html.


Library of Congress. 2024b. MARC 21 Format for Bibliographic Data. https://www.loc.gov/marc/bibliographic/.


Park, Jung-Ran. 2009. “Metadata Quality in Digital Repositories: A Survey of the Current State of the Art.” Cataloging and Classification Quarterly 47 (3–4): 213–28. https://doi.org/10.1080/01639370902737240.


RDA Steering Committee. 2026. RDA Registry. https://www.rdaregistry.info/.


RightsStatements.org. 2026. Rights Statements for Cultural Heritage Institutions. https://rightsstatements.org/en/.


Understanding Metadata (2004). https://www.niso.org/publications/understanding-metadata-2017.


Wolverton, Robert E., and Qiang Jin. 2013. “Authority Control in a Digital Repository: Preparing for Linked Data.” Journal of Library Metadata 13 (2–3): 95–113. https://doi.org/10.1080/19386389.2013.826061.
crumbs: 4  Metadata and Cataloguing

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
5.1 Chapter overview
A university purchases a repository platform and announces that it now has a digital library. Faculty upload PDFs, the archives scans photographs, and students build online exhibits. A year later, many records lack rights information, departments use inconsistent names, preservation copies are mixed with thumbnails, some files cannot be downloaded, and no one knows how to export the collection if the vendor contract ends.
The central problem of this chapter is how to turn selected digital content into a governed, discoverable, usable, and sustainable information service. A platform can store files and display pages, but it cannot decide what belongs in the collection, what an object represents, which metadata rules apply, whether consent permits online access, which copy should be preserved, or who will maintain the service after project funding ends.
Digital libraries, institutional repositories, digital archives, exhibits, collection management systems, and commercial content platforms overlap, but they are not synonyms. Their purposes, content models, workflows, user communities, rights responsibilities, and preservation commitments differ. This chapter compares those systems, follows a digital object from selection through ingest and access, and gives students a beginner framework for platform evaluation.
Chapter 4 established that metadata carries description, identity, rights, provenance, and relationships. Chapter 5 places that metadata inside operational workflows. The focus remains on service problems rather than software menus.
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
5.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Differentiate digital libraries, institutional repositories, digital archives, digital exhibits, collection management systems, and commercial content platforms.
Diagram a basic workflow from selection and rights review through ingest, quality assurance, publication, maintenance, and preservation.
Distinguish master files, preservation copies, access derivatives, thumbnails, metadata records, and submission packages.
Compare content platforms using metadata, interoperability, accessibility, security, preservation, governance, cost, and exit criteria.
Create a small prototype collection with coherent metadata, identifiers, rights statements, and file relationships.
Evaluate ethical and professional risks involving copyright, consent, privacy, cultural sensitivity, exclusion, takedown, and sustainability.
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
5.3 Key concepts
Table 5.1 supplies the working vocabulary. Several terms describe functions rather than specific products. A repository may support exhibits, and a digital library may use repository software, but their service commitments still need to be stated.



Table 5.1: Key concepts for digital collections and content platforms





Term
Working definition




Digital library
An organized digital collection with services for discovery, access, use, and stewardship.


Institutional repository
A service that collects, manages, preserves, and disseminates an institution's intellectual output.


Digital archive
A system and professional context for maintaining digital records with provenance, order, authenticity, and access controls.


Digital exhibit
A curated interpretive presentation using selected digital objects and narrative.


Collection management system
A system for documenting and managing collections, objects, locations, rights, and activities.


Content platform
A technical and organizational base on which content services are built.


Ingest
The controlled process of receiving, validating, describing, and accepting content into a system.


Submission package
Files, metadata, documentation, and agreements transferred for ingest.


Digital object
Content plus the metadata and relationships needed to identify and manage it.


Master file
The best available high-quality source created or selected for future reuse.


Preservation copy
A managed copy intended to support long-term continuity.


Access derivative
A version optimized for delivery, such as a compressed image or PDF.


Thumbnail
A small preview image used in lists and navigation.


OCR
Optical character recognition that converts image-based text into machine-readable text.


Persistent identifier
A managed identifier intended to remain stable when technical locations change.


Embargo
A defined delay before content becomes openly accessible.


Takedown
Removal or restriction of content in response to a justified request or policy.


Harvesting
Automated collection of metadata from another service.


Interoperability
The ability to exchange and meaningfully use content and metadata.


Platform governance
Policies, roles, decisions, resources, and oversight sustaining a platform.


Vendor lock-in
Dependence that makes moving data, functions, or workflows unusually difficult or costly.


Exit plan
A tested approach for exporting, reconstructing, migrating, or retiring a service.
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
5.4 Core discussion

5.4.1 Different systems, different promises
The phrase digital library emphasizes organized collections and services: selection, description, discovery, access, use, and stewardship. It is broader than a website containing files. Users should be able to understand what the collection contains, find relevant objects, interpret them, and know how they may be used.
An institutional repository is typically tied to an organization’s intellectual output: theses, articles, reports, conference materials, datasets, learning objects, and other works. Lynch describes institutional repositories as organizationally defined services and infrastructure for managing and disseminating digital materials (Lynch 2003). The emphasis is not only software but institutional commitment.
A digital archive maintains digital records with attention to provenance, original order, authenticity, restrictions, and long-term context. A public interface may show item-level descriptions, but archival control can also operate at collection, series, file, or aggregate levels.
A digital exhibit selects objects to support an interpretive argument or story. It is curated and intentionally partial. Exhibit captions should link to fuller object records rather than replacing them.
A collection management system supports internal documentation and workflows such as accessioning, location control, conservation, loans, valuation, rights, and exhibition history. Its public portal may expose selected fields, but internal records can contain confidential or operational data.
A commercial content platform provides licensed discovery and delivery. The library may configure authentication, links, branding, and holdings, but it usually does not control source metadata, preservation, interface code, or long-term availability.
Table 5.2 compares these systems by primary purpose. The comparison prevents a common procurement error: choosing software before agreeing on the service commitment.



Table 5.2: Purposes and risks of selected digital content systems





System
Primary purpose
Typical unit
Central risk




Digital library
Provide organized digital collections and services
Item, object, collection, or compound object
Weak context or unsustainable project growth


Institutional repository
Steward and disseminate institutional intellectual output
Submission, work, file, person, department, or collection
Low deposit, unclear rights, or poor preservation


Digital archive
Maintain records with provenance, context, authenticity, and restrictions
Fonds, collection, series, file, item, or record
Loss of context or inappropriate disclosure


Digital exhibit
Interpret selected objects through narrative and design
Story section, exhibit page, media object
Narrative without durable object stewardship


Collection management system
Manage collection objects, locations, rights, and activities
Object, accession, agent, location, event
Confusing internal control with public access


Licensed platform
Deliver vendor-controlled licensed content
Article, chapter, book, issue, media item
Vendor dependence and unstable access








5.4.2 A digital object is more than one file
A digitized newspaper issue may include master images, cropped access images, OCR text, a searchable PDF, page-order metadata, descriptive metadata, checksums, rights statements, and links to the source volume. A born-digital thesis may include the submitted PDF, supplemental files, deposit agreement, embargo date, author identifier, department authority, and preservation events.
Figure 5.1 shows a layered object. Users may encounter only the access derivative, but stewardship depends on the other layers.






flowchart TB
    O["Digital object"] --> F["Content files"]
    O --> M["Metadata"]
    O --> R["Relationships"]
    O --> G["Governance"]
    F --> F1["Master or submitted file"]
    F --> F2["Preservation copy"]
    F --> F3["Access derivative and thumbnail"]
    M --> M1["Descriptive and structural"]
    M --> M2["Technical, rights, and preservation"]
    R --> R1["Source object, parts, versions, agents, collections"]
    G --> G1["Consent, license, restriction, retention, takedown"]




Figure 5.1: A digital object combines content, metadata, relationships, and governance





As Figure 5.1 indicates, a file without metadata may be unreadable in context, while metadata without a managed file cannot deliver the object. Relationships are especially important for compound objects, versions, translations, supplements, and collections.


5.4.3 Selection and collection policy
Digital projects often begin with opportunity: a grant, donated scanner, available platform, or anniversary. Sustainable services begin with a collection policy. The policy should define:

mission and intended communities;
subject, geographic, chronological, institutional, and format scope;
selection and exclusion criteria;
relationship to existing collections and mandates;
rights, consent, privacy, and cultural-sensitivity requirements;
descriptive depth and languages;
file formats and quality targets;
preservation commitment;
access levels and restrictions;
responsibilities for correction and takedown; and
resource limits and review schedule.

Selection is not neutral. Digitizing prominent collections first may reinforce an institution’s existing historical emphasis. Selection should consider significance, demand, condition, rights, representational gaps, community priorities, feasibility, and preservation risk.


5.4.4 Digitization and born-digital content
Digitization converts analog information into digital form. It does not automatically create a digital library or preserve the original. A scanning workflow should record the source object, equipment, settings, operator, date, processing, quality checks, file names, and relationships among derivatives.
A master file should retain sufficient quality for future use. An access derivative is optimized for current delivery. Thumbnails support browsing but should not become the only surviving copy. Format and quality choices depend on content, use, storage, standards, and institutional capacity.
OCR makes text in images searchable and reusable. It introduces errors, especially with degraded paper, complex layouts, historical type, diacritics, and Southeast Asian scripts. OCR should be stored as a derivative with software and quality provenance. It should not silently replace an authoritative transcription.
Born-digital content brings different risks: proprietary formats, external links, embedded media, passwords, macros, personal data, version ambiguity, and malware. Ingest should include inventory, validation, security scanning, rights review, and documentation of original context.


5.4.5 Ingest as a controlled workflow
Ingest is the controlled transfer of content into a managed system. It is not the same as dragging files into a folder. Figure 5.2 follows a basic lifecycle.






flowchart LR
    S["Select and appraise"] --> A["Secure rights, consent, and agreement"]
    A --> P["Prepare files and metadata"]
    P --> V["Validate, scan, and quality-check"]
    V --> I["Ingest and assign identifier"]
    I --> Q["Metadata and access review"]
    Q --> U["Publish or restrict"]
    U --> M["Monitor use, integrity, links, and rights"]
    M --> C["Correct, migrate, preserve, or withdraw"]
    C --> M




Figure 5.2: A basic lifecycle from selection to continuing stewardship





The feedback loop in Figure 5.2 matters. Publication is not the end. Files can corrupt, links break, rights change, communities request corrections, and platforms require upgrades. Every stage needs acceptance criteria and an accountable role.
A submission package may include:

content inventory;
source and derivative files;
descriptive, structural, technical, and rights metadata;
deposit, transfer, or consent agreement;
checksums;
virus-scan result;
restrictions and embargo dates;
identifiers and relationships; and
processing or exception notes.

Not every small project needs an elaborate package, but every project needs enough evidence to explain what was received, what was changed, what was accepted, and under what authority.


5.4.6 Content models and metadata
A content model defines how a platform represents communities, collections, items, files, people, versions, and relationships. DSpace, for example, supports repository structures and workflows documented across its communities, collections, items, bundles, and bitstreams (DSpace Project 2026). Omeka S centers resources, items, item sets, media, vocabularies, and sites for digital collections and exhibits (Omeka Team 2026).
These examples are not universal templates. Before configuring a platform, teams should map professional entities to platform entities. If one oral-history interview has audio, transcript, consent form, and photographs, will they be media attached to one item, separate related items, or a compound object? The answer affects metadata, rights, discovery, export, and preservation.
Metadata should follow an application profile, not whatever fields a default form happens to show. Required fields should reflect service needs. Controlled values should use authority sources. Administrative and restricted metadata should not be exposed accidentally through public APIs or exports.


5.4.7 Identifiers and stable access
A stable identifier separates identity from current location. Persistent identifier systems such as DOI and Handle depend on organizational maintenance, not magical strings. The DOI system, for example, combines identifiers with resolution and metadata obligations (International DOI Foundation 2026).
A repository should define:

what entity receives an identifier;
who assigns and maintains it;
whether it resolves publicly;
what happens after withdrawal;
how versions are distinguished; and
how identifiers appear in citations and exports.

Changing a platform should not force every citation to break. Redirects, identifier resolution, domain control, and export are part of continuity planning.


5.4.8 Discovery, harvesting, and interoperability
Repositories rarely serve users only through their own search box. Records may be discovered through search engines, union catalogues, subject portals, aggregators, ORCID profiles, citation links, and APIs.
OAI-PMH provides a protocol through which service providers can harvest metadata records from repositories using defined requests, identifiers, datestamps, sets, and metadata formats (Open Archives Initiative 2008). Harvesting increases reach but exposes metadata quality problems. If names, dates, rights, and types are inconsistent locally, aggregation magnifies inconsistency.
IIIF provides interoperable APIs for presenting and working with images and audiovisual resources. Its Presentation API describes the structure and layout of compound digital objects through manifests and related resources (IIIF Consortium 2024). IIIF can support deep zoom, page sequence, annotation, and reuse across viewers while leaving content under institutional control.
Table 5.3 distinguishes several exchange mechanisms. None substitutes for meaningful metadata or rights governance.



Table 5.3: Interoperability services and governance requirements





Mechanism
Main function
Required governance




Search-engine indexing
Expose public pages to general web discovery
Robots, sitemaps, privacy, public metadata quality


OAI-PMH
Harvest metadata records incrementally
Sets, datestamps, metadata formats, deleted-record policy


REST API
Request or manipulate structured data through endpoints
Authentication, limits, versions, documentation, privacy


IIIF
Present and annotate image or audiovisual objects interoperably
Rights, manifests, media delivery, annotation policy


Structured export
Move records and relationships for audit or migration
Schema, vocabularies, provenance, completeness, testing


Persistent identifier
Maintain a managed reference independent of current location
Assignment scope, resolution, update, withdrawal, funding








5.4.9 Access copies, restrictions, and embargoes
Open access is not identical to unrestricted access. Content may be limited by copyright, donor agreement, privacy, cultural protocol, security, research ethics, or temporary embargo. A platform should represent access decisions explicitly rather than using hidden files and informal notes.
Access levels might include open, registered-user, institutional, reading-room, mediated request, embargoed-until-date, and closed. Each level needs an authority, rationale, review date, and user-facing explanation. Metadata may remain public while files are restricted, but descriptive metadata can itself disclose sensitive information.
An embargo should expire through monitored workflow. A permanent “temporary” restriction is a governance failure. Takedown procedures should provide a contact, criteria, documentation, interim actions, review, decision, and appeal or correction route.


5.4.10 Rights and licenses
Owning a physical object does not automatically transfer copyright. Permission to digitize does not always include permission to publish globally, allow download, or authorize reuse. Deposit agreements should state rights granted, representations by depositors, embargoes, preservation permissions, permitted transformations, and takedown conditions.
Standard rights statements help users understand known conditions (RightsStatements.org 2026). A rights field should identify the statement, rights holder where known, source of determination, jurisdiction or date assumptions, and contact route. “For educational use only” is often too vague to guide lawful reuse.


5.4.11 Platform selection
Platform selection should begin with requirements and capacity. Table 5.4 presents criteria that apply to open-source, proprietary, locally hosted, and managed services. Open source can support inspectability and local control but still requires hosting and expertise. Proprietary hosting can reduce some operational burden but may increase dependency.



Table 5.4: Platform evaluation criteria and evidence





Criterion
Questions
Evidence




Service fit
Does it support the users, collections, tasks, and scale in scope?
Task demonstration with representative users and content


Content model and metadata
Can it represent entities, files, relationships, profiles, authorities, and provenance?
Configured sample records and exported profile


Discovery and interoperability
Does it support stable URLs, search indexing, APIs, harvesting, and documented exports?
Test harvest, API request, and URL plan


Accessibility and performance
Can users operate it with assistive technology, mobile devices, and constrained connections?
Accessibility and low-bandwidth test


Rights and workflow
Can roles, review, embargo, restriction, correction, and takedown be managed?
Workflow demonstration and policy mapping


Security and privacy
How are accounts, permissions, patches, logs, vendors, and sensitive data governed?
Security documentation and data-flow review


Preservation and backup
Are integrity checks, copies, recovery, and preservation actions supported and tested?
Backup and recovery test


Administration and support
What skills, documentation, community, vendor response, and upgrade work are required?
Upgrade plan, service levels, and staff skills inventory


Cost and sustainability
What are implementation, migration, hosting, staffing, storage, and long-term costs?
Five-year total-cost scenario


Export and exit
Can complete data, files, identifiers, relationships, rights, and history be reconstructed elsewhere?
Trial export and reconstruction exercise






The crucial final row in Table 5.4 is often neglected. A screenshot of exported CSV is not enough. The team should verify that files, metadata, identifiers, relationships, rights, versions, and audit history can be reconstructed meaningfully.


5.4.12 Governance, sustainability, and community practice
A platform needs a governance group with authority over scope, priorities, metadata, rights, access, security, preservation, integrations, upgrades, and retirement. Roles may include collection owner, repository manager, metadata librarian, archivist, IT administrator, privacy or legal adviser, accessibility lead, and community representative.
The COAR Community Framework recommends good practices across discovery, access, reuse, integrity, quality assurance, preservation, sustainability, and governance (Confederation of Open Access Repositories 2023). The framework is useful because it treats repositories as community infrastructure rather than storage products.
Sustainability requires recurring resources: staff time, hosting, storage, backups, training, documentation, accessibility remediation, upgrades, identifiers, and user support. Grant funding can start a service but cannot substitute for an institutional owner.
Usage statistics should inform improvement without becoming the sole measure of value. Rare local-history resources may have modest traffic but high cultural importance. Metrics should document definitions, bot filtering, privacy controls, and limitations.

Reflective prompt. Choose a digital collection you have used. What promise does it appear to make about access, context, persistence, and correction? What evidence supports that judgment?
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
5.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

5.5.1 State university repository: theses and embargoes
A Philippine state university requires electronic theses. The repository receives a PDF, adviser approval, abstract, department, keywords, ORCID where available, and an access decision. Some theses have patent, publication, or participant-confidentiality concerns. The workflow records an embargo end date and responsible office. Staff test expiry rather than relying on memory, and public metadata explains when the file will open.


5.5.2 Provincial library: digitized newspapers
A provincial library scans fragile newspapers. Each issue becomes a compound object with page order, master TIFF files, smaller JPEG images, OCR text, a searchable PDF, title and date metadata, source-volume identification, and rights notes. OCR quality is sampled because historical printing and damaged pages create errors. The public interface offers page images and text search, while preservation copies remain outside routine delivery.


5.5.3 Museum exhibit: object records behind the story
A museum creates an online exhibit about regional weaving. Exhibit pages combine narrative, interviews, photographs, and object details. Every displayed object links to a durable collection record with maker, materials, dimensions, provenance, community terminology, rights, and correction contact. When the exhibit ends, the object records remain managed rather than becoming orphaned media.


5.5.4 Community archive: tiered cultural access
A community archive holds public festival photographs, family histories, and recordings with culturally sensitive knowledge. It avoids a simple open/closed switch. Community-approved categories specify who may view files, which metadata may display, and when decisions must be reviewed. Platform configuration follows the protocol rather than defining it.


5.5.5 Regional aggregation: local records, shared discovery
Several Southeast Asian repositories expose selected Dublin Core records for harvesting. The aggregator normalizes type and language codes for filtering but preserves local titles, original scripts, identifiers, and source links. Mapping provenance allows users to distinguish the aggregator’s transformations from the contributing institution’s description.
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
section: 5.6 Hands-on activity: Build a three-item prototype collection
text:
5.6 Hands-on activity: Build a three-item prototype collection

5.6.1 Purpose
Design and document a small digital collection from selection through public access, including files, metadata, rights, quality review, and export.


5.6.2 Tools
Choose one:

Omeka S using an instructor-provided installation and its user documentation (Omeka Team 2026);
another approved repository sandbox; or
a local simulation using folders, CSV metadata, HTML item pages, and a manifest.

Also use a text editor, spreadsheet, checksum tool, and image editor that preserves the master file.


5.6.3 Source package
Use three fictional or instructor-cleared items from one coherent collection. Do not upload personal or copyrighted material without authorization. Suitable items include public-domain posters, institution-created photographs with permission, or fully fictional surrogates.


5.6.4 Steps

Write a 150-word collection statement defining purpose, audience, scope, selection, exclusions, and stewardship commitment.
Inventory the three source objects and state what entity each record describes.
Create a master or submitted file and at least one access derivative where appropriate.
Record file names, formats, sizes, checksums, source, and derivative relationships.
Create item records using the Chapter 4 application profile. Include title, creator, date, type, format, identifier, source, language, subject, description, rights, and record provenance.
Create one collection-level record and connect all items to it.
Assign stable local identifiers and design public URLs that do not depend on file names.
Add a rights statement, access condition, and takedown contact for every item.
Configure or simulate browse, search, and one collection landing page.
Test one mobile task, one keyboard-only task, and one known-item search with a peer.
Export metadata and files. Verify whether relationships and identifiers can be reconstructed.
Submit a change log and one prioritized improvement.



5.6.5 Expected output
Submit:

collection statement and selection rationale;
three item records and one collection record;
file inventory with checksums and derivative relationships;
rights, access, and takedown notes;
screenshots or local HTML showing collection and item views;
usability and accessibility test notes;
metadata and file export;
reconstruction or exit note; and
a 200-word reflection.


Reflection question. Which part of the prototype depended on software, which depended on metadata policy, and which depended on institutional or community authority?



5.6.6 Activity checklist
Table 5.5 should be completed before submission. It emphasizes that a polished exhibit view is not sufficient without files, metadata, rights, testing, and an exit path.



Table 5.5: Chapter 5 prototype collection submission checklist





Check
Complete




Collection purpose, users, scope, selection, and exclusions are stated
[ ]


Three item entities and their source objects are distinguished
[ ]


Master or submitted files and access derivatives are inventoried
[ ]


Checksums, formats, sizes, and file relationships are recorded
[ ]


Metadata follows the application profile and controlled values
[ ]


Identifiers, collection relationships, and public URLs are coherent
[ ]


Rights, access condition, and takedown contact appear for every item
[ ]


Mobile, keyboard, and discovery tests are documented
[ ]


Export supports a documented reconstruction or exit assessment
[ ]








5.6.7 Prototype rubric
Table 5.6 allocates 30 points. Technical polish receives credit only when it supports a coherent service and stewardship plan.



Table 5.6: Rubric for the Chapter 5 prototype digital collection





Criterion
Points
Full credit




Collection rationale and users
5
Scope and selection answer a specific service need and acknowledge exclusions


Files and object relationships
6
Sources, masters, derivatives, checksums, and relationships are accurate and traceable


Metadata and discovery
7
Records follow the profile and support identification, browse, search, and context


Rights, access, and ethics
6
Authority, conditions, sensitivity, and takedown are explicit and appropriate


Testing, export, sustainability, and reflection
6
Evidence supports usability, accessibility, reconstruction, improvement, and reflection
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
section: 5.7 Ethical and professional issue: access is not the same as permission
text:
5.7 Ethical and professional issue: access is not the same as permission
Digitization can increase access while increasing exposure. A photograph may be legally publishable but humiliating to depicted people. An oral-history narrator may have consented to research-room use, not global streaming. A sacred object may be widely photographed but governed by community protocols. A student thesis may include personal information that passed an academic defense but should not be indexed openly.
Ethical review should ask:

Who created, owns, appears in, speaks in, or is affected by the content?
What permission was actually granted, for which uses and audiences?
Does public metadata reveal sensitive facts even when the file is closed?
Can people request correction, restriction, contextualization, or takedown?
Who has authority to decide, and how are decisions recorded and reviewed?
Does the platform force a simpler access rule than the community requires?

Copyright review and ethical review overlap but are not identical. A public-domain object can still present privacy or cultural concerns. A copyrighted object may be lawfully available under permission, license, or an applicable exception. Rights metadata should communicate evidence and uncertainty rather than making unsupported claims.
Consent should be specific enough to guide workflows. A checkbox saying “I agree” may not explain search-engine indexing, download, reuse, automated transcription, AI processing, or cross-border hosting. Meaningful consent also requires a feasible way to revisit decisions where the agreement allows.
Takedown is not an admission that the project failed. It is part of accountable access. Institutions need a visible contact, interim response, documented review, preservation of evidence, and communication with affected parties. Removed public content may still require a tombstone record so citations and identifiers do not silently disappear.
Platform design can also exclude. Image-heavy exhibits may consume mobile data; complex viewers may fail with assistive technology; mandatory accounts may block casual inquiry; English-only interfaces may hide local-language collections. Access must be evaluated through actual community conditions.

Ethical checkpoint. A donor signed a broad deed in 1995, but people depicted in the photographs object to online facial recognition and unrestricted download. What legal evidence, community authority, technical options, and interim actions should guide the response?
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
5.8 Review questions

Identification: Distinguish a digital library, institutional repository, digital archive, and digital exhibit.
Identification: Define master file, preservation copy, access derivative, and thumbnail.
Short answer: Why is ingest more than uploading files?
Short answer: What information should accompany a submission package?
Short answer: How do OAI-PMH and IIIF support different forms of interoperability?
Applied analysis: A project stores only JPEG thumbnails and a spreadsheet. Identify the service and stewardship risks.
Applied analysis: A repository migration exports metadata but loses file relationships and embargo dates. Which evaluation and exit tests were inadequate?
Scenario: A thesis embargo has no end-date automation or responsible office. Propose a corrected workflow.
Scenario: A digital exhibit receives a community takedown request. Outline an immediate and long-term response.
Synthesis: Explain how collection policy, metadata, platform configuration, rights, and preservation jointly determine access.
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
section: 5.9 Mini case: The centennial digital campus archive
text:
5.9 Mini case: The centennial digital campus archive
Bayview College plans a centennial portal containing yearbooks, student newspapers, photographs, theses, oral histories, and memorabilia. The president wants everything searchable before the anniversary in six months. The vendor proposes one hosted platform with unlimited users but charges for large exports. Most yearbooks are under copyright; newspapers name students in disciplinary reports; oral-history consent forms mention classroom research but not public streaming. Scans are stored on one external drive, metadata use inconsistent names, and no preservation budget exists after the grant. Alumni want facial tagging, while the Indigenous student association asks for restrictions on ceremonial images.
Guide questions

Divide the proposed content into appropriate system purposes, access categories, and phased priorities.
Design an ingest and governance workflow naming decisions, evidence, roles, checks, identifiers, and review points.
Create a platform evaluation and exit test that addresses files, metadata, relationships, rights, accessibility, costs, and community protocols.
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms
text:
5.10 Further reading and tools

5.10.1 Readings and frameworks

Lynch, “Institutional Repositories.” A foundational account of repositories as institutional infrastructure (Lynch 2003).
COAR Community Framework. Good practices for repository discovery, access, integrity, preservation, sustainability, and governance (Confederation of Open Access Repositories 2023).
OAI-PMH specification. Protocol for metadata harvesting (Open Archives Initiative 2008).
IIIF Presentation API. Interoperable structure and presentation of digital objects (IIIF Consortium 2024).
RightsStatements.org. Standardized rights statements for cultural heritage collections (RightsStatements.org 2026).
DOI Handbook. Identifier, metadata, and resolution responsibilities (International DOI Foundation 2026).



5.10.2 Open and widely accessible tools

Omeka S: digital collections, vocabularies, media, item sets, and exhibits (Omeka Team 2026).
DSpace: institutional repository software and documentation (DSpace Project 2026).
ArchivesSpace: archival description and collections management.
CollectiveAccess: collections management and public access.
OpenRefine: metadata cleanup and reconciliation before ingest.
ExifTool: extraction and inspection of embedded technical metadata.
BagIt tools: package files with manifests for transfer.
Fixity and checksum utilities: verify file integrity.



5.10.3 Chapter 5 bridge
Publishing content does not guarantee that users will find the right material. Chapter 6 examines information retrieval and discovery systems: indexes, fields, tokenization, Boolean logic, facets, relevance, precision, recall, federated search, discovery layers, and evidence-based evaluation.




Confederation of Open Access Repositories. 2023. COAR Community Framework for Good Practices in Repositories. COAR. https://coar-repositories.org/coar-community-framework-for-good-practices-in-repositories/.


DSpace Project. 2026. DSpace 8.x Documentation. https://wiki.lyrasis.org/display/DSDOC8x/DSpace+8.x+Documentation.


IIIF Consortium. 2024. IIIF Presentation API 3.0. https://iiif.io/api/presentation/3.0/.


International DOI Foundation. 2026. DOI Handbook. https://www.doi.org/the-identifier/resources/handbook.


Lynch, Clifford A. 2003. “Institutional Repositories: Essential Infrastructure for Scholarship in the Digital Age.” ARL Bimonthly Report, no. 226. https://www.cni.org/publications/cliffs-pubs/institutional-repositories-infrastructure-for-scholarship.


Omeka Team. 2026. Omeka s User Manual. https://omeka.org/s/docs/user-manual/.


Open Archives Initiative. 2008. The Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting, Version 2.0. https://www.openarchives.org/OAI/openarchivesprotocol.html.


RightsStatements.org. 2026. Rights Statements for Cultural Heritage Institutions. https://rightsstatements.org/en/.
crumbs: 5  Digital Libraries, Repositories, and Content Platforms

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
6.1 Chapter overview
A first-year student types climate change Philippines into a university discovery box. The system returns tens of thousands of records. A recent local policy report appears below older foreign articles; several promising records have no full-text link; a digitized map in the university repository does not appear at all. The search has produced results, but has it supported discovery?
The central problem of this chapter is how an information system turns a human information need into a useful, explainable, and actionable set of results. People ask questions in incomplete and changing language. Systems work with stored representations: terms, fields, identifiers, relationships, holdings, licenses, and usage signals. Information retrieval (IR) joins these worlds through indexing, query processing, matching, ranking, display, and access services.
Retrieval is not merely a search-box feature. It is an infrastructure connecting cataloguing, metadata, databases, repositories, licensed collections, archives, reference work, and user interfaces. A ranking algorithm cannot recover a subject term that was never assigned. A beautiful interface cannot supply a missing license link. A complete index may still fail a learner who does not know the vocabulary of a field. Discovery quality is therefore a shared responsibility among metadata workers, systems librarians, collection managers, vendors, reference librarians, and users.
This chapter explains the retrieval pipeline, Boolean and fielded searching, text processing, relevance ranking, facets, precision and recall, discovery layers, federated search, and link resolution. It also asks whose language, collections, and behavior become visible in a discovery system. The aim is not to memorize one vendor interface, but to develop concepts that transfer across catalogues, databases, repositories, archives, and web-scale discovery services.
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
6.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Explain how records, fields, tokens, indexes, queries, matching, and ranking support retrieval.
Construct Boolean, phrase, fielded, truncated, and faceted searches for a stated information need.
Calculate and interpret precision and recall in a small relevance-judgment set.
Compare catalogues, subject databases, repositories, federated search tools, and discovery layers by coverage and function.
Diagnose retrieval failures involving metadata, indexing, query processing, ranking, interface design, or access resolution.
Recommend an ethical and evidence-based improvement to a discovery service.
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
6.3 Key concepts
Table 6.1 provides the vocabulary used throughout the chapter. The terms describe different stages of a service; treating them as synonyms makes troubleshooting difficult.



Table 6.1: Key concepts in information retrieval and discovery





Term
Working definition




Information need
A gap in knowledge or evidence that motivates a search.


Corpus
The collection of items or records available to be searched.


Document
A retrievable unit, such as an article, book, record, image, dataset, or archival description.


Record
A structured representation of an item or resource.


Field
A named part of a record, such as title, creator, subject, or date.


Index
A structure that supports faster lookup than scanning every record at query time.


Inverted index
A mapping from terms to the documents or records in which they occur.


Token
A unit produced when text is divided for indexing or query processing.


Query
A formal expression submitted to a retrieval system.


Controlled vocabulary
An authorized set of terms and relationships used consistently for description and retrieval.


Boolean operator
AND, OR, or NOT used to combine or exclude search conditions.


Phrase search
A request for words in a specified sequence, often enclosed in quotation marks.


Truncation
A search technique that retrieves multiple word endings from a shared character string.


Stemming
Reduction of related word forms to a common stem for matching.


Synonym expansion
Automatic or deliberate addition of terms with similar meanings.


Facet
A category and value used to narrow or explore a result set.


Relevance
The degree to which a result helps satisfy an information need in context.


Ranking
The ordering of matched results using a scoring method.


Precision
The proportion of retrieved results judged relevant.


Recall
The proportion of all relevant items in the corpus that were retrieved.


Discovery layer
A user-facing service that searches and presents records from multiple collections or indexes.


Central index
A prebuilt aggregated index containing metadata or content from many sources.


Federated search
A method that sends a query to multiple remote systems and merges returned results.


Link resolver
A service that uses citation and institutional context to direct a user to an available copy or service.


OpenURL
A structured link carrying metadata about a requested item and the user's context to a resolver.


Relevance judgment
A documented decision that a result is or is not useful for a stated need.
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
6.4 Core discussion

6.4.1 From an information need to an accessible item
An information need is not identical to the words typed into a box. A student who enters rice pest may need a recent, locally applicable treatment guide written in Filipino and available without payment. The two-word query expresses only part of that need. During searching, the student may learn the scientific name of a pest, discover a government agency, or decide that date matters more than language. Searching is therefore iterative: the user and the system exchange signals, and the user’s understanding changes.
Figure 6.1 shows the service cycle. The upper path describes system work; the lower return path shows how results help a person revise the query or even the original question.






flowchart LR
    N["Information need"] --> Q["Query representation"]
    C["Corpus: records and content"] --> I["Processing and index"]
    Q --> M["Matching and ranking"]
    I --> M
    M --> D["Results display and facets"]
    D --> J["User judgment"]
    J --> A["Access, save, cite, or request"]
    J -->|"revise terms, filters, or scope"| Q
    A -->|"new knowledge or gap"| N




Figure 6.1: The information retrieval and access cycle





As Figure 6.1 indicates, successful discovery has at least two outcomes: the user identifies a suitable resource and can take a meaningful next action. A record marked “available” that leads to a broken link is a discovery failure even if the ranking was excellent.


6.4.2 Records, fields, and the searchable corpus
A corpus is the body of records or content that a system can search. In a catalogue it may consist mainly of bibliographic and holdings records. In a repository it may include metadata, abstracts, OCR text, and full text. In an archival system, collection- and series-level descriptions may be more prominent than item records. A discovery layer may combine a local catalogue, repository records, electronic-resource metadata, open-access sources, and a large vendor index.
Coverage determines what can possibly be found. A resource outside the corpus has zero chance of retrieval, regardless of the query. Coverage may be limited by collection decisions, licenses, harvesting failures, incompatible metadata, embargoes, or deliberate restrictions. NISO’s Open Discovery Initiative emphasizes transparency among content providers and discovery-service providers because coverage and indexing arrangements affect what users see (National Information Standards Organization 2020).
Structured records divide description into fields. Title, creator, subject, date, format, language, identifier, abstract, and full text can be indexed differently. A title field may receive a high ranking weight; a date field may support ranges; an identifier may require exact matching; a subject field may preserve controlled terms. This is one reason metadata remains central to discovery. When the title Mga Kuwentong Bayan ng Panay is placed in an undifferentiated note field, title searching and display may both fail.


6.4.3 How an inverted index works
Scanning every document for every query would be slow at library scale. Retrieval systems usually prepare an index in advance. A simplified pipeline normalizes text, divides it into tokens, and records where those tokens occur. An inverted index points from a term to documents, reversing the document-to-words relationship familiar to readers (Manning et al. 2008).
Suppose a tiny corpus contains these titles:

D1: Community Archives and Memory
D2: Digital Archives in Communities
D3: Memory Institutions and Digital Stewardship

After lowercasing and simplified tokenization, part of the index looks like Table 6.2.



Table 6.2: A simplified inverted index for three titles





Token
Posting list




archives
D1, D2


community
D1


communities
D2


digital
D2, D3


memory
D1, D3


stewardship
D3






Table 6.2 shows that exact tokenization treats community and communities as different terms. A stemmer might connect them, while a controlled subject heading could represent both records with the same authorized concept. Neither technique is automatically superior. Stemming can increase recall but may join unrelated forms; controlled vocabulary can improve consistency but requires governance and may not reflect community language.
Real indexes may store term positions for phrase searching, field identifiers for fielded search, frequency counts for ranking, language information, and permissions for access control. Indexing is not a one-time task. New and changed records must be added, deleted records removed, and mappings checked when schemas change.


6.4.4 Query formulation: translating a question
Good searching begins by separating concepts rather than typing a full assignment sentence. Consider: “How do public libraries support disaster preparedness in coastal communities?” The main concepts might be public libraries, disaster preparedness, and coastal communities. Each concept can have alternative terms:
("public librar*" OR "community librar*")
AND ("disaster preparedness" OR "disaster risk reduction" OR resilience)
AND (coastal OR island OR maritime)
This representation uses several common techniques:

AND requires both concepts and usually narrows a result set.
OR accepts alternatives and usually broadens a result set.
NOT excludes matches but can remove useful records and should be used cautiously.
Phrase searching requests adjacent words in order, although exact syntax varies.
Truncation such as librar* may retrieve library, libraries, and librarianship; the symbol and behavior vary by system.
Fielded searching limits a condition to a field, such as title, author, subject, or date.
Facets narrow a retrieved set by values such as format, year, language, location, or access status.

Table 6.3 connects each technique to a likely service problem. Interface labels and syntax must always be checked because systems implement these features differently.



Table 6.3: Search techniques, purposes, and cautions





Technique
Use when
Main caution




OR alternatives
Records may use different terms for one concept
Too many loose alternatives can add noise


AND concepts
Results discuss only one part of a multi-concept need
An absent concept can hide relevant records


Phrase
Word order carries a specific meaning
Different wording will be missed


Field
A term has a particular role, such as author or title
Incomplete or inconsistent metadata weakens matching


Truncation
Grammatical endings or related forms vary
The root may retrieve unintended words


Facet
The result set is useful but too broad
Missing or inconsistent values distort counts


Known-item identifier
An ISBN, DOI, call number, or exact identifier is known
Punctuation, normalization, or record errors can prevent a match






As Table 6.3 suggests, query formulation and metadata quality are inseparable. A language facet cannot reliably narrow results if language codes are missing. An author search depends on names being placed in creator fields and, ideally, connected through authority control.


6.4.5 Matching and relevance ranking
Boolean retrieval asks whether a record satisfies stated conditions. Ranked retrieval asks how strongly each record matches. Many systems combine both: filters impose conditions, while a scoring model orders the matches.
Ranking can consider term frequency, rarity across the corpus, field weights, phrase proximity, date, document type, popularity, citation signals, availability, and configured boosts. BM25, a widely used probabilistic ranking family, increases a score when query terms occur meaningfully in a document while moderating the effects of document length and repeated occurrences (Robertson and Zaragoza 2009). Apache Lucene, used within many search applications, provides a BM25 similarity implementation (Apache Lucene Project 2025). Students do not need to derive the formula in an introductory course, but they should understand three points:

A relevance score is calculated, not discovered as an objective property.
Different configurations can rank the same records differently.
High-ranked results reflect indexed evidence and design choices, not the full value of a work.

Relevance is contextual. A 2005 global review may be topically relevant to typhoon preparedness but unsuitable for a student who needs current Philippine local-government procedures. A primary source may be valuable to a historian even when it mentions the search terms only once. Systems approximate relevance; users and professionals interpret it.


6.4.6 Metadata as retrieval infrastructure
Full-text searching is powerful, but it does not make metadata obsolete. Metadata supplies distinctions that text alone often cannot:

a person’s role as author, editor, translator, or subject;
an authorized name across spelling and name changes;
a work’s language, format, date, audience, and rights status;
the relationship among an original, translation, edition, adaptation, and digitized copy;
the hierarchy of an archive or the sequence of a compound object;
local holdings, shelf location, license conditions, and access restrictions.

Controlled vocabularies connect records through consistent concepts, while user keywords capture current and local language. Effective discovery often uses both. A system may map a user’s term to an authorized heading, search notes and OCR text for additional recall, and display the authorized label as a facet. Metadata quality dimensions such as completeness, accuracy, consistency, and conformance directly influence discovery (Park 2009).
Crosswalking records from many sources introduces loss. A detailed local field may be mapped to a broad central field; names may arrive as uncontrolled strings; dates may use incompatible formats; subject headings may be combined with keywords. The interface may appear unified while the underlying evidence remains uneven. Discovery teams should therefore examine field mappings, not only the search screen.


6.4.7 Precision and recall
Retrieval evaluation requires a stated need and judgments about which items are relevant. Four counts organize the basic evaluation:

true positives (TP): relevant items retrieved;
false positives (FP): nonrelevant items retrieved;
false negatives (FN): relevant items not retrieved;
true negatives (TN): nonrelevant items not retrieved.

Precision and recall are then:
\[
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}
\]
\[
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}
\]
Imagine a corpus with 20 items, of which 8 are relevant. A search retrieves 10 items, including 6 relevant ones. The R code below calculates the measures reproducibly.



Table 6.4: Precision and recall for a small retrieval set





Measure
Calculation
Result
Interpretation




Precision
6 / (6 + 4)
60%
Six of every ten retrieved items were relevant.


Recall
6 / (6 + 2)
75%
The search found six of the eight relevant items.






Table 6.4 reports 60% precision and 75% recall. Neither value is “good” without context. A quick reference search may favor precision because the user needs one dependable answer. A systematic search for evidence or a legal records request may favor recall because missing a relevant item has serious consequences. Walters argues that relevance measures can complement use statistics in library assessment by focusing attention on what searches retrieve and miss (Walters 2016).
Precision and recall also have limits. Relevance judgments can differ between people and change over time. A binary relevant/not relevant label hides degrees of usefulness. Testing only popular English queries can conceal failures for Filipino, Cebuano, Bahasa Indonesia, Thai, or community terminology. Evaluation should combine measures with task observation, query-log analysis under privacy controls, interviews, accessibility testing, and metadata review.


6.4.8 Facets and exploratory discovery
A facet is not simply a filter displayed on the left side of a page. It is a structured view of values present in the result set. Format, topic, creator, date, language, collection, location, and access status can help users understand a corpus while narrowing it.
Useful facets require coherent metadata and understandable labels. A format facet containing Text, Book, eBook, Electronic resource, and PDF may mix content type, carrier, and file format. A geographic facet that lists both Philippines and Philippine Islands may split related records. Counts can mislead when records have multiple values or when metadata coverage is incomplete.
Facet design should follow user tasks. An archival researcher may need collection, record group, date, place, and creator. A school learner may understand Grade level better than a technical audience code. A government information service may need agency, issuance type, jurisdiction, and effectivity date. Good facets expose meaningful structure without demanding that users understand the database schema.


6.4.9 Catalogues, databases, repositories, and discovery layers
Different systems answer different questions. Table 6.5 compares common services.



Table 6.5: Comparison of retrieval and discovery systems





System
Typical strength
Typical limitation
Best question




Library catalogue
Known-item search, editions, holdings, location, circulation context
Limited article-level coverage
Does the library hold this book or edition?


Subject database
Focused disciplinary indexing, abstracts, controlled vocabulary
Licensed scope and specialized interface
What research has this discipline indexed?


Institutional repository
Local scholarship, theses, reports, datasets, and open files
Variable deposit and metadata completeness
What has this institution produced or preserved?


Web-scale discovery layer
Broad single search across local and aggregated metadata
Opaque coverage, mappings, and ranking
What can I discover across many library sources?


Federated search
Current querying of multiple remote sources
Slow response, duplicate records, inconsistent result merging
What do several live systems return now?


Archival finding-aid portal
Context, provenance, hierarchy, and collection-level description
Item-level discovery may be incomplete
Which records exist in this creator or collection context?






As Table 6.5 shows, no system is universally complete. A discovery layer offers a useful starting point but does not replace specialized databases, archival context, or professional search strategy. Teaching should avoid both extremes: presenting the single search box as comprehensive, or treating it as academically inferior. Its fitness depends on the task.
A central-index discovery service searches a preharvested index, which can be fast and consistently ranked. Federated search sends the query to remote sources at search time. Federated results may reflect current remote data, but differences in syntax, response speed, ranking, and record structure make merging difficult. The technical distinction matters when diagnosing missing records: did harvesting fail, did a remote target time out, or did field translation change the query?


6.4.10 From citation to copy: link resolution
Discovery often identifies a citation before it identifies an accessible copy. A link resolver receives metadata about the cited item plus institutional context, checks a knowledge base of holdings and licenses, and offers appropriate services. OpenURL standardizes a framework for carrying this context (The OpenURL Framework for Context-Sensitive Services 2010). A resolver may link to licensed full text, an open copy, the catalogue, document delivery, or interlibrary loan.
Figure 6.2 separates discovery from delivery.






sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant D as Discovery system
    participant R as Link resolver
    participant K as Knowledge base
    participant T as Target service
    U->>D: Select "Check access"
    D->>R: Send citation and institutional context
    R->>K: Check holdings, dates, licenses, and links
    K-->>R: Return candidate services
    R-->>U: Display full text, catalogue, or request options
    U->>T: Follow selected service




Figure 6.2: A simplified context-sensitive link-resolution workflow





In Figure 6.2, a broken journey can result from incorrect citation metadata, inaccurate holdings dates, a changed target URL, authentication failure, or license restrictions. “No full text” is not always a collection-development problem. It can be a knowledge-base or metadata-maintenance problem.
Older and specialized interoperability protocols remain relevant. Z39.50 supports search and retrieval across systems and continues to appear in library environments (Library of Congress 2026). OAI-PMH supports metadata harvesting for repositories (Open Archives Initiative 2008). These protocols do different jobs: one should not be described as a replacement for the other.


6.4.11 Diagnosing retrieval as a layered service
When users report “search is broken,” a useful diagnosis moves through layers:

Coverage: Is the source or record in the searchable corpus?
Metadata: Are essential values present, accurate, and mapped to the expected fields?
Indexing: Was the record processed, tokenized, and updated correctly?
Query: Did syntax, language processing, spelling correction, or field translation alter the request?
Matching and ranking: Was the record matched but placed too low? Which boosts or filters mattered?
Interface: Could the user interpret results, facets, availability, and system scope?
Delivery: Did authentication, link resolution, rights, or holdings prevent access?
Evaluation: Is the reported failure reproducible for a stated task and user context?

This layered method prevents expensive but irrelevant responses. Replacing the interface will not repair missing repository harvesting; changing the ranking model will not correct a broken subscription date range.
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
6.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

6.5.1 Academic library: one query, several evidence environments
A Philippine university student researching jeepney modernization might begin with a discovery layer, continue to a transport or policy database, search the institutional repository for local theses, and consult government websites for current issuances. The discovery layer offers breadth, but a local repository may contain the most contextually relevant work. Librarians can teach a search pathway rather than a preferred box: identify the evidence type, choose a likely corpus, translate concepts, judge results, and follow access options.


6.5.2 Public library: multilingual local-history discovery
A city library digitizes photographs labelled in English, Filipino, and a local language. Community members search former street names, nicknames, barangay names, and terms used before administrative boundary changes. The library can retain original captions, add current and historical names, record language, link place authorities, and invite documented community corrections. This approach improves retrieval while preserving provenance instead of silently replacing older description.


6.5.3 School library: age-appropriate facets
A school catalogue has subject headings suitable for professional control but difficult for younger pupils. The library retains the controlled headings for consistency and adds a student-facing topic facet with tested plain-language labels. Search logs are reviewed only in aggregate and with a short retention period. The design connects knowledge organization to age-appropriate UX and privacy.


6.5.4 Government information service: version and authority
A public information portal contains memoranda, circulars, forms, and superseded issuances. Keyword relevance alone can place an obsolete document first. Structured fields for issuing agency, issuance type, number, effectivity date, amendment relationship, and current status allow the interface to label superseded material and prioritize authoritative versions without erasing the historical record.


6.5.5 Regional cultural heritage aggregation
A Southeast Asian cultural heritage portal may aggregate records produced under different cataloguing traditions, scripts, languages, and levels of description. A single “creator” field can flatten distinctions among artist, collector, photographer, community, and donor. Crosswalks should document loss, interfaces should expose source institutions, and search evaluation should include local scripts and transliterations. Aggregation expands reach, but it does not remove the need to respect source context.

Reflect: Choose a local topic whose name has changed over time. What contemporary term, historical term, variant spelling, language form, broader term, and narrower term would you include in a test query set?
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
section: 6.6 Hands-on activity: Compare three discovery environments
text:
6.6 Hands-on activity: Compare three discovery environments

6.6.1 Purpose
Investigate how corpus coverage, metadata, search features, ranking, and access services affect results for one information need. This lab contributes to the short-lab portfolio and prepares the UX or discovery critique.


6.6.2 Tools

a spreadsheet application or R with Quarto;
access to three systems, preferably a library catalogue or discovery layer, a subject database or national portal, and an institutional repository;
browser privacy mode if testing personalization is relevant;
the search-log fields in Table 6.6.




Table 6.6: Required fields in the discovery comparison search log





Field
Record




System and date
Name, URL, date, and time


Stated information need
One sentence including audience, place, date, or format needs


Corpus claim
What the interface says it searches


Exact query
Copy exactly, including punctuation


Filters or facets
Record values and sequence


Reported result count
Record as displayed; treat as approximate


Top 10 relevance judgments
Relevant / partly relevant / not relevant, with one-line reason


Useful metadata
Fields or snippets that supported judgment


Access outcome
Opened, authenticated, requested, failed, or unavailable


Observed problem
Coverage, metadata, indexing, ranking, interface, or delivery


Proposed improvement
One feasible, evidence-linked change






Table 6.6 makes the procedure reproducible. Do not record usernames, patron identifiers, or sensitive personal topics in a shared file.


6.6.3 Steps

Write one information-need statement. Include at least two contextual requirements, such as Philippine coverage, publication date, language, audience, evidence type, or online availability.
Identify two or three main concepts. List synonyms, variant spellings, controlled terms, abbreviations, and local-language terms.
Select three discovery environments with meaningfully different corpora.
Run the same basic query in each system. Record the exact query, date, claimed scope, result count, available facets, and first ten results.
Judge each of the first ten results as relevant, partly relevant, or not relevant. State a reason based on the information need, not only the presence of keywords.
For a simple comparison, count “relevant” as relevant and compute precision at 10: relevant items in the first ten divided by ten. Do not call this full-system recall; the total number of relevant items is unknown.
Revise the search once in each system using a phrase, field, Boolean operator, controlled term, or facet. Record what changed.
Attempt to access the two most promising items from each system. Record whether the link opened, required authentication, led to a request service, or failed.
Classify one important problem per system as coverage, metadata, indexing/query processing, ranking, interface, or delivery.
Recommend one feasible improvement and identify who would need to act: metadata staff, systems staff, reference staff, vendor, content provider, or policy owner.



6.6.4 Expected output
Submit:

a completed search log for three systems;
a concept-and-synonym map;
a table comparing precision at 10 before and after one revision;
two annotated screenshots with personal data removed;
a 300-word recommendation connecting evidence to one service improvement; and
a 100-word reflection answering: What did the system make easy to see, and what did it make easy to overlook?



6.6.5 Lab checklist

The information need is specific enough to support relevance judgments.
Exact queries and filters are reproducible.
Three systems have different scopes.
Relevance reasons refer to the stated need.
Precision at 10 is labelled correctly and not presented as recall.
Access outcomes are tested, not assumed from icons.
The recommendation identifies a layer and responsible actor.
Screenshots and logs contain no personal information.



6.6.6 Assessment rubric
Table 6.7 emphasizes reasoning and documentation over the number of results found.



Table 6.7: Rubric for the three-system discovery comparison





Criterion
Weight
Strong performance




Search design
20%
Need, concepts, variants, and revisions are logically connected


Documentation
20%
Queries, filters, dates, scope, and access outcomes can be reproduced


Relevance analysis
25%
Judgments use explicit criteria; precision at 10 is correct and qualified


Systems diagnosis
20%
Problems are assigned to plausible layers with supporting evidence


Recommendation and reflection
15%
Improvement is feasible, professionally responsible, and attentive to what remains unseen

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
section: 6.7 Ethical and professional issue: ranking, privacy, and representational harm
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
6.8 Review questions

Identification: What is an inverted index, and how does it differ from a list of words stored inside one document record?
Identification: Define precision and recall. What does the denominator of each measure represent?
Short answer: Why can a relevant item fail to appear even when the user chooses reasonable search terms? Name three different layers.
Short answer: Explain why full-text indexing does not eliminate the need for controlled metadata and authority control.
Applied search: Translate the need “recent Filipino-language materials for parents about children’s online privacy” into a concept map and a sample Boolean query.
Applied analysis: A search retrieves 12 items, 9 of which are relevant. Evaluators know of 15 relevant items in the corpus. Calculate precision and recall and interpret both values.
Scenario: A repository record appears in the local repository but not in the university discovery layer. What evidence would you collect before changing the ranking configuration?
Scenario: A facet lists Thesis, Dissertation, Text, PDF, and Electronic resource under one heading. Diagnose the conceptual problem and propose a clearer design.
Scenario: The top result links to a journal page, but the user’s institution has access through another provider. Which services or data should be checked?
Critical analysis: How might usage-based ranking reproduce existing inequalities in a regional cultural heritage portal? Propose two safeguards.
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
