section: 6.9 Mini case: The missing coastal knowledge collection
text:
6.9 Mini case: The missing coastal knowledge collection
Baybay State College launches a discovery layer linking its catalogue, institutional repository, and selected databases. Faculty search for locally produced work on mangrove rehabilitation. The repository contains 46 theses and extension reports, but only 11 appear in discovery. Searching the municipality’s current name finds few older studies. Searching mangrove ranks international articles above local reports. Several local records have titles and dates but no subjects, abstracts, geographic fields, or stable links. The vendor recommends boosting the repository domain globally. The university archivist warns that some reports contain names and precise locations supplied by vulnerable coastal households.

6.9.1 Guide questions

Which coverage, metadata, indexing, ranking, access, privacy, and governance problems can you identify? Keep causes separate from symptoms.
What test set would you build to determine whether historical place names, local terminology, and repository records are retrievable?
What phased improvement would balance local visibility with privacy and avoid making every repository item rank highly regardless of relevance?
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems
text:
6.10 Further reading and tools

6.10.1 Readings and standards

Manning, Raghavan, and Schuetze, Introduction to Information Retrieval. A freely available foundational text on indexing, Boolean retrieval, ranking, and evaluation (Manning et al. 2008).
Robertson and Zaragoza, “The Probabilistic Relevance Framework.” A deeper explanation of BM25 and its assumptions (Robertson and Zaragoza 2009).
NISO Open Discovery Initiative. Recommended practice on transparency and exchange among content and discovery providers (National Information Standards Organization 2020).
ANSI/NISO Z39.88, OpenURL. The framework underlying many context-sensitive linking services (The OpenURL Framework for Context-Sensitive Services 2010).
Library of Congress Z39.50 Maintenance Agency. Background and documentation for a long-standing search-and-retrieval standard (Library of Congress 2026).
Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting. Essential for understanding repository metadata aggregation (Open Archives Initiative 2008).
Walters on recall, precision, and relevance in library assessment. A bridge from IR measures to collection and service evaluation (Walters 2016).



6.10.2 Open or widely accessible tools

Apache Solr and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch: search platforms for experimenting with fields, analyzers, facets, and ranking. Begin with a small non-sensitive dataset.
Apache Lucene documentation: technical reference for the search library beneath many systems, including BM25 scoring (Apache Lucene Project 2025).
VuFind: an open-source library resource discovery interface useful for examining records, facets, and integrations.
Blacklight: an open-source discovery framework commonly used with Solr.
OpenRefine: useful for inspecting and reconciling metadata values before indexing.
R and Quarto: appropriate for reproducible search logs, relevance calculations, tables, and reports. The calculations in this chapter use base R so that no additional package is required (R Core Team 2026; Posit 2026).



6.10.3 Closing synthesis
Information retrieval is a chain of representations and decisions. Users represent needs as queries; institutions represent resources as records and indexes; systems represent relevance as scores; interfaces represent choices as ranked lists and facets. At every stage, something can be clarified, lost, privileged, or hidden.
For LIS professionals, the practical question is not simply “Does search work?” It is: Which users can discover which resources, through which language and metadata, under what ranking and access rules, with what evidence of success, and with what consequences for privacy and equity? Chapter 7 builds on this question by introducing programming as a way to inspect, transform, and test structured information work reproducibly.




Apache Lucene Project. 2025. BM25Similarity API Documentation. https://lucene.apache.org/core/9_12_1/core/org/apache/lucene/search/similarities/BM25Similarity.html.


Library of Congress. 2026. Z39.50 International Standard Maintenance Agency. https://www.loc.gov/z3950/agency/.


Manning, Christopher D., Prabhakar Raghavan, and Hinrich Schuetze. 2008. Introduction to Information Retrieval. Cambridge University Press. https://nlp.stanford.edu/IR-book/.


National Information Standards Organization. 2020. Open Discovery Initiative: Promoting Transparency in Discovery. RP-19-2020. NISO. https://www.niso.org/publications/rp-19-2020-odi.


National Privacy Commission. 2023. Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://privacy.gov.ph/data-privacy-act/.


Open Archives Initiative. 2008. The Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting, Version 2.0. https://www.openarchives.org/OAI/openarchivesprotocol.html.


Park, Jung-Ran. 2009. “Metadata Quality in Digital Repositories: A Survey of the Current State of the Art.” Cataloging and Classification Quarterly 47 (3–4): 213–28. https://doi.org/10.1080/01639370902737240.


Posit. 2026. Quarto Documentation. https://quarto.org/docs/guide/.


R Core Team. 2026. R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing. R Foundation for Statistical Computing. https://www.R-project.org/.


Republic of the Philippines. 2012. Republic Act No. 10173: Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://lawphil.net/statutes/repacts/ra2012/ra_10173_2012.html.


Robertson, Stephen, and Hugo Zaragoza. 2009. “The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Beyond.” Foundations and Trends in Information Retrieval 3 (4): 333–89. https://doi.org/10.1561/1500000019.


The OpenURL Framework for Context-Sensitive Services, Pub. L. Nos. ANSI/NISO Z39.88-2004 (R2010) (2010). https://www.niso.org/publications/z3988-2004-r2010.


Walters, William H. 2016. “Beyond Use Statistics: Recall, Precision, and Relevance in the Assessment and Management of Academic Libraries.” Journal of Librarianship and Information Science 48 (4): 340–52. https://doi.org/10.1177/0961000615572174.
crumbs: 6  Information Retrieval and Discovery Systems

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
7.1 Chapter overview
A repository assistant receives 4,200 records exported from several departments. Dates appear as 2025-03-14, 14 March 2025, 03/14/25, and n.d.. Creator names contain extra spaces. Some titles are blank. File names mix spaces, punctuation, and inconsistent identifiers. Correcting every cell by hand would take days and would be difficult to audit. A global search-and-replace might be faster, but it could silently damage valid values.
The central problem of this chapter is how to express a repeatable information-processing procedure clearly enough that a computer can carry it out while people remain responsible for the rules, exceptions, and consequences. Programming is not magic and is not limited to professional software developers. In LIS work, a short, well-documented script can import a metadata export, preserve the original, standardize predictable values, flag uncertain cases, summarize changes, and produce a new file for review.
Programming should not be confused with indiscriminate automation. A program implements instructions. An automation connects instructions to a recurring workflow, often with inputs, schedules, outputs, logs, permissions, and failure handling. A human task may include contextual judgment that cannot safely be reduced to rules. The goal is therefore not “automate everything.” It is to decide what can be made consistent and reproducible, what evidence must be retained, and where professional review must remain.
This chapter introduces algorithms, scripts, variables, data types, vectors, data frames, conditions, functions, packages, input and output, errors, debugging, testing, and reproducibility through R. R is used because it is open source, works well with rectangular data, and integrates with Quarto documents (R Core Team 2026; Posit 2026). The concepts transfer to Python, JavaScript, shell scripting, spreadsheet formulas, and workflow tools.
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
7.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Explain algorithms, programs, scripts, data types, variables, functions, packages, and automation in LIS terms.
Read and modify a short R script containing objects, vectors, data frames, conditions, and functions.
Import, inspect, clean, validate, and export a small metadata CSV without overwriting the source file.
Apply conditional and vectorized operations to flag missing, inconsistent, or invalid values.
Test and debug a script using small known examples, assertions, logs, and exception reports.
Evaluate an automation proposal for risk, privacy, bias, maintainability, and appropriate human review.
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
7.3 Key concepts
Table 7.1 defines the chapter’s working vocabulary. The definitions emphasize actions and responsibilities rather than particular R syntax.



Table 7.1: Key concepts in programming and automation





Term
Working definition




Algorithm
A finite, ordered procedure for accomplishing a task or solving a defined problem.


Program
Instructions and related components that a computer can execute.


Script
A usually small source-code file used to perform a focused or sequential task.


Source code
Human-readable instructions written in a programming language.


Variable
A named reference whose associated value may be used or changed.


Object
A value stored and manipulated in R, including vectors, tables, functions, and models.


Data type
A category that determines how a value is represented and what operations are valid.


Vector
An ordered collection of values that normally share one data type.


Data frame
A rectangular table in which columns are vectors and rows are observations.


Expression
Code that produces or transforms a value.


Assignment
Binding a name to a value, commonly with the R operator <-.


Condition
A logical test used to choose an action or classify a value.


Iteration
Repeated execution over values, records, files, or another collection.


Vectorization
Applying an operation to a whole vector rather than explicitly processing one item at a time.


Function
A named, reusable block of instructions with defined inputs and output.


Argument
A value supplied to a function.


Return value
The value produced by a function after it finishes.


Package
A documented collection of R functions, data, and supporting materials.


Dependency
Software or data required for another program to run correctly.


Input
Data, files, parameters, or signals received by a program.


Output
Data, files, messages, or actions produced by a program.


Exception
A case requiring separate handling because it does not fit the normal rule.


Error
A condition that prevents code from completing as intended.


Warning
A message that indicates a possible problem while execution may continue.


Debugging
The systematic process of locating, understanding, and correcting defects.


Test
A controlled check comparing actual behavior with an expected result.


Assertion
A statement that stops or flags execution when a required condition is false.


Log
A time-stamped record of actions, results, decisions, or failures.


Reproducibility
The ability to obtain consistent results from documented data, code, software, and steps.


Automation
The scheduled or triggered execution of a defined process with limited manual intervention.


Idempotence
The property that rerunning a process has no unintended additional effect.
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
7.4 Core discussion

7.4.1 Programming as explicit procedural knowledge
LIS professionals already design procedures. A cataloguing manual states how to choose a preferred source of information. A digitization workflow specifies file naming, quality checks, and derivative creation. A reference interview uses questions to clarify a need. Programming makes one part of procedural knowledge explicit in a form a computer can execute.
Consider a rule: “Remove spaces at the beginning and end of every title; if nothing remains, flag the record for review.” The rule has an input, a transformation, a condition, and an output. Before coding it, the author must answer professional questions. Does whitespace inside the title remain? Is a title containing only punctuation missing? Should an absent supplied title be created automatically? Where will the original value be retained? The code cannot responsibly answer questions that its author has not resolved.
An algorithm is the procedure; source code expresses it in a language; a program is the executable arrangement; and a script is commonly a focused code file that coordinates a task. The same algorithm can be implemented in several languages. Syntax changes, but decomposition, validation, testing, and documentation remain.


7.4.2 A safe automation workflow
Figure 7.1 shows a beginner workflow for metadata processing. It separates source data, transformations, exceptions, validation, and approved delivery.






flowchart LR
    S["Read-only source export"] --> I["Import and inspect"]
    I --> R["Declare rules and assumptions"]
    R --> T["Transform a working copy"]
    T --> E["Separate exceptions"]
    T --> V["Validate expected results"]
    E --> H["Human review"]
    V --> H
    H --> O["Approved output"]
    T --> L["Change log and summary"]
    H --> L
    O --> A["Archive code, parameters, and report"]




Figure 7.1: A reviewable workflow for LIS data automation





As Figure 7.1 indicates, the source export is not overwritten. Predictable cases can move through transformations, while uncertain records enter an exception queue. Validation checks the output against stated rules. Human review is not evidence that automation failed; it is a designed control for cases requiring context.
This workflow also supports rollback. If a date rule is later found to be wrong, the team can correct the script and regenerate the output from the preserved source. Manual editing of the only copy would make the history harder to reconstruct.


7.4.3 Meeting R: objects, assignment, and expressions
R evaluates expressions and stores results as objects. The assignment operator <- binds a name to a value.


[1] "Barangay Reading Center Collection"


[1] 1300


[1] "logical"


In this example, text is placed in quotation marks, the number is numeric, and FALSE is a logical value. Names such as record_count communicate more than names such as x. R is case-sensitive: Title, title, and TITLE are different names.
Comments begin with # and explain decisions that code alone cannot make clear:
# Keep the source wording; only remove surrounding whitespace.
clean_title <- trimws(source_title)
A comment should explain why a rule exists, its source, or a non-obvious limitation. Comments that merely translate syntax, such as # add one above x <- x + 1, create noise.


7.4.4 Data types and missing values
Common R data types include character, numeric, integer, logical, and date-time classes. Data types affect valid operations. The text "2025" cannot automatically be treated as a publication year in every context, and identifiers with leading zeros should often remain character values.
R represents a missing value with NA. It is not the same as an empty string "", the text "N/A", the number 0, or the word "unknown". These may express different states:

no value was supplied;
the value is not applicable;
the value is unknown;
the value is intentionally withheld;
the field contains an invalid placeholder.

Collapsing these states without a policy can damage meaning. Use is.na(x) to test missingness rather than x == NA.
Table 7.2 gives LIS examples and common mistakes.



Table 7.2: Common R data types in LIS work





Type
LIS example
Caution




Character
Title, identifier, language code
Do not convert identifiers merely because they contain digits


Numeric
File size in megabytes
Floating-point values may require tolerance in comparisons


Integer
Page or item count
Counts should not contain decimal fractions


Logical
Rights reviewed: TRUE/FALSE
Unknown is not always equivalent to FALSE


Date
Embargo end or accession date
Ambiguous day/month order requires a declared rule


Missing value
Unknown creator represented as NA
NA, blank, not applicable, and withheld may have different meanings






As Table 7.2 shows, choosing a type is also a description decision. A barcode is an identifier, not a quantity: adding two barcodes is meaningless even when both contain only digits.


7.4.5 Vectors: working with a column of values
A vector holds an ordered set of values of a common type. The function c() combines values.


[1]  TRUE FALSE FALSE  TRUE


[1] FALSE  TRUE    NA  TRUE


[1] FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE


[1] "Book" "Book"


The comparison formats == "Book" returns a logical vector. This is important because many R operations are vectorized: one expression applies to a whole column. The expression years >= 2020 produces TRUE, TRUE, NA, and TRUE for the example. The missing year remains unknown rather than becoming false.
Useful vector operations include:
length(formats)             # number of values
unique(formats)             # distinct values
table(formats)              # frequency counts
sum(is.na(years))           # count missing years
sort(unique(formats))       # distinct values in order
Before changing metadata, frequency tables often reveal variants such as Book, book, Books, and BK. Inspection should precede normalization.


7.4.6 Data frames and tidy rectangular data
A data frame is a rectangular table whose columns are vectors. In a tidy arrangement, each variable is a column, each observation is a row, and each type of observational unit forms a table (Wickham 2014). This structure is not the only possible data model, but it supports consistent analysis and transformation.


'data.frame':   3 obs. of  4 variables:
 $ record_id: chr  "R001" "R002" "R003"
 $ title    : chr  "  Coastal livelihoods " "" "Mga Kuwentong Bayan"
 $ year     : chr  "2021" "n.d." "2019"
 $ language : chr  "eng" "eng" "fil"


  record_id                  title year language
1      R001   Coastal livelihoods  2021      eng
2      R002                        n.d.      eng
3      R003    Mga Kuwentong Bayan 2019      fil


Each row needs a stable identifier. Row numbers are weak identifiers because sorting or filtering changes their position. A record_id allows an exception report to point back to the source record.
Real bibliographic and archival data are not always naturally flat. One work can have multiple creators, subjects, files, or related works. Repeating these values in a single cell may be acceptable for a temporary exchange file, but a relational database or structured format may be more appropriate for long-term management. Chapter 3’s data-modeling principles still apply.


7.4.7 Import before transformation
Programs receive input and produce output. CSV is common for metadata exchange, but it has hazards: character encoding, embedded commas, line breaks, date interpretation, and automatic type guessing. The readr package provides functions for rectangular text data and reports parsing problems (Tidyverse Team 2026b).
The explicit import types prevent identifiers from being converted to numbers and preserve date strings until their patterns are understood. The na argument should reflect a documented local policy. Do not casually treat unknown, withheld, and not applicable as identical missing values.
Initial inspection can ask:
names(records_raw)
dim(records_raw)
summary(records_raw)
head(records_raw, 10)
sort(table(records_raw$language, useNA = "ifany"), decreasing = TRUE)
Never begin by writing changes back to the integrated library system or repository. Work with an export or test environment, confirm that the export is complete, and record when and how it was created.


7.4.8 Conditions: rules with consequences
A condition produces TRUE, FALSE, or sometimes NA. An if statement chooses one path for a single condition.


[1] "REVIEW_MISSING_TITLE"


[1] "OK"


For whole columns, vectorized functions are usually clearer. Base R’s ifelse() or dplyr’s case_when() can classify records. The following rule preserves the source column and creates new columns:


  record_id                  title         title_clean         title_status
1      R001   Coastal livelihoods  Coastal livelihoods                   OK
2      R002                                            REVIEW_MISSING_TITLE
3      R003    Mga Kuwentong Bayan Mga Kuwentong Bayan                   OK


This design is preferable to silently filling every missing title with [Untitled]. Whether a supplied title may be constructed depends on the descriptive standard, source evidence, and institutional policy.


7.4.9 Functions: packaging one rule
A function gives a procedure a name, accepts arguments, and returns a result. Functions reduce duplication and provide units that can be tested.


[1] "eng" "fil" "ceb" NA   


The unmatched Hiligaynon becomes NA, which is useful for teaching but insufficient for production: the script should preserve the original value and flag the row. The example also keeps fil and tgl distinct. A mapping table should cite the vocabulary and document local decisions rather than encode casual assumptions about language identity.
Good functions are focused. A function named clean_everything() that changes titles, dates, names, subjects, and rights is hard to test. Separate functions such as trim_title(), parse_date(), and validate_rights_uri() make failures easier to locate.


7.4.10 Iteration and vectorization
Iteration repeats an operation. A for loop is appropriate when the sequence itself matters or when processing files one at a time.
Vectorized operations are often shorter for column transformations, but loops are not inherently wrong. Clarity and correctness matter more than avoiding a particular construct. For many files, iteration must also handle failures: one corrupt CSV should be logged and isolated rather than causing silent loss or an incomplete batch presented as complete.


7.4.11 Packages and dependencies
Base R includes many data-processing functions. Packages extend R with documented functions. The tidyverse is a collection of packages with related conventions (Tidyverse Team 2026d). Three useful packages for this chapter are:

readr for rectangular text import and export;
dplyr for selecting, filtering, creating, and summarizing columns (Tidyverse Team 2026a);
stringr for consistent text operations (Tidyverse Team 2026c).

Packages must be installed once and loaded in a session. In a shared script, installation is normally kept separate from analysis:
# Run deliberately at setup time, not every time the script executes.
install.packages(c("readr", "dplyr", "stringr"))

# Run at the start of the analysis session.
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
Every dependency adds maintenance and supply-chain considerations. Record package versions, prefer well-maintained packages from trusted repositories, review major updates, and avoid installing unknown code merely because it solves a small problem. A short base R solution may be more sustainable than a dependency-heavy one; an established package may be safer than a custom parser. The choice depends on risk and maintenance capacity.


7.4.12 A readable metadata-cleaning pipeline
The pipe operator passes a result to the next operation. The following non-executed example shows a tidyverse workflow while preserving source columns:
The regular expressions in the pipeline recognize shapes, not truth. The string 2025-99-99 matches the date shape but is not a valid calendar date. Pattern checks should be followed by semantic validation. Similarly, a nonblank title is not necessarily accurate.


7.4.13 Validation, assertions, and exception reports
Cleaning and validation are different. Cleaning applies a declared transformation; validation asks whether data satisfy requirements. A script should fail clearly when essential assumptions are false.
stopifnot() is a simple assertion. In production, more informative error messages and a testing framework may be appropriate. The key idea is that the script should not quietly continue when identifiers are duplicated or required columns disappear.
An exception report converts uncertainty into work that can be assigned and reviewed:
exceptions <- records_clean |>
  filter(title_status != "OK" | !date_status %in% c("OK_YEAR", "OK_DATE")) |>
  select(record_id, title_source, title, title_status,
         date_source, date_issued, date_status)
Include the record identifier, source value, proposed value, rule, reason, and review decision. Do not export sensitive fields that reviewers do not need.


7.4.14 Errors, warnings, and debugging
An error stops an operation; a warning signals a possible problem while execution may continue; a message reports progress or information. Suppressing all warnings makes a report look clean while hiding evidence.
A useful debugging process is:

reproduce the failure with the smallest relevant input;
read the complete error and identify the first failing line under your control;
inspect object names, types, dimensions, and representative values;
state what you expected and what actually happened;
test one hypothesis at a time;
correct the cause, not merely the displayed symptom;
add a test that would catch the defect if it returned.

Common beginner problems are listed in Table 7.3.



Table 7.3: A first-response guide for common R problems





Symptom
Likely question
First check




Object not found
Was the object created, and is spelling/capitalization exact?
ls(), names(), and the preceding line


Unexpected NA values
Did conversion, unmatched mapping, or missing input produce NA?
is.na(), source values, and warnings


Numbers imported from identifiers
Did type guessing treat a character identifier as numeric?
str() and import column specifications


Condition has length greater than one
Was scalar if used where a vectorized operation was needed?
length() and the condition expression


Replacement has wrong number of rows
Do the source and replacement vectors have compatible lengths?
nrow() and length()


Output differs on another computer
Are R, package versions, locale, encoding, paths, and inputs documented?
sessionInfo(), file hashes, locale, and relative paths






Table 7.3 frames errors as evidence. Guessing random syntax changes can create a second defect while leaving the first unexplained.


7.4.15 Testing transformation rules
Tests should include typical values, boundaries, missing values, multilingual text, punctuation, and known exceptions. For the normalize_language() function:
The fourth test does not claim Hiligaynon lacks a valid code. It confirms that the current mapping is incomplete and must flag rather than invent a value. Once the authorized mapping is expanded, the expected result and documentation should change together.
Best-practice guidance for computational work emphasizes small steps, version control, testing, and automated repetition (Wilson et al. 2014). Reproducibility guidance likewise recommends recording how each result was produced and preserving intermediate reasoning (Sandve et al. 2013).


7.4.16 Output, logs, and reproducible reports
Output should be deliberately named and never confused with source data:
A run record should identify source file, source checksum when appropriate, run date, script version, parameters, row counts, exceptions, software versions, operator, and approval status. Quarto can combine narrative, code, results, tables, and citations in one reproducible document (Posit 2026). R for Data Science provides a freely accessible introduction to importing, transforming, and communicating data with R (Wickham et al. 2023).
Avoid absolute paths tied to one person’s computer. A project structure such as data/, R/, output/, and reports/ supports relative paths. Source data containing personal information should not be committed to public version control.


7.4.17 Deciding what to automate
Tasks are good initial candidates when they are frequent, rule-based, reversible, testable, and performed on consistent inputs. Tasks require caution when they involve ambiguous description, rights, identity, appraisal, deletion, access restriction, or direct changes to production systems.
Table 7.4 provides a decision framework.



Table 7.4: Factors for deciding whether and how to automate





Dimension
Lower risk signal
Higher risk signal




Rule clarity
Rule has authoritative, testable criteria
Rule depends on context or contested judgment


Input consistency
Schema and values are stable
Inputs vary without notice


Consequence of error
Error affects a review copy
Error changes rights, identity, deletion, or public access


Reversibility
Source and rollback are preserved
Action is destructive or difficult to undo


Testability
Expected outputs can be checked
Correctness cannot be observed


Exception rate
Exceptions are rare and visible
Exceptions are common or silently coerced


Privacy and security
Only necessary nonsensitive data are processed
Personal or restricted data move across systems


Maintenance ownership
Named staff can understand and maintain it
Only the original author understands the workflow






As Table 7.4 shows, automation is a governance decision. A high-risk task may still use code, but with a dry run, smaller batches, peer review, stronger testing, approvals, access controls, and an explicit rollback plan.
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
7.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

7.5.1 Academic library: thesis deposit quality checks
A university library receives thesis metadata from several colleges. A script checks that each submission has a stable identifier, nonblank title, department code, adviser field, date pattern, rights statement, and file reference. It normalizes whitespace and approved codes but does not invent missing titles or change names. Exceptions return to depositing units with record identifiers and clear reasons. The workflow reduces repetitive checking while preserving academic and cataloguing responsibility.


7.5.2 Public library: multilingual program statistics
A city library combines monthly CSV files from branch reading programs. Branch names and activity labels vary. A mapping table standardizes approved organizational codes, while unmatched labels appear in an exception report. Participant totals are aggregated only after duplicates and missing branch identifiers are checked. Public reporting uses grouped counts to reduce privacy risk, especially for small communities.


7.5.3 School library: accession and label preparation
A school library script produces draft spine-label data from an accession export. It checks required call-number components, detects duplicate accession numbers, and flags unusually long labels. Staff review every label before printing. The script is helpful because formatting rules are repeatable; classification and subject analysis remain professional judgments.


7.5.4 Government records center: file inventory validation
A records center validates transfer-list spreadsheets from offices. The script checks series codes, inclusive dates, retention-rule references, box-number sequences, and required security classifications. It never disposes of a record. Destruction requires authorized appraisal, approval, and documented action outside the validation script.


7.5.5 Regional digital heritage project: filename and manifest checks
Partner institutions in Southeast Asia contribute image files and metadata manifests. A script checks that every manifest identifier has a file, every file has a manifest row, checksums are present, and filenames conform to an agreed pattern. Unicode text in titles and names is preserved. Transliteration and culturally sensitive description are reviewed by contributing institutions rather than imposed automatically by the aggregator.

Reflect: Identify one repetitive task in an information organization. Which part is mechanical, which part requires contextual judgment, and what evidence would a reviewer need before accepting the output?
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
section: 7.6 Hands-on activity: Build a metadata quality and cleanup report
text:
7.6 Hands-on activity: Build a metadata quality and cleanup report

7.6.1 Purpose
Use R and Quarto to clean predictable formatting issues, flag uncertain records, validate assumptions, and export reviewable outputs. The lab contributes to short labs and can form the data-processing component of the database or API mini-project.


7.6.2 Tools

R 4.x and Quarto;
an editor such as RStudio, Positron, or VS Code with R support;
packages readr, dplyr, and stringr;
a teacher-provided CSV with the fields in Table 7.5;
a project folder containing data/, output/, and the .qmd report.




Table 7.5: Fields and boundaries for the metadata-cleaning lab





Field
Intended rule
Do not automate




record_id
Required, character, unique
Do not renumber silently


title
Required; surrounding/repeated whitespace may be normalized
Do not invent a title


creator
Preserve source form; normalize only whitespace
Do not invert or merge names without authority rules


date_issued
Accept YYYY or valid YYYY-MM-DD; flag other patterns
Do not guess ambiguous dates


language
Map only approved labels or codes
Do not infer language from title alone


rights_uri
Flag missing or non-HTTP(S) values for review
Do not assign a license without evidence


file_name
Required; compare stem with record identifier under the local convention
Do not rename source files before review and backup






Table 7.5 defines both transformation rules and boundaries. The “do not automate” column is part of the specification, not an optional caution.


7.6.3 Steps

Create a project folder with data/ and output/. Place the original CSV in data/ and treat it as read-only.
Create metadata_cleanup.qmd with title, purpose, date, author, and a short data-handling note.
Load readr, dplyr, and stringr. Import all identifier, date, language, rights, and filename fields as character values.
Record the source filename, row count, column names, duplicate identifiers, parsing problems, and missing counts.
Preserve original title, creator, date, language, rights, and filename values in _source columns.
Normalize leading, trailing, and repeated whitespace in title and creator. Do not alter internal punctuation or name order.
Create title_status, date_status, language_status, rights_status, and filename_status fields. Use explicit labels such as OK, REVIEW_MISSING, and REVIEW_PATTERN.
Build an exception table containing the stable identifier, source value, proposed value, status, and review note.
Add assertions: required columns exist, source and output row counts match, identifiers are nonmissing and unique, and no source columns were removed.
Export metadata_cleaned.csv, metadata_exceptions.csv, and run_summary.csv to output/. Never overwrite the source.
Render the Quarto report. Discuss the number and type of changes, unresolved exceptions, limitations, and required human decisions.
Exchange reports with a partner. The partner selects five records, traces source-to-output changes, and records whether each rule behaved as documented.



7.6.4 Expected output
Submit:

metadata_cleanup.qmd and its rendered HTML or DOCX;
metadata_cleaned.csv;
metadata_exceptions.csv;
run_summary.csv;
a data dictionary and rule table;
at least five documented tests;
a peer-verification checklist; and
a 200-word reflection: Which decisions became clearer when you had to express them as code, and which decisions resisted automation?



7.6.5 Lab checklist

The source file remains unchanged.
Identifier-like fields were imported as character data.
Original values are retained beside transformed values.
Rules and non-rules are documented.
Uncertain values are flagged instead of guessed.
Assertions protect required structure and row identity.
Outputs have clear names and separate locations.
At least five cases were traced from source to output.
No unnecessary personal or confidential data appear in the report.
Another student can rerun the workflow from the instructions.



7.6.6 Assessment rubric
Table 7.6 rewards correctness, evidence, and maintainability rather than code length.



Table 7.6: Rubric for the metadata quality and cleanup lab





Criterion
Weight
Strong performance




Problem and rules
20%
Rules, boundaries, inputs, and expected outputs are explicit


Code and data handling
25%
Code is readable and reproducible; source and original values are protected


Validation and tests
20%
Assertions and representative tests detect structural and value problems


Outputs and audit trail
20%
Cleaned data, exceptions, and summary agree and can be traced by identifier


Professional judgment and reflection
15%
Uncertainty, privacy, limitations, maintenance, and human review are addressed
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
section: 7.7 Ethical and professional issue: automation bias and accountable intervention
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
7.8 Review questions

Identification: Distinguish an algorithm, program, script, and automation.
Identification: What is the difference among NA, an empty string, 0, and the text unknown?
Short answer: Why should identifiers such as barcodes and record numbers often be imported as character data?
Short answer: Explain why preserving source columns improves rollback, auditing, and professional review.
Code reading: What values does is.na(c(2020, NA, 2024)) return, and how could the result be used in a quality report?
Applied analysis: Write pseudocode that trims title whitespace, flags blank titles, and leaves nonblank wording unchanged.
Scenario: A date-cleaning script converts 03/04/2025 to 3 April 2025. What assumption was made, and how should ambiguous dates be handled?
Scenario: A batch process completes 97 of 100 files but reports only “success.” What logging and failure-handling changes are needed?
Scenario: A colleague proposes automatically replacing all unmatched language values with eng. Identify the technical, metadata, and ethical problems.
Critical analysis: Choose one LIS task that should use automation only for recommendation or flagging. Explain the human decision that must remain and design one safeguard.
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
section: 7.9 Mini case: The authority cleanup that changed identities
text:
7.9 Mini case: The authority cleanup that changed identities
Maharlika University Library exports 18,000 repository creator strings. A student assistant writes a script that trims spaces, changes all names to title case, splits values at commas, and merges creators sharing the same surname and first initial. The script reduces the apparent number of creators by 22%. It also changes capitalization in several Indigenous and international names, separates a compound family name incorrectly, and merges distinct researchers. Because the cleaned file looks consistent, the repository manager is ready to import it directly. No source-value columns, exception report, or rollback test exist.

7.9.1 Guide questions

Which assumptions in the script are unsafe, and what kinds of identity or discovery harm could follow?
How would you redesign the workflow to preserve evidence, generate candidate matches, and route uncertain cases for authority review?
What tests, approval steps, and rollback evidence must be completed before any production import?
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation
text:
7.10 Further reading and tools

7.10.1 Readings and documentation

R manuals and documentation. The authoritative starting point for the R language, functions, packages, and data structures (R Core Team 2026).
R for Data Science, second edition. A free book on importing, transforming, visualizing, and communicating data (Wickham et al. 2023).
Tidy Data. A concise explanation of how consistent tabular structure supports analysis (Wickham 2014).
Best Practices for Scientific Computing. Practical guidance on readable code, testing, version control, and automation (Wilson et al. 2014).
Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research. Principles for connecting results to recorded inputs and steps (Sandve et al. 2013).
Library Carpentry lessons. Open, library-oriented introductions to data, software, regular expressions, OpenRefine, Git, R, Python, and related tools (Library Carpentry 2026).
Quarto documentation. Guidance for combining prose, executable code, citations, figures, and multi-format publishing (Posit 2026).



7.10.2 Open or widely accessible tools

R: open-source environment for data processing, analysis, and visualization (R Core Team 2026).
RStudio, Positron, or VS Code: editors that support R projects and source files.
readr, dplyr, and stringr: packages for import/export, data transformation, and text processing (Tidyverse Team 2026b, 2026a, 2026c).
OpenRefine: interactive tool for profiling and reconciling messy metadata; useful for comparing scripted and visual workflows.
Git: version-control system for source code and documentation. Sensitive data should remain outside public repositories.
renv: R package for recording and restoring project package dependencies.



7.10.3 Closing synthesis
Programming gives LIS professionals a way to make procedural choices inspectable, repeatable, and testable. Its value is not measured by how many lines of code are written or how many staff actions disappear. A strong script protects source data, states its assumptions, handles missingness, separates predictable transformations from uncertain cases, validates outputs, and leaves evidence another person can review.
Automation is therefore both technical and institutional. It redistributes labor, attention, risk, and authority. The LIS professional must ask not only “Can this be automated?” but “Which rule is being enacted, whose evidence supports it, what happens to exceptions, how will errors be detected and reversed, and who remains accountable?” Chapter 8 extends these foundations from local files to networked data exchange through APIs, JSON, identifiers, linked data, and semantic relationships.




Library Carpentry. 2026. Library Carpentry Lessons. https://librarycarpentry.org/lessons/.


National Privacy Commission. 2023. Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://privacy.gov.ph/data-privacy-act/.


Posit. 2026. Quarto Documentation. https://quarto.org/docs/guide/.


R Core Team. 2026. R: A Language and Environment for Statistical Computing. R Foundation for Statistical Computing. https://www.R-project.org/.


Republic of the Philippines. 2012. Republic Act No. 10173: Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://lawphil.net/statutes/repacts/ra2012/ra_10173_2012.html.


Sandve, Geir Kjetil, Anton Nekrutenko, James Taylor, and Eivind Hovig. 2013. “Ten Simple Rules for Reproducible Computational Research.” PLoS Computational Biology 9 (10): e1003285. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pcbi.1003285.


Tidyverse Team. 2026a. Dplyr: A Grammar of Data Manipulation. https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/.


Tidyverse Team. 2026b. Readr: Read Rectangular Text Data. https://readr.tidyverse.org/.


Tidyverse Team. 2026c. Stringr: Simple, Consistent Wrappers for Common String Operations. https://stringr.tidyverse.org/.


Tidyverse Team. 2026d. Tidyverse Packages. https://www.tidyverse.org/packages/.


Wickham, Hadley. 2014. “Tidy Data.” Journal of Statistical Software 59 (10): 1–23. https://doi.org/10.18637/jss.v059.i10.


Wickham, Hadley, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund. 2023. R for Data Science: Import, Tidy, Transform, Visualize, and Model Data. 2nd ed. O’Reilly Media. https://r4ds.hadley.nz/.


Wilson, Greg, D. A. Aruliah, C. Titus Brown, et al. 2014. “Best Practices for Scientific Computing.” PLoS Biology 12 (1): e1001745. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pbio.1001745.
crumbs: 7  Programming for LIS and Automation

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
8.1 Chapter overview
A reference librarian maintains a subject guide on Philippine children’s literature. Every month, the librarian searches several catalogues, copies titles and authors into a table, checks cover links, and updates availability notes. The guide is useful, but the process is slow and quickly becomes outdated. A public books API could supply structured records, yet its creator names, subjects, editions, and cover images do not always match local cataloguing practice. A linked-data service could connect authors and subjects to stable identifiers, but those links may also import errors or externally imposed descriptions.
The central problem of this chapter is how information systems exchange structured data and meaningful relationships without losing provenance, context, governance, or professional judgment. An application programming interface (API) lets one software system request functions or data from another under a documented contract. Linked data uses web identifiers and explicit statements to connect entities across datasets. The semantic web is the broader vision of web data whose meanings and relationships can be processed across organizational boundaries.
These technologies matter throughout LIS. A catalogue retrieves cover images and availability. A repository exposes metadata for harvesting. A digital exhibit embeds manifests. A reference guide draws from an authority service. A records dashboard combines data from agencies. A discovery layer queries indexes and link resolvers. An archives portal connects people, places, organizations, collections, and events. The technical exchange is only one layer: terms of use, privacy, attribution, rate limits, data quality, sustainability, and community authority determine whether reuse is responsible.
This chapter introduces API requests and responses, endpoints, parameters, authentication, HTTP methods and status codes, JSON and XML, pagination, rate limiting, error handling, identifiers, RDF triples, vocabularies, ontologies, knowledge graphs, JSON-LD, and SPARQL. R examples use httr2 and jsonlite, but the concepts apply across programming languages and platforms (Wickham 2026; Ooms 2026).
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
8.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Describe an API exchange using client, endpoint, method, parameters, headers, status, and response body.
Interpret nested JSON and XML structures and select fields needed for an LIS service.
Construct and document a read-only API request in R with error, pagination, rate-limit, and provenance handling.
Express bibliographic or cultural-heritage relationships as RDF subject-predicate-object triples using stable identifiers.
Read and modify a simple SPARQL SELECT query.
Evaluate data reuse for licensing, privacy, bias, provenance, accessibility, reliability, and sustainability.
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
8.3 Key concepts
Table 8.1 defines the chapter vocabulary. Several concepts belong to different layers: JSON is a syntax, an API is an interface contract, and RDF is a data model.



Table 8.1: Key concepts for APIs and linked data





Term
Working definition




API
A documented interface through which software requests data or operations from other software.


Client
The application that sends a request.


Server
The system that receives a request and returns a response.


Endpoint
A network address representing an API function or resource collection.


Resource
An entity or data object exposed through an interface.


Request
A message asking a service to perform an operation.


Response
A message reporting the outcome and often carrying returned data.


HTTP method
A verb such as GET or POST expressing the requested kind of action.


Parameter
A value that modifies a request, such as query, field, page, or limit.


Header
Metadata accompanying a request or response, such as content type or authorization.


Status code
A three-digit HTTP code summarizing the outcome of a request.


Authentication
The process of establishing the identity of a client or user.


Authorization
The process of deciding what an authenticated identity may do.


API key
A credential identifying or controlling an application that calls an API.


Rate limit
A rule limiting requests within a time period.


Pagination
A method for returning a large result set in smaller parts.


JSON
A text format representing objects, arrays, names, values, numbers, booleans, and nulls.


XML
A markup language representing nested elements, attributes, and text.


Serialization
A concrete syntax used to encode a data model for storage or exchange.


Schema
A formal or documented description of expected structure and constraints.


URI
A uniform resource identifier used to identify a resource.


IRI
An internationalized resource identifier that extends URI characters beyond ASCII.


Persistent identifier
A managed identifier intended to remain stable even when locations or systems change.


RDF
The Resource Description Framework model for statements about resources.


Triple
An RDF statement consisting of subject, predicate, and object.


Literal
A value such as a string, date, number, or language-tagged text rather than another identified resource.


Vocabulary
A managed set of terms used to describe classes, properties, or values.


Ontology
A formal representation of concepts, properties, constraints, and relationships in a domain.


Linked data
Data published with web identifiers and explicit links to related data.


Knowledge graph
A graph of identified entities and typed relationships assembled from one or more sources.


JSON-LD
A JSON serialization designed to express linked data.


SPARQL
A query language and protocol for RDF data.


Provenance
Information about the origin, custody, derivation, and transformations of data.
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
8.4 Core discussion

8.4.1 Interfaces as service contracts
An API is not simply “a URL that returns data.” It is a contract describing what may be requested, how requests are expressed, how responses are structured, what errors mean, which uses are authorized, and how service changes are communicated. Documentation may specify endpoints, parameters, examples, schemas, authentication, rate limits, versions, licensing, and support.
In a graphical interface, a person selects a search field and clicks a button. With an API, software expresses the action directly. This enables repeatable integration, but it removes many visual warnings. A programmer must read the documentation and make limits explicit.
Figure 8.1 shows a simplified read-only request. The server may consult an index or database before producing a response.






sequenceDiagram
    participant C as LIS client script
    participant A as API endpoint
    participant D as Data service
    C->>A: GET /search?q=children&limit=20
    A->>A: Authenticate, validate, and apply limits
    A->>D: Run approved query
    D-->>A: Matching records
    A-->>C: 200 + JSON body + response headers
    C->>C: Validate, transform, log, and attribute




Figure 8.1: A basic API request-response exchange





As Figure 8.1 indicates, a successful status does not guarantee trustworthy data. The client must validate structure, preserve provenance, and check whether the response is complete.


8.4.2 Anatomy of a request
Consider this illustrative URL:
https://openlibrary.org/search.json?q=philippine+folktales&fields=key,title,author_name,first_publish_year&limit=20
It contains:

the scheme https;
the host openlibrary.org;
the endpoint path /search.json;
query parameters after ?;
parameter-value pairs separated by &.

The query value must be URL-encoded. Libraries should use an HTTP client rather than construct complex URLs through manual concatenation. An HTTP client manages encoding, headers, timeouts, and errors more reliably.
HTTP methods have conventional meanings. GET retrieves a representation and should not alter data. POST commonly creates a resource or submits processing. PUT replaces or creates at a known identifier, PATCH applies a partial change, and DELETE requests removal. Actual behavior depends on the API contract. A method that modifies a production catalogue is a consequential action requiring authorization, validation, logging, and usually confirmation.
Table 8.2 lists common status families and reasonable client responses. HTTP semantics are standardized, while each API defines additional error details (Fielding et al. 2022).



Table 8.2: Common HTTP status codes in API work





Code
Meaning
Client action




200
Request succeeded
Validate body and completeness


201
Resource created
Verify location and created representation


204
Succeeded with no response body
Do not attempt to parse a body


400
Invalid request
Correct syntax, parameter, or data


401
Authentication required or failed
Refresh or correct credentials without exposing them


403
Identity lacks permission
Stop; review authorization and policy


404
Resource or endpoint not found
Check identifier, version, or deletion


409
Request conflicts with current state
Retrieve current state and resolve safely


429
Too many requests
Honor retry guidance and reduce rate


500/503
Server or service failure
Retry cautiously later; log and avoid duplicate writes






As Table 8.2 shows, retrying every failure is unsafe. Repeating a malformed request wastes capacity; repeating a write may create duplicates unless the operation is idempotent.


8.4.3 Authentication, authorization, and secrets
Some public APIs allow anonymous reading. Others require an API key, OAuth token, institutional identity, or user authorization. Authentication establishes identity; authorization determines allowed actions. An API key should not be embedded in a published .qmd, committed to Git, printed in logs, or pasted into a public issue.
Credentials can be stored in environment variables and read at runtime:
api_key <- Sys.getenv("BOOKS_API_KEY")
if (api_key == "") stop("BOOKS_API_KEY is not configured")
Environment variables reduce accidental disclosure but do not replace secure secret storage, rotation, restricted permissions, and incident response. A client should request only the scopes it needs. A guide that reads public book data does not need permission to modify records.


8.4.4 JSON: nested objects and arrays
JSON is a text interchange format built from objects, arrays, strings, numbers, booleans, and null (Bray 2017). Objects use name-value pairs; arrays contain ordered values.
{
  "key": "/works/OL123W",
  "title": "Mga Kuwentong Bayan",
  "authors": [
    {"name": "A. Dela Cruz", "id": "/authors/OL45A"}
  ],
  "first_publish_year": 1998,
  "languages": ["fil", "eng"],
  "open_access": true,
  "description": null
}
The top level is an object. authors is an array of objects; languages is an array of strings; open_access is logical; description is explicitly null. A rectangular table cannot represent every nested relationship without transformation. Flattening authors into one string is convenient but may discard identifiers and roles.
In R, jsonlite::fromJSON() parses JSON into R objects (Ooms 2026):
Before flattening, inspect names, types, nesting, optional fields, and nulls. An API may return no author_name, several authors, or different structures across record types.


8.4.5 XML and namespaces
XML represents nested elements, attributes, and text (World Wide Web Consortium 2008). Library standards frequently use XML, including MARCXML, Dublin Core exchanges, OAI-PMH responses, and some authority services.
<record xmlns:dc="http://purl.org/dc/elements/1.1/">
  <dc:identifier>local:123</dc:identifier>
  <dc:title xml:lang="fil">Mga Kuwentong Bayan</dc:title>
  <dc:creator>A. Dela Cruz</dc:creator>
  <dc:language>fil</dc:language>
</record>
The dc prefix is bound to a namespace URI. The prefix itself can change while the namespace identity remains. Parsing XML by searching strings such as <dc:title> is fragile because whitespace, prefixes, attributes, and nesting can vary. Use an XML parser that understands namespaces.
JSON and XML are serializations, not guarantees of semantics. A JSON property named author and an XML element named creator may or may not express the same rule. Interoperability requires documentation, schema mapping, and shared or reconciled vocabularies.
Table 8.3 compares common characteristics without declaring a universal winner.



Table 8.3: Selected characteristics of JSON and XML





Dimension
JSON
XML




Basic structure
Objects, arrays, names, and scalar values
Elements, attributes, text, and ordered nesting


Metadata features
No native namespaces; conventions or JSON-LD contexts add meaning
Namespaces, schemas, mixed content, and attributes


Typical API use
Common in contemporary web APIs
Common in established standards and enterprise exchange


LIS examples
Search results, IIIF manifests, JSON-LD records
MARCXML, OAI-PMH, METS, EAD


Main caution
Nested and optional data can be flattened incorrectly
Namespace-unaware parsing and string manipulation are error-prone






As Table 8.3 demonstrates, format selection follows service requirements, standards, tooling, and partner capacity.


8.4.6 Calling a read-only API in R
The httr2 package provides a structured way to build HTTP requests and process responses (Wickham 2026). The following example is not executed during book rendering because external services can be unavailable or change.
Open Library documents public APIs for searching and retrieving book-related data (Internet Archive 2026). Even when authentication is not required, a client should identify itself appropriately, limit traffic, cache responses when permitted, and follow published terms.
The script should check more than status:
stopifnot(resp_status(response) == 200)
stopifnot(is.list(payload$docs))
stopifnot(length(payload$docs) <= 20)
Do not assume every record contains every field. A safe extraction provides explicit missing values and preserves the source key:
This extraction selects only fields needed for the guide. It does not claim to create a complete bibliographic record. Source identifiers and retrieval dates allow later verification.


8.4.7 Pagination, rate limits, caching, and change
APIs rarely return every matching record in one response. Pagination may use page numbers, offsets, cursors, or continuation tokens. The response may include next, total, or limit information. A client should stop deliberately and detect repeated tokens to avoid an infinite loop.
Rate limits protect shared services. A 429 response often indicates that the client should wait, reduce its request frequency, or consult a response header. Adding many parallel requests can worsen the problem. Caching a permitted response reduces network use and supports reproducibility, but cached data need a retrieval date and refresh policy.
APIs evolve. Fields can be added, renamed, restructured, or deprecated. Versioned endpoints help but do not eliminate change. A sustainable integration requires:

schema and sample-response tests;
timeouts and bounded retries;
logs without secrets or unnecessary personal data;
a cached or saved raw response when licensing permits;
clear failure display rather than stale data presented as current;
an owner who monitors documentation and service notices;
a fallback or exit plan.



8.4.8 From identifiers to linked data
Traditional records often store labels: Manila, Jose Rizal, or Children's literature. Labels are ambiguous and vary by language and time. Linked data uses identifiers for things and properties, while retaining labels for people.
The foundational linked-data guidance recommends using HTTP identifiers, providing useful information when identifiers are looked up, and linking to other identifiers (Berners-Lee 2006). An identifier is not automatically correct or permanent, but it provides a reference that systems can distinguish from a text label.
Persistent identifier systems include DOI for digital objects, ORCID for researchers, and locally governed archival or authority identifiers. Persistence is an organizational promise supported by governance and redirection, not a visual property of a URL (International DOI Foundation 2026).


8.4.9 RDF triples: statements as a graph
RDF represents statements as triples: subject, predicate, object (World Wide Web Consortium 2014). The subject is an identified resource. The predicate identifies a property or relationship. The object is another resource or a literal.
<https://example.edu/work/123>
    <http://purl.org/dc/terms/title>
    "Mga Kuwentong Bayan"@fil .

<https://example.edu/work/123>
    <http://purl.org/dc/terms/creator>
    <https://example.edu/person/45> .
The first object is a Filipino-language literal. The second is another identified resource. Using a creator URI allows further statements about the person without repeating all details in every work record.
Figure 8.2 visualizes a small graph. Arrows are predicates, not unlabeled lines.






flowchart LR
    W["Work URI: /work/123"] -->|"dcterms:title"| T["Mga Kuwentong Bayan @fil"]
    W -->|"dcterms:creator"| P["Person URI: /person/45"]
    W -->|"dcterms:subject"| S["Concept URI: /concept/folktales"]
    W -->|"bf:instanceOf"| I["Instance URI: /instance/789"]
    P -->|"rdfs:label"| N["A. Dela Cruz"]
    S -->|"skos:prefLabel"| L["Folk literature"]




Figure 8.2: A small bibliographic RDF graph





As Figure 8.2 shows, a graph can connect work, instance, person, and concept while keeping labels and relationships distinct. BIBFRAME applies linked-data principles to bibliographic description and distinguishes conceptual and material aspects such as Work, Instance, and Item (Library of Congress 2024).


8.4.10 Vocabularies, ontologies, and application profiles
A vocabulary supplies identified terms; an ontology adds formal expression of classes, properties, relationships, and sometimes constraints. The difference is not always sharp in practice. DCMI Metadata Terms provides properties and classes useful across domains (Dublin Core Metadata Initiative 2020). BIBFRAME and RDA vocabularies express bibliographic entities and relationships (Library of Congress 2024; RDA Steering Committee 2026).
Reusing a vocabulary does not mean using every term. An application profile identifies which terms a service uses, whether they are required or repeatable, their expected value types, and local constraints. Chapter 4’s distinction among element sets, value vocabularies, content rules, and encodings remains essential.
The Library of Congress Linked Data Service publishes authorities and vocabularies as dereferenceable identifiers (Library of Congress 2026). Linking a local subject to an external authority can improve reconciliation, but the local institution remains responsible for whether that concept represents the community appropriately.


8.4.11 JSON-LD: linked data in JSON
JSON-LD expresses RDF-compatible linked data using JSON syntax and a context that maps short terms to identifiers (World Wide Web Consortium 2020).
{
  "@context": {
    "dcterms": "http://purl.org/dc/terms/",
    "title": "dcterms:title",
    "creator": {"@id": "dcterms:creator", "@type": "@id"}
  },
  "@id": "https://example.edu/work/123",
  "title": {"@value": "Mga Kuwentong Bayan", "@language": "fil"},
  "creator": "https://example.edu/person/45"
}
Without the context, title and creator are merely local JSON property names. The context connects them to DCMI properties and declares that the creator value is an identifier rather than ordinary text. Contexts must be governed and preserved; changing a mapping can change meaning without changing the visible short property name.


8.4.12 SPARQL: asking graph-shaped questions
SPARQL queries RDF graphs by matching triple patterns (World Wide Web Consortium 2013). A basic query uses prefixes, selects variables, and specifies patterns in a WHERE block:
PREFIX dcterms: <http://purl.org/dc/terms/>

SELECT ?work ?title
WHERE {
  ?work dcterms:subject <https://example.edu/concept/folktales> ;
        dcterms:title ?title .
  FILTER(LANG(?title) = "fil")
}
LIMIT 20
The semicolon reuses the same subject. The query asks for works associated with one concept and Filipino-language titles. SPARQL can join across relationships without flattening everything into one table. Public endpoints such as the Wikidata Query Service provide environments for learning, subject to service limits and data quality (Wikimedia Foundation 2026).
SPARQL results are not inherently authoritative. They reflect what is present in the graph, which identifiers were linked, and which modeling decisions were made.


8.4.13 APIs, harvesting, and linked data are different tools
Table 8.4 distinguishes several mechanisms encountered in LIS.



Table 8.4: Selected approaches to data exchange and query





Approach
Primary use
Strength
Main caution




REST-style API
Request resources or operations through HTTP
Flexible application integration
Version, limits, authentication, and vendor dependence


OAI-PMH
Harvest repository metadata incrementally
Established repository harvesting protocol
Metadata-only exchange and variable source quality


IIIF Presentation API
Describe and present digital objects and their structure
Interoperable image and audiovisual presentation
Manifest interpretation, rights, and media-service dependencies


SPARQL endpoint
Query RDF graphs by relationship patterns
Graph traversal and semantic joins
Endpoint load, modeling complexity, and uneven links


Bulk data export
Transfer a complete or scheduled dataset snapshot
Efficient reproducible processing at scale
Freshness, secure transfer, storage, and schema drift






As Table 8.4 shows, an API is not automatically more modern or appropriate than harvesting or bulk export. Repositories may expose OAI-PMH for aggregation (Open Archives Initiative 2008). Digital collections may publish IIIF manifests for interoperable presentation (IIIF Consortium 2024). Large research datasets may be more responsibly distributed as versioned bulk files than through millions of API calls.
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
8.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

8.5.1 Academic library: an API-powered subject guide
A Philippine university library builds a guide to disaster-risk-reduction publications. A script queries an open bibliographic API for candidate titles, stores the raw response and retrieval date, and produces a review table. Librarians verify local relevance, editions, creator names, rights, and links before publication. The guide labels external data sources and includes locally catalogued government reports that the API does not cover.


8.5.2 National and local authority work
Libraries and archives may reconcile names of Philippine agencies across reorganizations. A knowledge graph can represent that one agency succeeded another, used an earlier name during a period, and issued particular records. The model should preserve dates, source evidence, and uncertainty. It should not merge organizations only because acronyms match.


8.5.3 Cultural heritage and community identifiers
A regional museum portal links objects to place and community identifiers. External global datasets improve cross-institution discovery but may contain colonial names, disputed boundaries, or categories rejected by communities. The portal preserves local preferred labels, records external matches with provenance and confidence, and allows contributing institutions to reject or revise links.


8.5.4 Public library mobile service
A city library’s mobile page calls an API for branch hours and service alerts. The interface caches the last successful response for low-bandwidth use but clearly displays when it was updated. If the API fails, the page shows a telephone and text-message contact rather than an empty schedule. Operational data are separated from patron account data.


8.5.5 Government open-data integration
A civic information service combines agency datasets. Similar field names such as location, region, and office do not necessarily have the same meaning. The team publishes a data dictionary and transformation log, retains source identifiers, checks licenses, and avoids joining personal records merely because technical identifiers make linkage possible.

Reflect: Name two datasets that could improve a local information service if linked. What identifier would connect them, and what harm could result from an incorrect or excessive link?
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
section: 8.6 Hands-on activity: Build an API-powered subject-guide dataset
text:
8.6 Hands-on activity: Build an API-powered subject-guide dataset

8.6.1 Purpose
Retrieve a small public API response, preserve the raw data, extract selected fields, document provenance and limitations, and produce a review-ready subject-guide table. This lab can complete the database or API mini-project.


8.6.2 Tools

R and Quarto;
packages httr2, jsonlite, dplyr, readr, and knitr;
the official Open Library API documentation (Internet Archive 2026);
a topic connected to an LIS service, such as local history, children’s literature, archives, preservation, media literacy, or disaster information.



8.6.3 Steps

Write a one-sentence audience and information need. Define inclusion and exclusion criteria before requesting data.
Read the API documentation. Record endpoint, parameters, fields, authentication, rate limits, pagination, terms, and attribution requirements.
Design a request for no more than 20 results and only necessary fields. Use GET, a descriptive user agent, HTTPS, and a timeout.
Run the request once. Record retrieval time, final URL without secrets, HTTP status, relevant headers, and the source’s documentation URL.
Save the raw JSON response unchanged as data/raw_response_YYYY-MM-DD.json if the terms permit.
Inspect structure before flattening. Note missing fields, arrays, nested objects, identifiers, and nulls.
Extract source key, title, first creator, first publication year, language values, and one source link. Preserve multiple values or document how they were reduced.
Add local fields: selection_status, selection_reason, verified_by, verified_date, and local_note. Do not present API results as endorsed resources before review.
Validate row count, unique source keys, required titles, plausible years, and valid source URLs. Send anomalies to an exception table.
Produce a review table and a separate five-item publishable table. Include source attribution and retrieval date.
Express one selected book, creator, and topic as at least five RDF-like triples. Distinguish URI objects from literals and add a language tag where appropriate.
Render a Quarto report containing method, request documentation, data dictionary, tables, limitations, ethics, and maintenance plan.



8.6.4 Expected output
Submit:

a reproducible .qmd report and rendered HTML or DOCX;
saved raw response, subject to source terms;
cleaned CSV and exception CSV;
request and response metadata without credentials;
data dictionary and attribution statement;
five RDF-like triples with namespace notes;
five reviewed subject-guide entries; and
a 200-word reflection: What meaning was lost or added when the API response became a local guide?



8.6.5 Lab checklist

The information need and selection criteria precede the request.
Official API documentation and terms were consulted.
The request is bounded, read-only, identified, and reproducible.
Raw data and retrieval metadata are preserved when permitted.
Nested and repeated values are handled explicitly.
External identifiers remain attached to transformed records.
Errors, missing fields, and exceptions are visible.
Results receive human selection and verification.
Attribution and limitations appear in the output.
Credentials and unnecessary personal data are absent.



8.6.6 Assessment rubric
Table 8.5 emphasizes service reasoning and responsible data handling alongside technical execution.



Table 8.5: Rubric for the API-powered subject-guide mini-project





Criterion
Weight
Strong performance




Service question and source
20%
Audience, criteria, API fitness, scope, and terms are justified


Request and error handling
20%
Request is bounded and reproducible; status, limits, timeouts, and failures are handled


Transformation and validation
25%
Raw data and identifiers are preserved; nesting, missingness, and exceptions are explicit


Linked-data modeling
15%
Triples distinguish resources and literals and reuse terms with documented meaning


Documentation, ethics, and sustainability
20%
Attribution, provenance, privacy, bias, accessibility, maintenance, and fallback are addressed
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
section: 8.7 Ethical and professional issue: linkage, consent, and contextual integrity
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
8.8 Review questions

Identification: Distinguish endpoint, parameter, header, status code, and response body.
Identification: What is the difference between authentication and authorization?
Short answer: Why should a client preserve a raw response and retrieval metadata when source terms permit?
Short answer: Explain why JSON, an API, and RDF are not interchangeable terms.
Code reading: In a JSON record, authors is an array of objects. What information might be lost by keeping only the first author name?
Applied analysis: A service returns 429 with a retry header. What should the client do, and what should it avoid doing?
Applied modeling: Write three RDF-like triples connecting a digitized photograph, its photographer, and a depicted place. Identify resources and literals.
Scenario: A public API changes author_name from a string to an array. How should schema tests, error handling, and maintenance respond?
Scenario: A knowledge graph links two people because their romanized names and birth years match. What additional evidence and review are needed?
Critical analysis: When would bulk export be more appropriate than an API or SPARQL endpoint for an LIS project?
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
section: 8.9 Mini case: The regional heritage graph
text:
8.9 Mini case: The regional heritage graph
Five Southeast Asian museums create a shared portal linking objects, makers, places, communities, and historical events. A contractor reconciles names against a global knowledge graph and automatically accepts matches above 90% confidence. The portal gains multilingual labels and external links, but it also merges two artists with similar romanized names, assigns present-day nationalities to people who lived before current borders, exposes the location of a sacred site, and replaces a community-preferred object name with a colonial term. Source institutions cannot see which statements came from them, the contractor, or the external graph.

8.9.1 Guide questions

Which failures involve identity, modeling, provenance, privacy, community authority, and workflow governance?
How should the graph represent competing labels, historical jurisdictions, uncertain matches, and statement-level sources?
Design a correction and approval workflow that preserves useful links without treating automated reconciliation as authoritative.
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web
text:
8.10 Further reading and tools

8.10.1 Readings and standards

HTTP Semantics. The authoritative specification for methods, statuses, headers, and HTTP behavior (Fielding et al. 2022).
RFC 8259. The JSON data interchange specification (Bray 2017).
XML 1.0. The W3C recommendation for XML syntax (World Wide Web Consortium 2008).
RDF 1.1 Concepts and Abstract Syntax. The core RDF data model (World Wide Web Consortium 2014).
SPARQL 1.1 Query Language. The W3C recommendation for querying RDF (World Wide Web Consortium 2013).
JSON-LD 1.1. A JSON-based serialization for linked data (World Wide Web Consortium 2020).
BIBFRAME 2.0 Model. Library of Congress documentation for a bibliographic linked-data model (Library of Congress 2024).
DCMI Metadata Terms. Reusable properties and classes for resource description (Dublin Core Metadata Initiative 2020).



8.10.2 Open or widely accessible tools

Open Library APIs: public book and catalogue-related interfaces for bounded learning projects (Internet Archive 2026).
Library of Congress Linked Data Service: browsable and machine-readable authority and vocabulary data (Library of Congress 2026).
Wikidata Query Service: a public SPARQL learning and exploration environment (Wikimedia Foundation 2026).
httr2 and jsonlite: R packages for HTTP requests and JSON processing (Wickham 2026; Ooms 2026).
Postman, Insomnia, or Bruno: graphical clients for inspecting API requests and responses; do not save secrets in shared workspaces.
RDF playgrounds and validators: useful for checking syntax, but sensitive institutional data should not be pasted into external services.



8.10.3 Closing synthesis
APIs make functions and data addressable across systems. JSON and XML encode responses. Identifiers distinguish resources from labels. RDF expresses statements as graph relationships. Vocabularies contribute shared meaning, JSON-LD carries that meaning in JSON, and SPARQL asks questions across graphs. Each layer solves a different problem.
For LIS professionals, successful integration is not simply receiving a 200 status or producing a dense network visualization. The relevant question is: Can users and maintainers understand where the data came from, what each relationship means, which values were transformed, what the source permits, which people or communities could be harmed, and what happens when the external service changes? Chapter 9 turns from data exchange to the experience of people using these services, emphasizing usability, accessibility, responsive design, and mobile contexts.




Berners-Lee, Tim. 2006. Linked Data—Design Issues. https://www.w3.org/DesignIssues/LinkedData.html.


Bray, Tim. 2017. The JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) Data Interchange Format. RFC No. 8259. Internet Engineering Task Force. https://doi.org/10.17487/RFC8259.


Dublin Core Metadata Initiative. 2020. DCMI Metadata Terms. https://www.dublincore.org/specifications/dublin-core/dcmi-terms/.
