Fielding, Roy T., Mark Nottingham, and Julian Reschke. 2022. HTTP Semantics. RFC No. 9110. Internet Engineering Task Force. https://doi.org/10.17487/RFC9110.


IIIF Consortium. 2024. IIIF Presentation API 3.0. https://iiif.io/api/presentation/3.0/.


International DOI Foundation. 2026. DOI Handbook. https://www.doi.org/the-identifier/resources/handbook.


Internet Archive. 2026. Open Library APIs. https://openlibrary.org/developers/api.


Library of Congress. 2024. BIBFRAME 2.0 Model. https://www.loc.gov/bibframe/docs/bibframe2-model.html.


Library of Congress. 2026. Linked Data Service: Authorities and Vocabularies. https://id.loc.gov/.


National Privacy Commission. 2023. Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://privacy.gov.ph/data-privacy-act/.


Ooms, Jeroen. 2026. Jsonlite: A Simple and Robust JSON Parser and Generator for r. https://jeroen.r-universe.dev/jsonlite/.


Open Archives Initiative. 2008. The Open Archives Initiative Protocol for Metadata Harvesting, Version 2.0. https://www.openarchives.org/OAI/openarchivesprotocol.html.


RDA Steering Committee. 2026. RDA Registry. https://www.rdaregistry.info/.


Republic of the Philippines. 2012. Republic Act No. 10173: Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://lawphil.net/statutes/repacts/ra2012/ra_10173_2012.html.


Wickham, Hadley. 2026. Httr2: Perform HTTP Requests and Process the Responses. https://httr2.r-lib.org/.


Wikimedia Foundation. 2026. Wikidata Query Service User Manual. https://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/Wikidata_Query_Service/User_Manual.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2008. Extensible Markup Language (XML) 1.0, Fifth Edition. https://www.w3.org/TR/xml/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2013. SPARQL 1.1 Query Language. https://www.w3.org/TR/sparql11-query/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2014. RDF 1.1 Concepts and Abstract Syntax. https://www.w3.org/TR/rdf11-concepts/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2020. JSON-LD 1.1: A JSON-Based Serialization for Linked Data. https://www.w3.org/TR/json-ld11/.
crumbs: 8  APIs, Linked Data, and the Semantic Web

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.1 Chapter overview
A student opens the university library discovery service on a low-cost phone while riding home. The search box fits the screen, but a cookie notice covers the search button. The results page loads several megabytes of images. Filters are hidden behind an unlabeled icon. The student finds an article, yet the sign-in form loses entered text when the connection drops. Another student using a keyboard cannot reach the date filter, while a screen-reader user hears five links all announced as “Read more.” The site is technically online, but the service is not equally usable.
The central problem of this chapter is how to design and evaluate information services so that people with varied goals, abilities, devices, languages, connections, and circumstances can complete meaningful tasks with dignity and control. User experience (UX) is not decoration. It includes the complete encounter with a service: discovering that it exists, understanding its scope, navigating it, interpreting metadata, authenticating, obtaining content, recovering from errors, asking for help, and deciding whether to trust the institution.
Accessibility is also not a checklist added after design. It is the requirement that people with disabilities can perceive, operate, understand, and use a service, including through assistive technologies. Mobile design is more than shrinking desktop pages. It requires prioritizing tasks, supporting touch and keyboard interaction, minimizing data and battery costs, tolerating interruption, and making status and recovery visible.
LIS professionals shape UX through metadata labels, search defaults, navigation, content, forms, policies, procurement, support, and assessment. A vendor may write the interface code, but the library remains responsible for the service it offers. This chapter introduces user research, task analysis, information architecture, usability testing, responsive and mobile-first design, plain language, assistive technology, WCAG 2.2, accessibility evaluation, and ethical UX research.
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Distinguish user experience, usability, accessibility, inclusive design, and responsive design.
Construct a realistic task scenario, usability-test protocol, and observation guide for an LIS service.
Evaluate selected pages and workflows against relevant WCAG 2.2 success criteria using manual and automated checks.
Identify barriers involving navigation, metadata labels, content, forms, keyboard use, assistive technology, mobile layout, bandwidth, and error recovery.
Prioritize findings by user impact, frequency, task criticality, reach, and implementation effort.
Recommend a redesign that addresses evidence without collecting unnecessary personal data or excluding affected users.
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.3 Key concepts
Table 9.1 establishes the working vocabulary. The terms overlap, but they answer different questions.



Table 9.1: Key concepts in UX, accessibility, and mobile design





Term
Working definition




User experience
A person's complete experience before, during, and after interacting with a product or service.


Usability
The extent to which specified users achieve specified goals effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily in context.


Accessibility
The quality of enabling people with disabilities to perceive, operate, understand, and robustly use a service.


Inclusive design
A process that includes varied people and edge conditions in design rather than assuming one typical user.


Universal design
Design intended to be usable by the widest practical range of people without separate versions.


User research
Systematic study of users, tasks, contexts, barriers, expectations, and outcomes.


Stakeholder
A person or group affected by or able to affect a service.


Persona
A research-based representation of a user group used to focus design decisions.


Task
A goal-directed activity a person attempts to complete.


Scenario
A concise context and goal given to a participant without prescribing steps.


Journey map
A representation of stages, touchpoints, decisions, emotions, evidence, and failures in a service experience.


Information architecture
The organization, naming, relationships, and navigation of information spaces.


Navigation
Mechanisms that help people know where they are and move to desired content or actions.


Prototype
A representation built to explore or test a design before full implementation.


Usability test
Observation of representative participants attempting realistic tasks with a service or prototype.


Think-aloud
A method in which participants verbalize what they notice, expect, and decide while attempting a task.


Assistive technology
Hardware or software supporting interaction for people with disabilities.


Screen reader
Software that presents interface content through speech, braille, or both.


Keyboard access
The ability to reach and use interactive functions without a pointing device.


Focus indicator
A visible indication of which element currently receives keyboard input.


Semantic HTML
HTML elements used according to their meaning so structure and controls are programmatically available.


Alternative text
A text equivalent communicating the purpose or information of a meaningful image.


Caption
Synchronized text representing speech and important sound in time-based media.


Contrast
A measurable difference in luminance or color that supports perceiving text and interface components.


Plain language
Clear, audience-appropriate communication organized so readers can find and use what they need.


Responsive design
An approach in which layout and components adapt to viewport and device conditions.


Mobile first
A design approach beginning with essential tasks and constrained screens before enhancing larger contexts.


Breakpoint
A condition at which responsive presentation rules change.


Progressive enhancement
Providing a reliable core experience first, then adding supported enhancements.


WCAG
The Web Content Accessibility Guidelines published by W3C.


Success criterion
A testable WCAG requirement at Level A, AA, or AAA.


Conformance
A claim that identified content meets defined requirements at a stated WCAG level and version.


Severity
A reasoned rating of a problem's impact on users and tasks.
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.4 Core discussion

9.4.1 UX is the service, not only the screen
The user interface is the visible and interactive part of a service. UX includes much more. A catalogue search may be easy, but the overall experience fails if holdings are stale, call-number labels are unclear, an elevator is inaccessible, or staff cannot explain an authentication error. A repository may meet technical standards while deposit instructions frighten authors with legal language. A digital exhibit may be visually elegant but omit transcription and provenance.
Usability commonly examines whether intended users can complete tasks effectively, efficiently, and satisfactorily in context. Accessibility examines whether people with disabilities can participate and whether the technology works with varied ways of perceiving and operating it. Inclusive design attends to human diversity and exclusion throughout the design process. These qualities reinforce one another, but one cannot substitute for another. A quick task completion rate does not prove screen-reader access; passing an automated accessibility scan does not prove understandable navigation.
Table 9.2 distinguishes the evidence sought by related practices.



Table 9.2: Questions and evidence in UX-related evaluation





Practice
Central question
Evidence
Limitation




UX research
Who is trying to accomplish what, in which context, and why?
Interviews, observation, diaries, contextual inquiry, support records
Small samples require careful interpretation


Usability evaluation
Can representative users complete defined tasks with acceptable effort and confidence?
Task success, paths, errors, time, comments, observation
A task and participant set cannot represent everyone


Accessibility evaluation
Can people with disabilities perceive, operate, understand, and robustly use the service?
Standards review, keyboard test, assistive-technology test, disabled-user testing
Automated tools and standards checks cannot replace lived experience


Analytics
What aggregate patterns of use and failure appear in instrumented events?
Privacy-protected counts, funnels, search patterns, performance measures
Observed events do not explain motivation and may exclude nonusers


Service assessment
Does the service produce equitable, mission-aligned outcomes worth sustaining?
Mixed methods, outcomes, costs, reach, equity, policy evidence
Broad outcomes can be difficult to attribute to one interface






As Table 9.2 shows, methods answer different questions. Combining them produces a stronger account than declaring that one score represents “the user.” Library usability studies demonstrate the value of observing actual users rather than relying only on professional expectations (Emde et al. 2009; Vargas 2026).


9.4.2 The evidence-based design cycle
UX work is iterative. Teams frame a service problem, study contexts, model tasks, prototype, evaluate, prioritize, implement, and monitor. Results often reveal that the original problem was incomplete.






flowchart LR
    F["Frame service problem and equity goal"] --> R["Research users, contexts, tasks, and barriers"]
    R --> M["Model journeys and information architecture"]
    M --> P["Prototype content and interaction"]
    P --> E["Evaluate usability and accessibility"]
    E --> Q["Prioritize by impact, reach, evidence, and effort"]
    Q --> I["Implement and document"]
    I --> O["Monitor outcomes and new exclusion"]
    O --> F




Figure 9.1: An iterative, evidence-based UX and accessibility cycle





As Figure 9.1 indicates, accessibility begins during framing and research. If disabled users are invited only after procurement, the team may discover barriers it cannot contractually or technically correct.


9.4.3 Researching people without inventing a typical user
User research begins with a question, not a favored method. If students cannot find reserve readings, observation and task testing may reveal navigation barriers. If rural users rarely access the repository, interviews may reveal data cost, device sharing, language, or trust issues invisible in analytics.
Useful methods include:

interviews for goals, expectations, language, workarounds, and meaning;
contextual inquiry for observing tasks in their actual setting;
diary studies for experiences distributed over time;
surveys for structured self-report across a larger group;
analytics for aggregate paths and events, with privacy controls;
support-log review for recurrent questions and failures;
participatory design for developing ideas with affected people;
usability testing for observing task performance.

A persona is useful only when based on evidence and used to focus decisions. A fictional “Maria, age 19, digital native” can hide more than it reveals. Relevant dimensions might include first-language reading, assistive-technology use, research experience, device access, prepaid data, shared-device privacy, and time pressure. Do not turn demographic stereotypes into design requirements.
Recruitment should include people affected by likely barriers. Offering only an inaccessible online sign-up form contradicts inclusive research. Compensation, transport, remote access, language choice, assistive technology, breaks, and consent format may be necessary participation supports.


9.4.4 Tasks and scenarios
A task is a goal, not a series of interface instructions. Compare:

weak: “Click Databases, choose A-Z, and search Academic Search.”
stronger: “You need two recent peer-reviewed articles about coastal community archives for a paper due tomorrow. Find one article you can access and show how you would save its citation.”

The stronger scenario supplies context and success evidence without teaching the interface. A test protocol should define starting state, task, success criteria, maximum time if relevant, permitted assistance, observation fields, and closing questions.
Tasks should represent critical and risky journeys, not only the homepage. Examples include:

find today’s branch hours and confirm an unexpected closure;
locate a known book and determine where and when it is available;
discover an article and obtain accessible full text;
request an archival appointment without exposing sensitive research interests;
deposit a thesis and understand license and embargo choices;
recover from a failed login or interrupted form.



9.4.5 Information architecture and metadata language
Information architecture concerns how content and functions are grouped, labelled, related, and navigated. Library interfaces inherit professional structures that may not match public language. OPAC, e-resources, serials, vertical file, finding aid, and institutional repository can be accurate terms but require context or alternatives for novice users.
Metadata choices become interface choices. Facet names, format labels, availability states, and relationship displays determine what users can understand. A facet labelled Electronic resource may combine e-books, streamed video, datasets, and web pages. A record labelled Available may still require campus authentication. UX work therefore extends into cataloguing, knowledge-base maintenance, and systems configuration.
Card sorting can investigate how users group content. Tree testing can examine whether a proposed hierarchy supports finding. Neither method determines institutional policy by majority vote. Professional, legal, archival, or cultural requirements may justify structures that need explanation rather than removal.


9.4.6 Plain language and multilingual content
Plain language helps readers find, understand, and use information (Plain Language Action and Information Network 2026). It involves organization, headings, familiar words, short focused sentences, explicit actions, and useful error messages. It does not mean deleting necessary technical precision.
Compare:

Authentication unsuccessful.

with:

We could not sign you in. If you are off campus, start from the library’s database page and choose your institution. Your search terms have been kept. Contact the library if the problem continues.

The second message states what happened, suggests recovery, and reassures the user about lost work. It should also expose a technical reference code for support without requiring users to interpret it.
Translation requires more than replacing strings. Navigation labels may expand, date and address formats differ, and professional terms may lack direct equivalents. Interfaces should identify language changes, preserve page language metadata, and avoid mixing untranslated controls into translated content. Local language testing is necessary because an English layout cannot predict every result.


9.4.7 Accessibility and disability
Web accessibility supports people with visual, auditory, physical, speech, cognitive, language, learning, and neurological disabilities (World Wide Web Consortium 2026c). Disability may be permanent, temporary, or situational. Captions help Deaf users and also people in noisy spaces. Large touch targets help users with limited dexterity and people standing in a moving vehicle. Clear instructions help cognitive access and novice users.
Assistive technologies include screen readers, magnifiers, refreshable braille displays, switch devices, speech input, alternative keyboards, and system-level settings. People also use browser zoom, reader modes, increased text size, high contrast, reduced motion, captions, and keyboard navigation.
Semantic HTML provides structure and behavior to browsers and assistive technologies. Native headings, links, buttons, lists, tables, labels, and landmarks should be used before custom components. ARIA can add accessibility semantics when needed, but incorrect ARIA can make an interface less usable. The WAI-ARIA Authoring Practices Guide documents patterns and keyboard behavior for complex widgets (World Wide Web Consortium 2026a).


9.4.8 WCAG 2.2 and POUR
WCAG 2.2 organizes guidance under four principles: perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust (World Wide Web Consortium 2023). Success criteria have conformance levels A, AA, and AAA. Many policies target Level AA, but legal and institutional requirements must be verified locally.
Table 9.3 connects selected concerns to LIS services. It is an introduction, not a conformance checklist.



Table 9.3: WCAG principles applied to library services





Principle
Question
LIS example
Common failure




Perceivable
Can users perceive content through different senses or alternatives?
Alt text for exhibit images; captions and transcripts; sufficient contrast; reflowed text
Scanned PDF has no text layer or reading order


Operable
Can users operate controls with varied input methods and enough time?
Keyboard-accessible facets; visible focus; skip links; adequate targets; no keyboard trap
Date picker or modal cannot be completed by keyboard


Understandable
Can users understand content, navigation, status, and errors?
Clear availability labels; consistent navigation; identified language; useful form errors
Session expires without warning and deletes a request


Robust
Can current and future user agents and assistive technologies interpret the service?
Valid semantic elements; programmatic labels, names, roles, values, and status messages
Custom control looks like a button but exposes no button role






As Table 9.3 shows, accessibility spans content, code, and workflow. A perfectly labelled download link still fails if the linked PDF is inaccessible.
Selected checks for an introductory review include:

meaningful page title and one clear top-level heading;
logical heading hierarchy;
meaningful images with appropriate alt text and decorative images ignored;
text and interface contrast;
page usable at 200% zoom and narrow reflow without loss;
every interactive element reachable and operable by keyboard;
visible focus and logical focus order;
descriptive link names rather than repeated click here;
explicit form labels, instructions, required states, and error recovery;
captions and transcripts for media;
page language identified;
status messages exposed without unexpectedly moving focus.

W3C provides preliminary “easy checks” and tutorials, while warning that a quick review cannot determine full conformance (World Wide Web Consortium 2026b, 2026d).


9.4.9 Alternative text and complex cultural objects
Alt text communicates an image’s purpose in context. The same image may need different treatment in a search result, decorative banner, exhibit, or scholarly analysis. image.jpg and “picture of an image” are not useful. A decorative flourish may use empty alt text. A photograph central to an exhibit needs concise identifying information, while a complex map may need a short alternative plus a longer description or accessible data.
Alt text does not replace cataloguing metadata, captions, transcriptions, or scholarly description. A caption identifies and contextualizes an object for all users. Alt text supports the image’s function and information when it is not seen. Cultural sensitivity also matters: description should not expose restricted knowledge or treat people as scenery.


9.4.10 Keyboard access and focus
Keyboard testing is one of the most productive manual checks. Starting at the address bar, use Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, arrow keys where a documented widget pattern requires them, and Escape for dismissible overlays. Observe:

Can every interactive item be reached?
Is the focus indicator visible?
Does focus order follow meaning?
Can menus, filters, modals, and date pickers be operated?
Does focus become trapped or disappear?
When a modal closes, does focus return logically?

Do not remove browser focus outlines without providing an accessible replacement. Visual order created with CSS should not contradict keyboard or reading order.


9.4.11 Accessible forms and authentication
Library forms collect searches, requests, deposits, appointments, and personal data. A placeholder is not a durable label; it disappears when users type and may have low contrast. Native HTML labels and controls provide strong defaults (Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group 2026).
Good forms:

ask only necessary information;
group related fields with meaningful legends;
explain why sensitive information is needed;
identify required fields in text and programmatically;
provide input purpose and appropriate autocomplete where safe;
preserve valid input after errors;
place error summaries and field errors where users can find them;
avoid short timeouts or warn and allow extension;
provide confirmation and correction for consequential submissions.

Authentication deserves end-to-end testing. A library homepage may be accessible while a vendor sign-in, CAPTCHA, multifactor prompt, or proxy error blocks access. Procurement and incident reporting must cover third-party components.


9.4.12 Responsive and mobile-first services
Responsive design adapts layout and presentation to available space. Mobile-first design begins with the core content and tasks under constraint, then progressively enhances larger screens. It should not mean removing essential services from mobile users.






flowchart TB
    U["User goal: find and access one resource"] --> D["Device and input"]
    U --> C["Connection, data cost, and interruption"]
    U --> A["Ability, assistive technology, and settings"]
    U --> L["Language, literacy, and domain knowledge"]
    U --> P["Privacy and shared-device context"]
    D --> X["Responsive, resilient service"]
    C --> X
    A --> X
    L --> X
    P --> X
    X --> O["Search, understand, access, recover, and get help"]




Figure 9.2: A mobile library task depends on more than viewport width





Figure 9.2 emphasizes that mobile context is not a screen-size category. A desktop user may have slow connectivity; a phone user may connect a keyboard; a shared device changes privacy needs.
Mobile service design should consider:

prioritize core tasks and content before institutional promotion;
use responsive images and avoid autoplay media;
minimize scripts, fonts, trackers, and unnecessary network requests;
make touch targets adequately sized and spaced;
support portrait and landscape orientation unless essential;
avoid horizontal scrolling for ordinary content;
allow browser zoom and text resizing;
keep labels visible and controls close to their purpose;
preserve entered data across brief interruptions;
show loading, offline, stale, and retry states clearly;
provide low-bandwidth alternatives and contact channels.

W3C mobile guidance connects small screens, limited input, bandwidth, and device diversity to web design (World Wide Web Consortium 2008). Mobile accessibility guidance further explains that accessibility requirements apply across mobile web and application contexts (World Wide Web Consortium 2015).


9.4.13 Performance is an access issue
A large page consumes time, data, battery, and attention. Performance barriers disproportionately affect users with older devices, prepaid data, intermittent networks, and assistive technologies processing complex pages. Measure actual transferred bytes, request counts, and loading behavior on throttled connections rather than assuming institutional Wi-Fi.
The fastest component is one not sent. Compress images, use appropriate dimensions, lazy-load noncritical media, limit third-party scripts, cache stable assets, and avoid forcing users to download large PDFs before learning their size and format. Performance optimization should not remove text alternatives, labels, or error messages.


9.4.14 Testing methods: no single tool is enough
Automated accessibility tools can detect missing labels, some contrast failures, duplicate IDs, and structural issues. They cannot decide whether alt text communicates purpose, heading structure makes sense, an error is understandable, or a workflow is dignified. Manual keyboard, zoom, reflow, content, and assistive-technology testing remain necessary. Disabled users’ evaluation adds evidence that standards checks cannot supply.
Table 9.4 maps methods to strengths and limits.



Table 9.4: Complementary UX and accessibility evaluation methods





Method
Best at
Cannot alone establish




Automated accessibility scan
Rapidly detecting machine-testable patterns
Full WCAG conformance or usable experience


Manual standards review
Interpreting selected success criteria in context
Representative user success


Keyboard and zoom test
Finding focus, order, reflow, and operation barriers
All assistive-technology behavior


Screen-reader test
Experiencing programmatic structure, names, roles, and announcements
Experience of every screen-reader user or disability


Task-based usability test
Observing expectations, paths, errors, recovery, and confidence
Standards conformance or population prevalence


Performance and network test
Measuring load, transfer, and behavior under constraints
Content clarity or task relevance


Analytics and support review
Finding aggregate patterns and recurrent service incidents
Motivation, nonuser exclusion, or causation






As Table 9.4 demonstrates, triangulation matters. A finding becomes more actionable when observation, standards evidence, and service data point to the same barrier.


9.4.15 Prioritizing findings
Teams cannot fix everything at once, but prioritization must not reduce accessibility to popularity. A barrier affecting fewer users may completely block a legally or professionally essential service.
Consider:

impact: inconvenience, delay, serious error, or complete block;
task criticality: optional exploration versus required access or submission;
reach: how many people or workflows encounter it;
frequency: how often it occurs;
equity: whether it disproportionately excludes a group;
evidence confidence: strength and sources of evidence;
effort and dependency: complexity, vendor control, and time;
risk: privacy, legal, security, rights, or reputational consequence.

Table 9.5 offers a simple scale. Severity describes user impact, not developer difficulty.



Table 9.5: A user-impact severity scale for findings





Severity
User effect
Example
Response




Critical
Blocks an essential task with no reasonable accessible alternative
Keyboard user cannot submit an archival appointment request
Stop or provide immediate accessible path; escalate


Major
Causes likely failure, serious error, exclusion, or dependence on assistance
Screen reader receives unlabeled search fields and cannot distinguish them
Prioritize in current improvement cycle


Moderate
Creates delay, confusion, repeated effort, or avoidable uncertainty
Mobile filter resets after rotating or returning from a record
Schedule with evidence and owner


Minor
Creates friction but task remains understandable and achievable
Link wording is vague but surrounding context identifies the destination
Repair when adjacent work occurs; monitor






As Table 9.5 indicates, an accessibility block is not downgraded because only one participant encountered it. Document the affected task, evidence, relevant criterion, recommended outcome, owner, and verification method.
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

9.5.1 Academic library: off-campus article access
A Philippine university tests article access with students using prepaid mobile data. The discovery page performs adequately, but three vendor redirects, institutional sign-in, and a large PDF create failure. The redesign explains the path, retains citation details, offers an HTML or smaller-file option when available, displays file size, and provides a request link when access fails. The library includes vendor accessibility and performance in renewal review.


9.5.2 Public library: multilingual branch information
A city public library publishes branch hours, closures, events, and contact details in Filipino, English, and a locally used language. Residents help test labels and directions. Essential information appears as text rather than posters alone. Each poster has an accessible text equivalent, while emergency closures can be reached through a low-bandwidth page and SMS-compatible contact route.


9.5.3 School library: shared devices and privacy
Students use shared tablets. The catalogue avoids displaying borrowing history on the landing page, provides a clear sign-out action, and does not save searches by default. Text can be resized, controls work with a connected keyboard, and reading-list pages avoid motion and distracting autoplay. Teachers and students with disabilities participate in testing.


9.5.4 Government information service: accessible forms
A government records request form uses visible labels, clear purpose statements, appropriate input types, an error summary, preserved entries, and a confirmation page. Required identity information is explained and minimized. A downloadable form is not offered only as an inaccessible scanned PDF; an assisted offline channel remains available.


9.5.5 Regional cultural heritage exhibit
A Southeast Asian digital exhibit contains multilingual object labels, transcripts for oral histories, captions for video, alt text for meaningful images, and long descriptions for maps. Community advisors determine whether certain images or knowledge should have restricted access. Responsive image sizes reduce data cost without reducing descriptive access.

Reflect: Think of the last library or government service you used on a phone. Which barrier came from interface design, which from organizational policy, and which from an external provider?
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
section: 9.6 Hands-on activity: Conduct a mobile UX and accessibility critique
text:
9.6 Hands-on activity: Conduct a mobile UX and accessibility critique

9.6.1 Purpose
Evaluate one meaningful library task on desktop and mobile using task observation, manual accessibility checks, and a limited automated scan. Produce prioritized findings and a redesign sketch. This activity is the UX or discovery critique worth 10% of the course assessment.


9.6.2 Tools

one public library, academic library, school library, repository, archive, museum, or government information website;
desktop and mobile-sized browser views or two devices;
keyboard only for one test pass;
browser zoom and text-resize controls;
one automated accessibility checker approved by the instructor;
performance information from browser developer tools when available;
observation sheet based on Table 9.6.




Table 9.6: Fields for the mobile UX and accessibility observation log





Field
Record




Task and success evidence
Goal, starting point, completion evidence


Participant and context
Relevant experience only; avoid unnecessary identity data


Device and viewport
Device class, input, orientation, zoom


Connection context
Normal or throttled; do not collect network identifiers


Observed step
Action and interface response


Expectation or comment
Quote or concise paraphrase with consent


Barrier and recovery
What prevented progress and whether recovery succeeded


Accessibility evidence
Criterion, keyboard behavior, semantics, contrast, or tool evidence


Severity
Critical, major, moderate, or minor with rationale


Recommendation
Desired outcome, not merely a preferred visual style


Owner and verification
Responsible team and retest method






Table 9.6 keeps observations separate from interpretations and recommendations.


9.6.3 Steps

Select a service and one critical task, such as finding an available item, accessing an article, requesting archival material, or locating branch information.
Write a realistic scenario and observable success criteria. Do not include click-by-click instructions.
Define the test scope, participant consent, recording method, data retention, and stopping rule. Do not test a sensitive personal topic.
Conduct a pilot yourself. Correct unclear instructions before involving a peer.
Ask one peer participant to attempt the task on a mobile viewport while thinking aloud. Remind the participant that the service is being tested, not the person.
Observe without teaching. If help is requested, record when and what was provided.
Repeat the task independently on a desktop viewport and compare content, order, labels, and functionality.
Test the essential path by keyboard. Record reachability, visible focus, focus order, overlays, menus, filters, forms, and recovery.
Review at 200% zoom and at a narrow viewport. Check reflow, text size, orientation, horizontal scrolling, and hidden content.
Inspect page title, headings, link names, form labels, meaningful images, media alternatives, language, errors, and status messages.
Run one automated checker. Verify each reported issue manually and state what the tool did not test.
Inspect page transfer size or loading behavior on a throttled connection when tools permit. Record large blockers and failure states.
Group observations into findings. Assign severity using user effect, task criticality, reach, equity, and evidence.
Create one annotated redesign sketch for the highest-priority finding. Include keyboard, screen-reader, mobile, error, and low-bandwidth behavior as relevant.
Write a concise recommendation identifying owner, dependency, expected outcome, and verification method.



9.6.4 Expected output
Submit:

test protocol and consent script;
participant and evaluator observation notes with unnecessary personal data removed;
annotated desktop and mobile screens;
keyboard, zoom, reflow, content, and automated-check results;
five to eight findings ranked by severity;
one redesigned task flow or wireframe;
an 800-word evidence-based critique; and
a 150-word reflection: Which barrier would professional staff have missed without observing a user or testing a different mode of access?



9.6.5 Lab checklist

The task represents a meaningful service goal.
Success criteria are observable and not tied to one path.
Consent and data minimization are documented.
Mobile and desktop experiences are compared.
Keyboard, zoom, reflow, and content checks are manual.
Automated results are verified and not treated as full conformance.
Findings distinguish evidence, interpretation, and recommendation.
Severity is based on user and task impact.
Redesign notes include accessible behavior, not only appearance.
Each priority finding has an owner and retest method.



9.6.6 Assessment rubric
Table 9.7 rewards evidence, inclusion, and actionable analysis.



Table 9.7: Rubric for the mobile UX and accessibility critique





Criterion
Weight
Strong performance




Task and protocol
15%
Scenario, success criteria, scope, and method align with a real service need


Observation and evidence
25%
Notes separate observed behavior from interpretation and triangulate evidence


Accessibility and mobile analysis
25%
Manual and automated methods address POUR, keyboard, reflow, content, forms, and connection constraints


Prioritization and redesign
20%
Severity reflects impact; redesign addresses cause and includes a verification plan


Ethics and communication
15%
Consent, privacy, respectful facilitation, limitations, and accessible reporting are clear
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
section: 9.7 Ethical and professional issue: surveillance, dark patterns, and digital exclusion
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.8 Review questions

Identification: Distinguish UX, usability, accessibility, inclusive design, and responsive design.
Identification: What are WCAG’s four POUR principles?
Short answer: Why can neither an automated scan nor one usability test establish that a service is accessible?
Short answer: Explain how metadata labels and availability statements affect UX.
Applied design: Rewrite “Click Advanced Search and select Format: Serial” as a realistic task scenario.
Applied accessibility: A filter opens with a mouse but cannot receive keyboard focus. Identify the user effect, likely WCAG principle, severity, and retest.
Scenario: A mobile form clears all entries after a brief connection loss. Recommend content, technical, and recovery changes.
Scenario: A library’s translated site retains English error messages and unlabeled icons. Why is translation alone insufficient?
Scenario: Analytics show few screen-reader users. Explain why this is not evidence that accessibility work has low priority.
Critical analysis: When might simplifying a service for mobile become harmful removal rather than useful prioritization?
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.9 Mini case: The one-box redesign
Sampaguita University launches a new discovery homepage built around one large search box. Senior managers praise its clean appearance. Mobile analytics show fewer searches ending in full-text access, but the vendor dashboard records only page views and clicks. Student testing reveals that filters are hidden after zoom, the sign-in prompt appears only after users select an article, and failed authentication deletes the query. A blind student reports repeated unlabeled “View” links. A student using prepaid data abandons pages with large promotional images. Librarians suggest adding instructions everywhere; the vendor proposes collecting session recordings to learn more.

9.9.1 Guide questions

Which usability, accessibility, mobile, metadata, authentication, performance, analytics, and privacy problems are present?
What mixed-method evaluation would produce useful evidence without collecting passwords, queries, or unnecessary personal data?
Prioritize three interventions, identify responsible owners, and specify how each would be verified with affected users.
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services
text:
9.10 Further reading and tools

9.10.1 Readings and standards

Web Content Accessibility Guidelines 2.2. The central W3C accessibility recommendation used in this chapter (World Wide Web Consortium 2023).
Introduction to Web Accessibility. A concise W3C overview of disability, barriers, and benefits (World Wide Web Consortium 2026c).
Easy Checks. A guided preliminary review that explicitly does not establish full conformance (World Wide Web Consortium 2026b).
WAI Tutorials. Practical guidance on page structure, menus, images, tables, forms, and carousels (World Wide Web Consortium 2026d).
ARIA Authoring Practices Guide. Patterns and keyboard interaction for complex web widgets (World Wide Web Consortium 2026a).
Mobile accessibility guidance. W3C discussion connecting accessibility guidelines to mobile contexts (World Wide Web Consortium 2015).
Library usability studies. Examples of applying usability and accessibility inquiry in academic library websites (Billingham 2014; Emde et al. 2009; Vargas 2026).



9.10.2 Open or widely accessible tools

Browser keyboard, zoom, responsive view, and developer tools: essential manual testing capabilities.
WAVE, axe DevTools, Accessibility Insights, or Lighthouse: automated support for finding selected issues; none proves conformance.
NVDA: free Windows screen reader. Testing requires learning expected screen-reader interaction rather than treating speech output as a simple scanner.
Colour Contrast Analyser: desktop tool for testing text and graphical contrast.
WebPageTest or browser network throttling: tools for examining performance under constrained connections; do not submit private URLs or data to public services.
Penpot, diagrams.net, paper, or HTML prototypes: accessible ways to communicate redesigns when accompanied by behavior notes and text alternatives.



9.10.3 Closing synthesis
UX connects institutional systems to lived experience. Accessibility asks whether people with disabilities can participate independently and effectively. Mobile design exposes assumptions about devices, bandwidth, privacy, interruption, and attention. These are not separate finishing tasks: they shape metadata labels, content, authentication, vendor selection, help, assessment, and policy.
An attractive interface can still exclude. A standards-conformant page can still confuse. A fast mobile page can still manipulate consent. LIS professionals therefore need multiple forms of evidence and sustained participation by affected users. The governing question is: Who can complete which information task, under what conditions, with what effort and dignity, and who is missing from the evidence? Chapter 10 extends this lifecycle responsibility from immediate access to long-term digital continuity, preservation, storage, and cloud services.




Billingham, Lisa. 2014. “Improving Academic Library Website Accessibility for People with Disabilities.” Library Management 35 (8/9): 565–81. https://doi.org/10.1108/LM-11-2013-0107.


Emde, Judith Z., Sara E. Morris, and Monica Claassen-Wilson. 2009. “Testing an Academic Library Website for Usability with Faculty and Graduate Students.” Evidence Based Library and Information Practice 4 (3): 24–36. https://doi.org/10.18438/B8TK7Q.


National Privacy Commission. 2023. Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://privacy.gov.ph/data-privacy-act/.


Plain Language Action and Information Network. 2026. Federal Plain Language Guidelines. https://www.plainlanguage.gov/guidelines/.


Republic of the Philippines. 2012. Republic Act No. 10173: Data Privacy Act of 2012. https://lawphil.net/statutes/repacts/ra2012/ra_10173_2012.html.


Vargas, Isabel. 2026. “Elements, Structure, and Aesthetics: Improving Usability and Accessibility on a Library Website Homepage.” Weave: Journal of Library User Experience, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.3998/weaveux.7583.


Web Hypertext Application Technology Working Group. 2026. HTML Living Standard: Forms. https://html.spec.whatwg.org/multipage/forms.html.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2008. Mobile Web Best Practices 1.0. https://www.w3.org/TR/mobile-bp/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2015. Mobile Accessibility: How WCAG 2.0 and Other W3C/WAI Guidelines Apply to Mobile. W3C. https://www.w3.org/TR/mobile-accessibility-mapping/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2023. Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG) 2.2. https://www.w3.org/TR/WCAG22/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2026a. ARIA Authoring Practices Guide. https://www.w3.org/WAI/ARIA/apg/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2026b. Easy Checks—a First Review of Web Accessibility. https://www.w3.org/WAI/test-evaluate/preliminary/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2026c. Introduction to Web Accessibility. https://www.w3.org/WAI/fundamentals/accessibility-intro/.


World Wide Web Consortium. 2026d. Web Accessibility Tutorials. https://www.w3.org/WAI/tutorials/.
crumbs: 9  UX, Accessibility, and Mobile Library Services

title: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
text:
10.1 Chapter overview
A provincial archive digitizes photographs and stores the master TIFF files on an external drive. Staff also upload JPEG copies to a cloud folder and describe the collection in a spreadsheet. Five years later, the drive still turns on, but several folders were renamed, the spreadsheet no longer matches the files, one cloud account belongs to a former employee, and no one has checked whether the copies are identical. The institution has storage and copies, yet it cannot demonstrate that the collection is complete, authentic, understandable, recoverable, or sustainable.
The central problem of this chapter is how to keep digital information understandable, trustworthy, findable, and usable for as long as it is needed despite technological, organizational, financial, legal, and environmental change. Digital preservation is not a one-time technical treatment. It is an ongoing service combining selection, policy, metadata, file management, storage, monitoring, security, rights, and planned action.
Preservation must be distinguished from digitization, storage, synchronization, and backup. Digitization creates digital representations. Storage holds bits. Synchronization makes locations reflect changes, including accidental deletion. Backup enables recovery from selected failures. Preservation coordinates these functions with identity, context, authenticity, access, and long-term responsibility.
Cloud services can contribute geographic separation, scalable storage, managed infrastructure, and automated controls. They do not transfer accountability away from the library or archive. Cost, connectivity, encryption, identity management, jurisdiction, service changes, data egress, environmental impact, and exit planning remain institutional concerns.
This chapter introduces digital objects, bitstreams, fixity, checksums, integrity, authenticity, redundancy, the 3-2-1 heuristic, file-format risk, migration, emulation, preservation metadata, OAIS information packages, storage tiers, cloud service and deployment models, service-level agreements, shared responsibility, vendor lock-in, and recovery testing.
crumbs: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services

title: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
text:
10.2 Learning objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

Distinguish digitization, storage, synchronization, backup, disaster recovery, and digital preservation.
Identify and assess technological, environmental, human, organizational, legal, and vendor threats to digital continuity.
Generate, record, verify, and interpret SHA-256 checksums for a small preservation package.
Propose a basic redundant-storage, monitoring, and recovery plan using the 3-2-1 heuristic critically.
Compare preservation strategies and cloud options using format, cost, control, security, privacy, jurisdiction, sustainability, and exit criteria.
Document preservation objects, events, agents, rights, and outcomes in a reviewable action plan.
crumbs: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services

title: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
text:
10.3 Key concepts
Table 10.1 provides the working vocabulary. The distinctions matter because each term supports a different control.



Table 10.1: Key concepts in digital preservation and cloud services





Term
Working definition




Digital preservation
Managed activities that maintain access to authentic digital information for as long as required.


Digital object
Content together with the metadata and relationships needed to identify, manage, render, and understand it.


Bitstream
A sequence of bits considered as a unit, whether or not stored as a standalone file.


File
A named sequence of bytes represented within a file system or storage service.


Storage
Infrastructure and services that hold data.


Backup
A recoverable copy made according to a defined schedule and retention policy.


Synchronization
Propagation of changes among locations, which may also propagate corruption or deletion.


Disaster recovery
Plans and capabilities for restoring systems and services after disruption.


Fixity
The property of remaining unchanged at the bit level, assessed against a recorded value.


Checksum
A compact value calculated from data and later recalculated to detect change.


Hash function
An algorithm mapping data of arbitrary size to a fixed-length value.


Integrity
The quality of being complete and unaltered except through authorized, documented action.


Authenticity
The quality of being what it claims to be, supported by identity, context, custody, and evidence.


Provenance
Information about origin, custody, ownership, and transformations over time.


Redundancy
Deliberate duplication of components or copies so one failure does not eliminate access.


3-2-1 rule
A heuristic: at least three copies, on two types of storage, with one copy off-site.


Media failure
Loss or unreadability caused by deterioration or failure of a storage device or medium.


Bit rot
Informal term for unintended data change or unreadability over time.


Obsolescence
Loss of practical support for a format, software, hardware, interface, or dependency.


Migration
Controlled transformation to a different format or environment to maintain usability.


Normalization
Conversion at ingest to one or more selected preservation or access formats.


Emulation
Re-creation of an earlier hardware or software environment so original digital objects can run or render.


Significant property
A characteristic judged necessary to preserve meaning, behavior, appearance, or evidential value.


Preservation metadata
Metadata supporting continuity, identity, provenance, rights, technical management, and preservation actions.


OAIS
The Open Archival Information System reference model and terminology for long-term preservation functions.


SIP
Submission Information Package transferred by a producer for ingest.


AIP
Archival Information Package managed for preservation.


DIP
Dissemination Information Package delivered to a consumer.


Storage tier
A storage class defined by access speed, durability, retrieval conditions, or cost.


Cloud computing
On-demand network access to configurable shared computing resources under defined service characteristics.


IaaS
Infrastructure as a Service: provider-managed computing infrastructure with customer-managed systems and data.


PaaS
Platform as a Service: provider-managed platform for deploying customer applications and data.


SaaS
Software as a Service: provider-hosted application used through a service interface.


SLA
A service-level agreement defining measurable service commitments and responsibilities.


Shared responsibility
Allocation of controls between provider and customer rather than transfer of all responsibility to one party.


Vendor lock-in
Dependence that makes migration or replacement unusually difficult, slow, or expensive.


Data egress
Transfer of data out of a service, often subject to time, bandwidth, and charges.


Exit plan
A tested plan for exporting data, metadata, logs, identities, and functions and securely ending a service.
crumbs: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services

title: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
text:
10.4 Core discussion

10.4.1 Preservation is a managed commitment
Digital preservation begins with a mandate: what must remain available, for whom, for how long, and with which properties and restrictions? A university repository may preserve accepted theses indefinitely. A records center may retain records for legally defined periods and then destroy them through authorized disposition. A museum may preserve a complex interactive artwork whose behavior matters as much as its files.
Preserving everything forever is not a strategy. Selection, appraisal, retention, rights, and resource limits determine scope. The preservation policy should name responsible roles, designated communities, content types, minimum metadata, storage and monitoring expectations, access conditions, review cycles, and authority for actions.
The OAIS reference model describes an archive as an organization of people and systems that accepts responsibility for preserving information and making it available to a designated community (Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems 2012). OAIS is a conceptual model, not a software product or certificate.


10.4.2 Storage, sync, backup, and preservation
Table 10.2 distinguishes related functions.



Table 10.2: Distinguishing digital continuity functions





Function
Primary goal
Does not alone provide
Evidence




Digitization
Create digital representations
Long-term management or preservation rights
Capture specifications and quality review


Storage
Hold data on managed media or services
Identity, monitoring, context, or recovery
Inventory, configuration, monitoring, and access controls


Synchronization
Keep working locations aligned
Protection from propagated deletion
Sync logs, conflict rules, and version behavior


Backup
Restore data after loss or corruption
Meaning, provenance, format action, or permanent retention
Schedules, retention, success logs, and restore tests


Disaster recovery
Restore systems and priority services after disruption
Appraisal, representation information, or long-term access
Recovery objectives, roles, exercises, and incident records


Digital preservation
Maintain authentic, understandable, usable information over time
Unlimited retention or zero-risk continuity
Policy, packages, metadata, fixity, actions, audits, and access tests






As Table 10.2 shows, copies become preservation resources only when they are identified, monitored, protected, recoverable, and connected to policy and metadata.


10.4.3 Threats to digital continuity
Digital objects face interacting threats:

media degradation and hardware failure;
accidental deletion, overwriting, misfiling, or incomplete transfer;
malicious alteration, ransomware, account takeover, or insider abuse;
software, format, dependency, and interface obsolescence;
incomplete metadata or separation of files from context;
undocumented migration or transformation;
loss of keys, credentials, domains, subscriptions, or staff knowledge;
rights uncertainty and restrictions preventing preservation action;
vendor failure, contract termination, price change, or service redesign;
disasters, power instability, heat, humidity, flooding, fire, and political disruption;
institutional neglect, unclear ownership, and insufficient recurring funding.

A threat model connects assets, threats, vulnerabilities, consequences, and controls. “The cloud might fail” is too vague. A useful statement is: “A single administrator account controls deletion of all cloud copies; credential compromise could remove every version before detection.” Controls might include separate roles, multifactor authentication, immutable retention, alerts, delayed deletion, and an independently administered copy.


10.4.4 Files, bitstreams, and representation information
A file extension is a hint, not proof of format. A file named .pdf may be malformed or may contain embedded fonts, images, scripts, encryption, signatures, or attachments. Format identification examines signatures and structure. Validation asks whether the file conforms to a specification. Characterization records technical features needed for management.
A bitstream may be embedded within a file. A container such as a PDF, ZIP, video file, or disk image can contain multiple streams. Preserving bytes does not guarantee that future users can interpret them. Documentation, software dependencies, fonts, codecs, schemas, encryption keys, and contextual metadata may be necessary representation information.


10.4.5 Fixity and checksums
A checksum is calculated from a sequence of bytes. If one byte changes, a strong cryptographic hash such as SHA-256 will almost certainly produce a different value. Preservation systems record a checksum, later recalculate it, and compare the values.
Fixity checking can demonstrate that a bitstream has or has not changed since a recorded event. It cannot prove that the original file was correct, complete, authentic, virus-free, or meaningful. If a damaged file is ingested and hashed, later fixity checks can faithfully confirm the damaged bytes.
The openssl R package exposes SHA-256 hashing functions (Ooms 2026). A manifest should record algorithm, relative path, checksum, size, date, tool, and event outcome. Relative paths allow the package to move without rewriting every entry.
Verification recalculates values from the files rather than trusting the old manifest:
A failed check is an incident, not an instruction to overwrite the expected value. Quarantine the affected copy, compare independent copies, investigate cause, restore from a verified source, and record every action.


10.4.6 Authenticity requires more than fixity
Integrity concerns completeness and authorized change. Authenticity concerns whether an object is what it claims to be. Evidence may include provenance, chain of custody, identifiers, relationships, creator and transfer documentation, access controls, signatures, validation, and preservation events.
An authorized migration intentionally changes bytes. The new checksum differs, yet authenticity can be supported when the source and output, agent, tool, parameters, validation, date, reason, and relationship are documented. Conversely, an unchanged anonymous file with no provenance may have fixity but weak authenticity evidence.


10.4.7 Redundancy and the 3-2-1 heuristic
The 3-2-1 rule recommends at least three copies, on two kinds of storage, with one copy off-site. It is a useful beginner heuristic, not a guarantee. Three folders on one cloud account are not independent copies. Two media types in the same flood zone share a location risk. An offline disk that is never read may be unusable.






flowchart TB
    P["Preservation package and manifest"] --> L["Copy A: managed local storage"]
    P --> C["Copy B: cloud object storage in another region"]
    P --> O["Copy C: offline or separately administered storage"]
    L --> M["Scheduled fixity and access monitoring"]
    C --> M
    O --> M
    M --> R["Documented restore test and incident response"]
    R --> P




Figure 10.1: A basic preservation storage arrangement with independent controls





As Figure 10.1 shows, each copy participates in monitoring and recovery. Independence includes technology, location, credentials, administration, and failure mode. The NDSA Levels of Digital Preservation provide a staged framework across storage, integrity, control, metadata, and content (National Digital Stewardship Alliance 2019).


10.4.8 Inventory and identity
An organization cannot preserve what it does not know it holds. A preservation inventory records stable identifier, path or object key, size, format, dates, rights, source, responsible unit, retention, access restrictions, checksum, storage copies, and last check.
File names should be stable, unique within scope, and machine-safe, but filenames should not carry the only metadata. Renaming files can break links and authenticity evidence. If renaming is necessary, preserve original names and document the mapping.
Packaging formats can bind content and manifests for transfer. BagIt defines a hierarchical package with payload files and manifests (Kunze et al. 2018). A bag is not automatically a complete preservation repository; it supports reliable transfer and validation within a larger workflow.


10.4.9 File-format sustainability
No format is permanently safe. Format assessment considers:

public documentation and openness;
adoption and community support;
transparency versus compression or encryption;
self-documentation and embedded metadata;
external dependencies;
patent or licensing restrictions;
validation and rendering tools;
ability to preserve significant properties;
institutional capacity.

The Library of Congress sustainability guidance evaluates formats through factors such as disclosure, adoption, transparency, self-documentation, external dependencies, patents, and technical protection (Library of Congress 2026). Preferred-format lists should be reviewed, not treated as timeless rankings.
Table 10.3 compares common responses.



Table 10.3: Selected digital preservation format strategies





Strategy
Use
Strength
Risk




Preserve as received
Retain original evidence and future options
Avoids immediate transformation loss
Future rendering may become difficult


Normalization
Create a selected format at ingest while retaining source
Simplifies some management and access workflows
Significant properties can be lost at ingest


Migration
Transform content when support or risk changes
Maintains use with current tools
Repeated transformations and undocumented changes


Emulation
Recreate an environment for software-dependent behavior
Can preserve original interaction and behavior
Complexity, licensing, security, and specialist dependence


Encapsulation and documentation
Keep content with software, specifications, metadata, or dependencies
Preserves context needed for later interpretation
Package may remain unusable without future action






As Table 10.3 shows, strategies can be combined. Preserve the original, create a normalized preservation version, and generate an accessible derivative, while documenting relationships and validation.


10.4.10 Significant properties and acceptable change
Migration cannot preserve every technical feature perfectly. Significant properties are characteristics judged necessary to retain meaning, evidential value, appearance, structure, or behavior for the designated community.
For a thesis, text, page order, figures, equations, citations, and accessibility may matter. For a spreadsheet, formulas, values, cell relationships, comments, formatting, and macros may have different significance. For an interactive artwork, timing, input, audiovisual behavior, and network dependencies may be central.
Significance is not purely technical. It follows mandate, user needs, records requirements, creator intent, cultural authority, and feasible resources. Document the judgment and validate migrations against it.


10.4.11 Preservation metadata and PREMIS
Preservation metadata records what an object is, what happened to it, who or what acted, which rights authorize action, and how components relate. PREMIS organizes preservation information around objects, events, agents, and rights (PREMIS Editorial Committee 2015).
Table 10.4 uses a simple migration example.



Table 10.4: PREMIS-oriented entities in a preservation workflow





Entity
Example
Question answered




Object
Source TIFF and normalized TIFF, each with identifiers and fixity
What was managed?


Event
Format validation, migration, fixity check, or restore with date and outcome
What happened and with what result?


Agent
Staff member, institution, script, or software tool responsible for an event
Who or what acted?


Rights
Permission or basis allowing preservation copying, migration, and controlled access
What action was permitted and under what conditions?


Relationship
Normalized file derived from source; access JPEG derived from preservation object
How do versions and components connect?






As Table 10.4 indicates, preservation metadata supports accountability and future interpretation. A spreadsheet can introduce these ideas at small scale, but larger programs need governed systems and controlled values.


10.4.12 OAIS packages and functions
OAIS uses three package concepts:

SIP: what a producer submits;
AIP: what the archive manages for preservation;
DIP: what the archive provides to a consumer.

The packages need not be one ZIP file. They are conceptual combinations of content information and preservation description information. Ingest validates and transforms submissions into managed archival packages. Access creates dissemination packages appropriate to user rights and needs.






flowchart LR
    PR["Producer"] --> SIP["Submission Information Package"]
    SIP --> IN["Ingest: verify, identify, validate, document"]
    IN --> AIP["Archival Information Package"]
    AIP --> ST["Archival storage and data management"]
    ST --> PM["Preservation planning and administration"]
    ST --> AC["Access"]
    AC --> DIP["Dissemination Information Package"]
    DIP --> CO["Consumer / designated community"]




Figure 10.2: A simplified OAIS-oriented preservation flow





Figure 10.2 highlights that preservation planning and administration continue after ingest. Deposit is the beginning of managed custody, not the end.


10.4.13 Cloud computing and service models
NIST defines cloud computing through on-demand access to shared configurable resources and identifies service and deployment models (Mell and Grance 2011). In simplified terms:

IaaS: the provider manages facilities, network, servers, and virtualization; the customer manages operating systems, applications, identities, configuration, and data.
PaaS: the provider also manages more of the application platform; the customer manages deployed applications, data, and access.
SaaS: the provider operates the application; the customer configures use, identities, data, workflows, and governance.

“Managed by provider” does not mean “no customer responsibility.” The institution must configure access, monitor use, classify data, maintain metadata, check exports, and understand contractual controls. Security and privacy responsibilities vary by service and agreement (Jansen and Grance 2011).
Cloud deployment can be public, private, community, or hybrid. The labels do not by themselves determine security or suitability. Architecture, configuration, operations, contracts, and evidence matter.


10.4.14 Storage tiers, durability, availability, and recovery
Cloud object storage often offers hot, cool, and archival tiers. Lower storage prices may bring retrieval delays, minimum retention periods, request charges, or egress fees. A preservation copy that takes hours to restore may be acceptable if another access copy exists, but incident plans must reflect that delay.
Durability estimates the likelihood that stored objects remain intact. Availability concerns whether they can be accessed when needed. Neither is a substitute for independent verification and recovery testing. Provider replication within one account may protect hardware failure but not customer deletion, compromised credentials, billing suspension, legal seizure, or provider-wide defects.


10.4.15 Evaluating cloud preservation services
Table 10.5 identifies questions beyond monthly storage price.



Table 10.5: Questions for evaluating cloud preservation services





Dimension
Questions




Data and metadata
Can all files, metadata, relationships, logs, and versions be exported completely?


Integrity
Who calculates and exposes checksums? Can customer fixity be verified independently?


Identity and access
Are least privilege, MFA, separate roles, service accounts, and account recovery supported?


Security and privacy
How are data encrypted, keys managed, incidents notified, and subcontractors controlled?


Location and jurisdiction
Where can replicas, backups, support access, and processing occur? Which laws apply?


Availability and recovery
What are measured commitments, restoration times, dependencies, and tested alternatives?


Cost
What are storage, request, retrieval, egress, support, growth, and migration costs?


Interoperability and exit
Are standard formats and APIs available? How long and how costly is complete exit?


Governance and evidence
What audit reports, logs, change notices, deletion evidence, and contract remedies exist?






As Table 10.5 shows, procurement is part of preservation planning. A low entry price with expensive egress can make exit financially difficult.


10.4.16 Service-level agreements and shared responsibility
An SLA should define measurable service scope, availability, support response, durability where applicable, incident notice, backup and restore, maintenance, data location, deletion, export, and remedies. A service credit after failure may not compensate for irreplaceable cultural loss.
A shared-responsibility matrix names each control and assigns provider, institution, or joint responsibility. Avoid vague statements such as “security is shared.” For example:

provider: physical data-center controls;
institution: classification and least-privilege roles;
joint: incident communication and evidence exchange;
institution: independent preservation metadata and off-provider copy.



10.4.17 Cost and total cost of stewardship
Preservation costs include selection, appraisal, rights, transfer, metadata, quality control, storage, monitoring, validation, security, access, staff training, format action, audits, incident response, migration, and exit. Cloud invoices add operations, retrieval, egress, API requests, monitoring, and support.
A cost model should state assumptions: annual growth, number and size of copies, access frequency, retention, geographic regions, retrieval tests, staffing, currency exposure, and price change. Comparing one local disk purchase with one month of cloud storage ignores staffing, replacement, power, facilities, risk, and exit.


10.4.18 Exit planning and vendor lock-in
An exit plan should exist before signing a contract. It identifies triggers, authority, export formats, APIs, metadata, checksums, logs, identities, encryption keys, bandwidth, egress cost, destination capacity, validation, parallel operation, final deletion, and proof.
A tabletop exit test asks: Can the institution list everything? Export a sample? Reconstruct relationships? Verify checksums? Estimate time and cost for all content? Continue access during transition? Confirm vendor deletion afterward?
If the answer depends on an employee’s memory or a proprietary interface, preservation risk is high.
crumbs: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services

title: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
text:
10.5 Philippine and Southeast Asian examples

10.5.1 Provincial archive: typhoon-aware redundancy
A provincial archive stores digitized land and municipal records in a flood-prone area. One managed copy remains on-site for work, a second is held at a partner institution in another island group, and a third is stored in cloud object storage under a separately administered account. Quarterly fixity checks and annual restoration exercises are documented. The arrangement considers geographic hazards, connectivity, and the need for local access after disruption.


10.5.2 University repository: theses and embargoes
A university repository preserves submitted PDFs, supplemental files, deposit agreements, embargo dates, metadata, and preservation events. Public access copies are separate from restricted archival copies. Cloud storage rules do not accidentally publish embargoed files, and exit tests verify that rights and restriction metadata export with content.


10.5.3 Public library: community oral histories
A city library preserves uncompressed or losslessly compressed audio masters, interview agreements, transcripts, access derivatives, and community restrictions. Migration tests compare duration, channels, sampling characteristics, embedded metadata, and intelligibility. Consent is reviewed before cloud replication across jurisdictions.


10.5.4 Government records center: retention and authorized deletion
A government records center distinguishes preservation from permanent retention. Cloud immutability protects records during their retention period, but authorized disposition remains possible after legal, audit, and archival requirements are satisfied. Deletion includes replicas and provider backups according to contract, with evidence recorded.


10.5.5 Regional museum consortium: shared capacity without shared passwords
Several Southeast Asian museums pool preservation storage. Each institution retains identifiers, metadata, and authority over its collections. Federated identity and role-based access replace shared administrator passwords. Agreements define incident response, cost allocation, withdrawal, community restrictions, and recovery when one partner loses connectivity.

Reflect: Which disaster is most visible in your institution’s planning, and which slower organizational or technical threat is easier to overlook?
crumbs: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services

title: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
section: 10.6 Hands-on activity: Build and test a small preservation package
text:
10.6 Hands-on activity: Build and test a small preservation package

10.6.1 Purpose
Create a preservation package for three to five non-sensitive files, record inventory and SHA-256 fixity, maintain three controlled copies, simulate unintended change, restore from a verified copy, and document the event. This activity contributes to the ethics and preservation brief.


10.6.2 Tools

R and Quarto;
R package openssl (Ooms 2026);
three to five instructor-provided or self-created non-sensitive files in at least two formats;
three storage locations approved for class use, with at least one logically or geographically separate;
the package fields in Table 10.6.




Table 10.6: Minimum manifest fields for the preservation-package lab





Field
Purpose




object_id
Stable identity within the exercise


relative_path
Location independent of the storage root


original_name
Evidence of the supplied filename


bytes
Expected file size


format
Identified format rather than extension alone


format_version
Version when known


sha256
Recorded fixity value and algorithm


source
Creator, transfer, or source context


rights_access
Preservation and access authority or restriction


copy_locations
Controlled identifiers for three copies


last_fixity
Date and outcome of last verification


status
Active, quarantined, missing, or superseded






Table 10.6 combines technical and stewardship evidence. Do not place passwords, personal data, or sensitive locations in the exercise package.


10.6.3 Steps

State the collection purpose, designated users, retention expectation, significant properties, rights, and excluded sensitive content.
Create package/data/, package/metadata/, and package/logs/. Copy the selected source files into data/; do not alter the originals.
Inventory each file with stable object ID, relative path, original filename, size, format, source, rights, and significant properties.
Calculate SHA-256 checksums in R. Export metadata/manifest-sha256.csv and record tool and date.
Verify every file immediately against the manifest. Record a FIXITY_CHECK event with PASS or FAIL.
Create three named copies using two storage types, with one separate from the primary location. Record location, administrator, encryption, access, and sync behavior.
Verify all three copies independently. Do not assume the copy command succeeded.
On a disposable test copy only, change one byte or edit one file. Recalculate checksums and observe the failure.
Quarantine the altered copy. Restore the affected file from an independently verified copy. Recalculate fixity and record investigation, restoration, agent, time, and outcome.
Select one format and complete a risk note covering documentation, adoption, dependencies, validation tools, and significant properties.
Draw a storage and responsibility diagram showing copies, failure domains, administrators, monitoring, and recovery path.
Compare one local and one cloud option using the criteria in Table 10.5. Estimate storage and exit assumptions rather than quoting only a headline price.
Write a one-page preservation action plan with schedule, owners, incident steps, annual restore test, format review, and exit trigger.
Render the Quarto report and verify that another student can reconstruct what happened without oral explanation.



10.6.4 Expected output
Submit:

preservation package with data/, metadata/, and logs/;
inventory and SHA-256 manifest;
fixity log showing initial checks, deliberate failure, quarantine, restore, and final pass;
storage and responsibility diagram;
format-risk and significant-properties note;
local-versus-cloud comparison;
one-page preservation action plan; and
a 200-word reflection: Which evidence demonstrates continuity, and which preservation claims remain assumptions?



10.6.5 Lab checklist

The package contains no sensitive or copyrighted content unsuitable for class copying.
Original files remain unchanged.
Relative paths and stable object IDs are used.
SHA-256 algorithm, tool, and dates are recorded.
Every copy is verified independently.
The deliberate failure occurs only in a disposable test copy.
A failed checksum is investigated, not overwritten.
Three copies have meaningfully different failure domains.
Rights, significant properties, owners, and schedules are documented.
Recovery and exit are tested or estimated with evidence.



10.6.6 Assessment rubric
Table 10.7 emphasizes evidence and recovery rather than the mere presence of copies.



Table 10.7: Rubric for the preservation package and recovery test





Criterion
Weight
Strong performance




Scope and package
20%
Objects, identifiers, metadata, rights, significant properties, and exclusions are coherent


Fixity and event evidence
25%
Checksums are reproducible; failure, quarantine, restore, and outcomes are accurately logged


Redundancy and recovery
20%
Copies have independent failure domains and a demonstrated recovery path


Format and cloud analysis
20%
Format risk and local/cloud comparison use explicit evidence, cost, jurisdiction, and exit criteria


Governance, ethics, and sustainability
15%
Owners, privacy, access, retention, monitoring, review, and limitations are professionally addressed
crumbs: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services

title: 10  Digital Preservation and Cloud Services
